 volume I 
 CHAPTER I 
 Phineas Finn propose to stand for Loughshane 
 Dr. Finn , of Killaloe , in county Clare , be as well know in those 
 part , - the confine , that be , of the county Clare , Limerick , 
 Tipperary , and Galway , - as be the bishop himself who live in the 
 same town , and be as much respect . many say that the doctor be 
 the rich man of the two , and the practice of his profession be 
 extend over almost as wide a district . indeed the bishop whom he 
 be privileged to attend , although a roman Catholic , always speak of 
 their diocese be conterminate . it will therefore be understand 
 that Dr. Finn , - Malachi Finn be his full name , - have obtain a wide 
 reputation as a country practitioner in the west of Ireland . and he 
 be a man sufficiently well to do , though that boast make by his 
 friend , that he be as warm a man as the bishop , have but little 
 truth to support it . bishop in Ireland , if they live at home , even 
 in these day , be very warm man ; and Dr. Finn have not a penny in the 
 world for which he have not work hard . he have , moreover , a costly 
 family , five daughter and one son , and , at the time of which we 
 be speak , no provision in the way of marriage or profession have 
 be make for any of they . of the one son , Phineas , the hero of the 
 follow page , the mother and five sister be very proud . the 
 doctor be accustom to say that his goose be as good as any other 
 man 's goose , as far as he could see as yet ; but that he should like 
 some very strong evidence before he allow himself to express an 
 opinion that the young bird partook , in any degree , of the quality 
 of a swan . from which it may be gather that Dr. Finn be a man of 
 common - sense . 
 Phineas have come to be a swan in the estimation of his mother and 
 sister by reason of certain early success at college . his father , 
 whose religion be not of that bitter kind in which we in England 
 be apt to suppose that all the Irish Roman Catholics indulge , have 
 send his son to Trinity ; and there be some in the neighbourhood of 
 Killaloe , - patient , probably , of Dr. Duggin , of Castle Connell , a 
 learn physician who have spend a fruitless life in endeavour to 
 make head against Dr. Finn , - who declare that old Finn would not be 
 sorry if his son be to turn protestant and go in for a fellowship . 
 Mrs. Finn be a protestant , and the five Miss Finns be Protestants , 
 and the doctor himself be very much give to din out among his 
 protestant friend on a Friday . our Phineas , however , do not turn 
 protestant up in Dublin , whatever his father 's secret wish on that 
 subject may have be . he do join a debating society , to success 
 in which his religion be no bar ; and he there achieve a sort of 
 distinction which be both easy and pleasant , and which , make 
 its way down to Killaloe , assist in engender those idea as 
 to swanhood of which maternal and sisterly mind be so sweetly 
 susceptible . " I know half a dozen old windbag at the present 
 moment , " say the doctor , " who be great fellow at debate club 
 when they be boy . " " Phineas be not a boy any long , " say Mrs. 
 Finn . " and windbag do not   get college scholarship , " say Matilda 
 Finn , the second daughter . " but papa always snub Phinny , " say 
 Barbara , the young . " I will snub you , if you do not   take care , " say 
 the doctor , take Barbara tenderly by the ear ; - for his young 
 daughter be the doctor 's pet . 
 the doctor certainly do not snub his son , for he allow he to go 
 over to London when he be twenty - two year of age , in order that he 
 might read with an english barrister . it be the doctor 's wish that 
 his son might be call to the Irish Bar , and the young man 's desire 
 that he might go to the English Bar . the doctor so far give way , 
 under the influence of Phineas himself , and of all the young woman of 
 the family , as to pay the usual fee to a very competent and learn 
 gentleman in the Middle Temple , and to allow his son one hundred and 
 fifty pound per annum for three year . Dr. Finn , however , be still 
 firm in his intention that his son should settle in Dublin , and take 
 the Munster Circuit , - believe that Phineas might come to want home 
 influence and home connection , in spite of the swanhood which be 
 attribute to he . 
 Phineas sit his term for three year , and be duly call to 
 the Bar ; but no evidence come home as to the acquirement of any 
 considerable amount of law lore , or even as to much law study , on 
 the part of the young aspirant . the learn pundit at whose foot he 
 have be sit be not especially loud in praise of his pupil 's 
 industry , though he do say a pleasant word or two as to his pupil 's 
 intelligence . Phineas himself do not boast much of his own hard 
 work when at home during the long vacation . no rumour of expect 
 success , - of expect professional success , - reach the ear of 
 any of the Finn family at Killaloe . but , nevertheless , there come 
 tiding which maintain those high idea in the maternal bosom of 
 which mention have be make , and which be of sufficient strength to 
 induce the doctor , in opposition to his own judgment , to consent to 
 the continued residence of his son in London . Phineas belong to an 
 excellent club , - the Reform Club , - and go into very good society . 
 he be hand in glove with the Hon . Laurence Fitzgibbon , the young 
 son of Lord Claddagh . he be intimate with Barrington Erle , who have 
 be private secretary , - one of the private secretary , - to the 
 great Whig Prime Minister who be lately in but be now out . he have 
 din three or four time with that great Whig nobleman , the Earl of 
 Brentford . and he have be assure that if he stick to the English 
 Bar he would certainly do well . though he might fail to succeed in 
 court or in chamber , he would doubtless have give to he some 
 one of those numerous appointment for which none but clever young 
 barrister be suppose to be fitting candidate . the old doctor 
 yield for another year , although at the end of the second year he 
 be call upon to pay a sum of three hundred pound , which be then 
 due by Phineas to creditor in London . when the doctor 's male friend 
 in and about Killaloe hear that he have do so , they say that he 
 be dot . not one of the Miss Finns be as yet marry ; and , after 
 all that have be say about the doctor 's wealth , it be suppose 
 that there would not be above five hundred pound a year among they 
 all , be he to give up his profession . but the doctor , when he pay 
 that three hundred pound for his son , buckle to his work again , 
 though he have for twelve month talk of give up the midwifery . 
 he buckle to again , to the great disgust of Dr. Duggin , who at this 
 time say very ill - natured thing about young Phineas . 
 at the end of the three year Phineas be call to the Bar , and 
 immediately receive a letter from his father ask minutely as to 
 his professional intention . his father recommend he to settle 
 in Dublin , and promise the one hundred and fifty pound for three 
 more year , on condition that this advice be follow . he do not 
 absolutely say that the allowance would be stop if the advice be 
 not follow , but that be plainly to be imply . that letter come 
 at the moment of a dissolution of Parliament . Lord de Terrier , the 
 conservative Prime Minister , who have now be in office for the 
 almost unprecedentedly long period of fifteen month , have find that 
 he could not face continue majority against he in the House of 
 Commons , and have dissolve the House . Rumour declare that he would 
 have much prefer to resign , and betake himself once again to the 
 easy glory of opposition ; but his party have naturally be obdurate 
 with he , and he have resolve to appeal to the country . when Phineas 
 receive his father 's letter , it have just be suggest to he at 
 the Reform Club that he should stand for the irish borough of 
 Loughshane . 
 this proposition have take Phineas Finn so much by surprise that when 
 first make to he by Barrington Erle it take his breath away . what ! 
 he stand for Parliament , twenty - four year old , with no vestige 
 of property belong to he , without a penny in his purse , as 
 completely dependent on his father as he be when he first go to 
 school at eleven year of age ! and for Loughshane , a little borough 
 in the county Galway , for which a brother of that fine old irish 
 peer , the Earl of Tulla , have be sit for the last twenty 
 year , - a fine , high - minded representative of the thorough - go 
 Orange protestant feeling of Ireland ! and the Earl of Tulla , to 
 whom almost all Loughshane belong , - or at any rate the land about 
 Loughshane , - be one of his father 's staunch friend ! loughshane 
 be in county Galway , but the Earl of Tulla usually live at his seat 
 in county Clare , not more than ten mile from Killaloe , and always 
 confide his gouty foot , and the weak nerve of the old countess , and 
 the stomach of all his domestic , to the care of Dr. Finn . how be 
 it possible that Phineas should stand for Loughshane ? from whence 
 be the money to come for such a contest ? it be a beautiful dream , 
 a grand idea , lift Phineas almost off the earth by its glory . 
 when the proposition be first make to he in the smoking - room at 
 the Reform Club by his friend Erle , he be aware that he blush 
 like a girl , and that he be unable at the moment to express 
 himself plainly , - so great be his astonishment and so great his 
 gratification . but before ten minute have pass by , while Barrington 
 Erle be still sit over his shoulder on the club sofa , and before 
 the blush have altogether vanish , he have see the improbability of 
 the scheme , and have explain to his friend that the thing could not 
 be do . but to his increase astonishment , his friend make nothing 
 of the difficulty . loughshane , accord to Barrington Erle , be 
 so small a place , that the expense would be very little . there be 
 altogether no more than 307 register elector . the inhabitant be 
 so far remove from the world , and be so ignorant of the world 's 
 good thing , that they know nothing about bribery . the Hon . George 
 Morris , who have sit for the last twenty year , be very unpopular . he 
 have not be near the borough since the last election , he have hardly 
 do more than show himself in Parliament , and have neither give a 
 shilling in the town nor get a place under Government for a single 
 son of Loughshane . " and he have quarrel with his brother , " say 
 Barrington Erle . " the devil he have ! " say Phineas . " I think they 
 always swear by each other . " " it be at each other they swear now , " 
 say Barrington ; " George have ask the Earl for more money , and the 
 Earl have cut up rusty . " then the negotiator go on to explain that 
 the expense of the election would be defray out of a certain fund 
 collect for such purpose , that Loughshane have be choose as a 
 cheap place , and that Phineas Finn have be choose as a safe and 
 promise young man . as for qualification , if any question be 
 raise , that should be make all right . an irish candidate be want , 
 and a roman Catholic . so much the Loughshaners would require on 
 their own account when instigate to dismiss from their service 
 that thorough - go protestant , the Hon . George Morris . then " the 
 party , " - by which Barrington Erle probably mean the great man in 
 whose service he himself have become a politician , - require that 
 the candidate should be a safe man , one who would support " the 
 party , " - not a cantankerous , red - hot semi - fenian , run about to 
 meeting at the Rotunda , and such - like , with view of his own about 
 tenant - right and the Irish Church . " but I have view of my own , " say 
 Phineas , blush again . " of course you have , my dear boy , " say 
 Barrington , clap he on the back . " I should not   come to you unless 
 you have view . but your view and our be the same , and you be 
 just the lad for Galway . you might not   have such an opening again 
 in your life , and of course you will stand for Loughshane . " then the 
 conversation be over , the private secretary go away to arrange 
 some other little matter of the kind , and Phineas Finn be leave alone 
 to consider the proposition that have be make to he . 
 to become a member of the British Parliament ! in all those hot 
 contest at the two debating club to which he have belong , this 
 have be the ambition which have move he . for , after all , to what 
 purpose of their own have those empty debate ever tend ? he and 
 three or four other who have call themselves Liberals have be 
 pit against four or five who have call themselves Conservatives , 
 and night after night they have discuss some ponderous subject 
 without any idea that one would ever persuade another , or that their 
 talking would ever conduce to any action or to any result . but each 
 of these combatant have feel , - without dare to announce a hope on 
 the subject among themselves , - that the present arena be only a 
 trial - ground for some possible great amphitheatre , for some future 
 debate club in which debate would lead to action , and in which 
 eloquence would have power , even though persuasion might be out of 
 the question . 
 Phineas certainly have never dare to speak , even to himself , of such 
 a hope . the labour of the Bar have to be encounter before the dawn 
 of such a hope could come to he . and he have gradually learn to 
 feel that his prospect at the Bar be not as yet very promising . as 
 regard professional work he have be idle , and how then could he 
 have a hope ? 
 and now this thing , which he regard as be of all thing in the 
 world the most honourable , have come to he all at once , and be 
 possibly within his reach ! if he could believe Barrington Erle , he 
 have only to lift up his hand , and he might be in Parliament within 
 two month . and who be to be believe on such a subject if not 
 Barrington Erle ? this be Erle 's special business , and such a man 
 would not have come to he on such a subject have he not be in 
 earnest , and have he not himself believe in success . there be an 
 opening ready , an opening to this great glory , - if only it might be 
 possible for he to fill it ! 
 what would his father say ? his father would of course oppose the 
 plan . and if he oppose his father , his father would of course stop 
 his income . and such an income as it be ! could it be that a man 
 should sit in Parliament and live upon a hundred and fifty pound 
 a year ? since that payment of his debt he have become again 
 embarrassed , - to a slight amount . he owe a tailor a trifle , and a 
 bootmak a trifle , - and something to the man who sell glove and 
 shirt ; and yet he have do his good to keep out of debt with more 
 than irish pertinacity , live very closely , breakfast upon tea 
 and a roll , and din frequently for a shilling at a luncheon - house 
 up a court near Lincoln 's Inn . where should he dine if the 
 Loughshaners elect he to Parliament ? and then he paint to 
 himself a not untrue picture of the probable misery of a man who 
 begin life too high up on the ladder , - who succeed in mount 
 before he have learn how to hold on when he be aloft . for our 
 Phineas Finn be a young man not without sense , - not entirely a 
 windbag . if he do this thing the probability be that he might 
 become utterly a castaway , and go entirely to the dog before he be 
 thirty . he have hear of penniless man who have get into Parliament , 
 and to whom have come such a fate . he be able to name to himself a 
 man or two whose bark , carry more sail than they could bear , have 
 go to piece among early breaker in this way . but then , would 
 it not be well to go to piece early than never to carry any 
 sail at all ? and there be , at any rate , the chance of success . he 
 be already a barrister , and there be so many thing open to a 
 barrister with a seat in Parliament ! and as he know of man who have 
 be utterly ruin by such early mounting , so also do he know of 
 other whose fortune have be make by happy audacity when they be 
 young . he almost think that he could die happy if he have once take 
 his seat in Parliament , - if he have receive one letter with those 
 grand initial write after his name on the address . young man in 
 battle be call upon to lead forlorn hope . three fall , perhaps , 
 to one who get through ; but the one who get through will have 
 the Victoria Cross to carry for the rest of his life . this be his 
 forlorn hope ; and as he have be invite to undertake the work , he 
 would not turn from the danger . on the follow morning he again see 
 Barrington Erle by appointment , and then write the following letter 
 to his father : - 
 Reform Club , Feb. , 186 - . 
 MY DEAR FATHER , 
 I be afraid that the purport of this letter will startle 
 you , but I hope that when you have finish it you will 
 think that I be right in my decision as to what I be go 
 to do . you be no doubt aware that the dissolution of 
 Parliament will take place at once , and that we shall be 
 in all the turmoil of a general election by the middle of 
 March . I have be invite to stand for Loughshane , and 
 have consent . the proposition have be make to I by my 
 friend Barrington Erle , Mr. Mildmay 's private secretary , 
 and have be make on behalf of the Political Committee of 
 the Reform Club . I need hardly say that I should not have 
 thought of such a thing with a less thorough promise of 
 support than this give I , nor should I think of it now 
 have I not be assure that none of the expense of the 
 election would fall upon I . of course I could not have 
 ask you to pay for it . 
 but to such a proposition , so make , I have feel that it 
 would be cowardly to give a refusal . I can not but regard 
 such a selection as a great honour . I own that I be fond 
 of politic , and have take great delight in their study 
  -   " stupid young fool ! " his father say to himself as he 
 read this   - and it have be my dream for year past to 
 have a seat in Parliament at some future time .   " Dream ! 
 yes ; I wonder whether he have ever dream what he be to 
 live upon . "   the chance have now come to I much early 
 than I have look for it , but I do not think that it 
 should on that account be throw away . look to my 
 profession , I find that many thing be open to a 
 barrister with a seat in Parliament , and that the House 
 need not interfere much with a man 's practice .   " not if 
 he have get to the top of his tree , " say the doctor . 
 my chief doubt arise from the fact of your old friendship 
 with Lord Tulla , whose brother have fill the seat for I 
 do not   know how many year . but it seem that George Morris 
 must go ; or , at least , that he must be oppose by a 
 liberal candidate . if I do not stand , some one else will , 
 and I should think that Lord Tulla will be too much of a 
 man to make any personal quarrel on such a subject . if he 
 be to lose the borough , why should not I have it as well 
 as another ? 
 I can fancy , my dear father , all that you will say as to 
 my imprudence , and I quite confess that I have not a word 
 to answer . I have tell myself more than once , since last 
 night , that I shall probably ruin myself .   " I wonder 
 whether he have ever tell himself that he will probably 
 ruin I also , " say the doctor .   but I be prepared to ruin 
 myself in such a cause . I have no one dependent on I ; 
 and , as long as I do nothing to disgrace my name , I may 
 dispose of myself as I please . if you decide on stop 
 my allowance , I shall have no feeling of anger against 
 you .   " how very considerate ! " say the doctor .   and in 
 that case I shall endeavour to support myself by my pen . 
 I have already do a little for the magazine . 
 give my good love to my mother and sister . if you will 
 receive I during the time of the election , I shall see 
 they soon . perhaps it will be good for I to say that I 
 have positively decide on make the attempt ; that be to 
 say , if the Club Committee be as good as its promise . I 
 have weigh the matter all round , and I regard the prize 
 as be so great , that I be prepared to run any risk to 
 obtain it . indeed , to I , with my view about politic , 
 the running of such a risk be no more than a duty . I 
 can not keep my hand from the work now that the work have 
 come in the way of my hand . I shall be most anxious to get 
 a line from you in answer to this . 
 your most affectionate son , 
 phinea FINN . 
 I question whether Dr. Finn , when he read this letter , do not feel 
 more of pride than of anger , - whether he be not rather gratify 
 than displeased , in spite of all that his common - sense tell he on 
 the subject . his wife and daughter , when they hear the news , be 
 clearly on the side of the young man . Mrs. Finn immediately express 
 an opinion that Parliament would be the making of her son , and that 
 everybody would be sure to employ so distinguish a barrister . the 
 girl declare that Phineas ought , at any rate , to have his chance , 
 and almost assert that it would be brutal in their father to stand 
 in their brother 's way . it be in vain that the doctor try to 
 explain that go into Parliament could not help a young barrister , 
 whatever it might do for one thoroughly establish in his 
 profession ; that Phineas , if successful at Loughshane , would at once 
 abandon all idea of earn any income , - that the proposition , come 
 from so poor a man , be a monstrosity , - that such an opposition 
 to the Morris family , come from a son of his , would be gross 
 ingratitude to Lord Tulla . Mrs. Finn and the girl talk he down , 
 and the doctor himself be almost carry away by something like 
 vanity in regard to his son 's future position . 
 nevertheless he write a letter strongly advise Phineas to abandon 
 the project . but he himself be aware that the letter which he write 
 be not one from which any success could be expect . he advise 
 his son , but do not command he . he make no threat as to stop 
 his income . he do not tell Phineas , in so many word , that he be 
 propose to make an ass of himself . he argue very prudently against 
 the plan , and Phineas , when he receive his father 's letter , of 
 course feel that it be tantamount to a paternal permission to 
 proceed with the matter . on the next day he get a letter from his 
 mother full of affection , full of pride , - not exactly tell he to 
 stand for Loughshane by all mean , for Mrs. Finn be not the woman to 
 run openly counter to her husband in any advice give by she to their 
 son , - but give he every encouragement which motherly affection and 
 motherly pride could bestow . " of course you will come to we , " she 
 say , " if you do make up your mind to be member for Loughshane . we 
 shall all of we be so delighted to have you ! " Phineas , who have fall 
 into a sea of doubt after write to his father , and who have demand 
 a week from Barrington Erle to consider the matter , be elated to 
 positive certainty by the joint effect of the two letter from home . 
 he understand it all . his mother and sister be altogether in 
 favour of his audacity , and even his father be not dispose to 
 quarrel with he on the subject . 
 " I shall take you at your word , " he say to Barrington Erle at the 
 club that evening . 
 " what word ? " say Erle , who have too many iron in the fire to be 
 think always of Loughshane and Phineas Finn , - or who at any rate 
 do not choose to let his anxiety on the subject be see . 
 " About Loughshane . " 
 " all right , old fellow ; we shall be sure to carry you through . the 
 irish writ will be out on the third of March , and the soon you be 
 there the well . " 
 CHAPTER II 
 Phineas Finn be elect for Loughshane 
 one great difficulty about the borough vanish in a very wonderful 
 way at the first touch . Dr. Finn , who be a man stout at heart , 
 and by no mean afraid of his great friend , drive himself over to 
 Castlemorris to tell his news to the Earl , as soon as he get a second 
 letter from his son declare his intention of proceed with the 
 business , let the result be what they might . Lord Tulla be a 
 passionate old man , and the doctor expect that there would be a 
 quarrel ; - but he be prepared to face that . he be under no special 
 debt of gratitude to the lord , have give as much as he have take 
 in the long intercourse which have exist between they ; - and he 
 agree with his son in think that if there be to be a liberal 
 candidate at Loughshane , no consideration of old pill - box and 
 gallipot should deter his son Phineas from stand . other 
 consideration might very probably deter he , but not that . the Earl 
 probably would be of a different opinion , and the doctor feel it to 
 be incumbent on he to break the news to Lord Tulla . 
 " the devil he be ! " say the Earl , when the doctor have tell his story . 
 " then I will tell you what , Finn , I will support he . " 
 " you support he , Lord Tulla ! " 
 " yes ; - why should not   I support he ? I suppose it be not so bad with I 
 in the country that my support will rob he of his chance ! I will tell 
 you one thing for certain , I will not   support George Morris . " 
 " but , my lord - " 
 " well ; go on . " 
 " I have   never take much part in politic myself , as you know ; but my 
 boy Phineas be on the other side . " 
 " I do not   care a - - for side . what have my party do for I ? 
 look at my cousin , Dick Morris . there be not a clergyman in Ireland 
 stauncher to they than he have be , and now they have   give the deanery 
 of Kilfenora to a man that never have a father , though I condescend 
 to ask for it for my cousin . let they wait till I ask for anything 
 again . " Dr. Finn , who know all about Dick Morris 's debt , and who have 
 hear of his mode of preach , be not surprised at the decision 
 of the conservative bestower of Irish Church patronage ; but on this 
 subject he say nothing . " and as for George , " continue the Earl , " I 
 will never lift my hand again for he . his standing for Loughshane 
 would be quite out of the question . my own tenant would not   vote for 
 he if I be to ask they myself . Peter Blake " - Mr. Peter Blake be 
 the lord 's agent - " tell I only a week ago that it would be useless . 
 the whole thing be go , and for my part I wish they would disenfranchise 
 the borough . I wish they would disenfranchise the whole country , and send 
 we a military governor . what be the use of such member as we send ? 
 there be not   one gentleman among ten of they . your son be welcome for 
 I . what support I can give he he shall have , but it be not   much . I 
 suppose he have well come and see I . " 
 the doctor promise that his son should ride over to Castlemorris , 
 and then take his leave , - not specially flatter , as he feel that 
 be his son to be return , the Earl would not regard he as the 
 one gentleman among ten whom the county might send to leaven the 
 remainder of its member , - but aware that the great impediment 
 in his son 's way be already remove . he certainly have not go to 
 Castlemorris with any idea of canvass for his son , and yet he have 
 canvass for he most satisfactorily . when he get home he do not 
 know how to speak of the matter otherwise than triumphantly to his 
 wife and daughter . though he desire to curse , his mouth would speak 
 blessing . before that evening be over the prospect of Phineas at 
 Loughshane be speak of with open enthusiasm before the doctor , 
 and by the next day 's post a letter be write to he by Matilda , 
 inform he that the Earl be prepared to receive he with open 
 arm . " Papa have be over there and manage it all , " say Matilda . 
 " I be tell George Morris be not   go to stand , " say Barrington Erle 
 to Phineas the night before his departure . 
 " his brother will not   support he . his brother mean to support I , " 
 say Phineas . 
 " that can hardly be so . " 
 " but I tell you it be . my father have know the Earl these twenty 
 year , and have manage it . " 
 " I say , Finn , you be not go to play we a trick , be you ? " say Mr. 
 Erle , with something like dismay in his voice . 
 " what sort of trick ? " 
 " you be not come out on the other side ? " 
 " not if I know it , " say Phineas , proudly . " let I assure you I 
 would not   change my view in politic either for you or for the Earl , 
 though each of you carry seat in your breech pocket . if I go 
 into Parliament , I shall go there as a sound liberal , - not to support 
 a party , but to do the good I can for the country . I tell you so , and 
 I shall tell the Earl the same . " 
 Barrington Erle turn away in disgust . such language be to he 
 simply disgusting . it fall upon his ear as false maudlin sentiment 
 fall on the ear of the ordinary honest man of the world . Barrington 
 Erle be a man ordinarily honest . he would not have be untrue to 
 his mother 's brother , William Mildmay , the great Whig Minister of the 
 day , for any earthly consideration . he be ready to work with wage 
 or without wage . he be really zealous in the cause , not ask 
 very much for himself . he have some undefined belief that it be much 
 well for the country that Mr. Mildmay should be in power than 
 that Lord de Terrier should be there . he be convince that Liberal 
 politic be good for Englishmen , and that Liberal politic and the 
 Mildmay party be one and the same thing . it would be unfair to 
 Barrington Erle to deny to he some praise for patriotism . but he 
 hat the very name of independence in Parliament , and when he be 
 tell of any man , that that man intend to look to measure and not 
 to man , he regard that man as be both unstable as water and 
 dishonest as the wind . no good could possibly come from such a one , 
 and much evil might and probably would come . such a politician be a 
 Greek to Barrington Erle , from whose hand he fear to accept even 
 the gift of a vote . parliamentary hermit be distasteful to he , 
 and dweller in political cave be regard by he with aversion 
 as be either knavish or impractical . with a good conservative 
 opponent he could shake hand almost as readily as with a good Whig 
 ally ; but the man who be neither flesh nor fowl be odious to he . 
 accord to his theory of parliamentary government , the House of 
 Commons should be divide by a marked line , and every member should 
 be require to stand on one side of it or on the other . " if not 
 with I , at any rate be against I , " he would have say to every 
 representative of the people in the name of the great leader whom he 
 follow . he think that debate be good , because of the people 
 outside , - because they serve to create that public opinion which be 
 hereafter to be use in create some future House of Commons ; but he 
 do not think it possible that any vote should be give on a great 
 question , either this way or that , as the result of a debate ; and he 
 be certainly assure in his own opinion that any such change of 
 vote would be dangerous , revolutionary , and almost unparliamentary . 
 a member 's vote , - except on some small crotchety open question throw 
 out for the amusement of crotchety member , - be due to the leader of 
 that member 's party . such be Mr. Erle 's idea of the english system 
 of Parliament , and , lend semi - official assistance as he do 
 frequently to the introduction of candidate into the House , he be 
 naturally anxious that his candidate should be candidate after his 
 own heart . when , therefore , Phineas Finn talk of measure and not 
 man , Barrington Erle turn away in open disgust . but he remember 
 the youth and extreme rawness of the lad , and he remember also the 
 career of other man . 
 Barrington Erle be forty , and experience have teach he something . 
 after a few second , he bring himself to think mildly of the young 
 man 's vanity , - as of the vanity of a plunge colt who resent the 
 liberty even of a touch . " by the end of the first session the thong 
 will be crack over his head , as he patiently assist in pull the 
 coach up hill , without produce from he even a flick of his tail , " 
 say Barrington Erle to an old parliamentary friend . 
 " if he be to come out after all on the wrong side , " say the 
 parliamentary friend . 
 Erle admit that such a trick as that would be unpleasant , but 
 he think that old Lord Tulia be hardly equal to so clever a 
 stratagem . 
 Phineas go to Ireland , and walk over the course at Loughshane . 
 he call upon Lord Tulla , and hear that venerable nobleman talk a 
 great deal of nonsense . to tell the truth of Phineas , I must confess 
 that he wish to talk the nonsense himself ; but the Earl would not 
 hear he , and put he down very quickly . " we will not   discuss politic ,

 time come ; and so , for the present , he allow the Earl to ramble 
 on about the sin of his brother George , and the want of all proper 
 pedigree on the part of the new Dean of Kilfenora . the conference 
 end with an assurance on the part of Lord Tulla that if the 
 Loughshaners choose to elect Mr. Phineas Finn he would not be in the 
 least offend . the elector do elect Mr. Phineas Finn , - perhaps 
 for the reason give by one of the Dublin Conservative paper , which 
 declare that it be all the fault of the Carlton Club in not send 
 a proper candidate . there be a great deal say about the matter , 
 both in London and Dublin , and the blame be suppose to fall on

 meantime , our hero , Phineas Finn , have be duly elect member of 
 Parliament for the borough of Loughshane . 
 the Finn family could not restrain their triumphing at Killaloe , and 
 I do not know that it would have be natural have they do so . a 
 gosling from such a flock do become something of a real swan by 
 get into Parliament . the doctor have his misgiving , - have great 
 misgiving , fearful foreboding ; but there be the young man elect , 
 and he could not help it . he could not refuse his right hand to his 
 son or withdraw his paternal assistance because that son have be

 out of his hoard what suffice to pay off outstanding debt , - they 
 be not heavy , - and undertake to allow Phineas two hundred and fifty 
 pound a year as long as the session should last . 
 there be a widow lady live at Killaloe who be name Mrs. Flood 
 Jones , and she have a daughter . she have a son also , bear to inherit 
 the property of the late Floscabel Flood Jones of Floodborough , as

 he , now serve with his regiment in India , we shall have no

 widow 's jointure , - Floodborough have , to tell the truth , pretty 
 nearly fall into absolute ruin , - and with her one daughter , Mary . 
 now on the evening before the return of Phineas Finn , Esq . , M.P. , to 
 London , Mrs. and Miss Flood Jones drink tea at the doctor 's house . 
 " it will not   make a bit of change in he , " Barbara Finn say to her 
 friend Mary , up in some bedroom privacy before the tea - drinking 
 ceremony have altogether commence . 
 " oh , it must , " say Mary . 
 " I tell you it will not , my dear ; he be so good and so true . " 
 " I know he be good , Barbara ; and as for truth , there be no question 
 about it , because he have never say a word to I that he might not 
 say to any girl . " 
 " that be nonsense , Mary . " 
 " he never have , then , as sure as the bless Virgin watch over 
 we ; - only you do not   believe she do . " 
 " never mind about the Virgin now , Mary . " 
 " but he never have . your brother be nothing to I , Barbara . " 
 " then I hope he will be before the evening be over . he be walk 
 with you all yesterday and the day before . " 
 " why should not   he , - and we that have know each other all our life ? 
 but , Barbara , pray , pray never say a word of this to any one ! " 
 " be it I ? would not   I cut out my tongue first ? " 
 " I do not   know why I let you talk to I in this way . there have never 
 be anything between I and Phineas , - your brother I mean . " 
 " I know whom you mean very well . " 
 " and I feel quite sure that there never will be . why should there ? 
 he will go out among great people and be a great man ; and I have   already 
 find out that there be a certain Lady Laura Standish whom he admire 
 very much . " 
 " Lady Laura Fiddlestick ! " 
 " a man in Parliament , you know , may look up to anybody , " say Miss 
 Mary Flood Jones . 
 " I want Phin to look up to you , my dear . " 
 " that would not   be look up . place as he be now , that would be 
 look down ; and he be so proud that he will never do that . but come 
 down , dear , else they will wonder where we be . " 
 Mary Flood Jones be a little girl about twenty year of age , with 
 the soft hair in the world , of a colour varying between brown and 
 auburn , - for sometimes you would swear it be the one and sometimes 
 the other ; and she be as pretty as ever she could be . she be one 
 of those girl , so common in Ireland , whom man , with taste that way 
 give , feel inclined to take up and devour on the spur of the moment ; 
 and when she like her lion , she have a look about she which seem to 
 ask to be devour . there be girl so cold - look , - pretty girl , 
 too , ladylike , discreet , and arm with all accomplishment , - whom to 
 attack seem to require the same sort of courage , and the same sort 
 of preparation , as a journey in quest of the north - west passage . one 
 think of a pedestal near the Athenaeum as the most appropriate and 
 most honourable reward of such courage . but , again , there be other 
 girl to abstain from attack whom be , to a man of any warmth 
 of temperament , quite impossible . they be like water when one be 
 athirst , like plover ' egg in March , like cigar when one be out 
 in the autumn . no one ever dream of deny himself when such 
 temptation come in the way . it often happen , however , that in spite 
 of appearance , the water will not come from the well , nor the egg 
 from its shell , nor will the cigar allow itself to be light . a girl of 
 such appearance , so charming , be Mary Flood Jones of Killaloe , and 
 our hero Phineas be not allow to thirst in vain for a drop from 
 the cool spring . 
 when the girl go down into the drawing - room Mary be careful to 
 go to a part of the room quite remote from Phineas , so as to seat 
 herself between Mrs. Finn and Dr. Finn 's young partner , Mr. Elias 
 Bodkin , from Ballinasloe . but Mrs. Finn and the Miss Finns and all 
 Killaloe know that Mary have no love for Mr. Bodkin , and when Mr. 
 Bodkin hand she the hot cake she hardly so much as smile at he . 
 but in two minute Phineas be behind her chair , and then she smile ;

 in two more minute Barbara have return to Mr. Elias Bodkin , so that 
 Phineas and Mary be uninterrupte . they manage these thing very 
 quickly and very cleverly in Killaloe . 
 " I shall be off to - morrow morning by the early train , " say Phineas . 
 " so soon ; - and when will you have to begin , - in Parliament , I mean ? " 
 " I shall have to take my seat on Friday . I be go back just in 
 time . " 
 " but when shall we hear of your say something ? " 
 " never , probably . not one in ten who go into Parliament ever do say 
 anything . " 
 " but you will ; will not   you ? I hope you will . I do so hope you will 
 distinguish yourself ; - because of your sister , and for the sake of 
 the town , you know . " 
 " and be that all , Mary ? " 
 " be not   that enough ? " 
 " you do not   care a bit about myself , then ? " 
 " you know that I do . have not   we be friend ever since we be 
 child ? of course it will be a great pride to I that a person whom 
 I have know so intimately should come to be talk about as a great 
 man . " 
 " I shall never be talk about as a great man . " 
 " you be a great man to I already , be in Parliament . only 
 think ; - I never see a member of Parliament in my life before . " 
 " you have   see the bishop score of time . " 
 " be he in Parliament ? ah , but not like you . he could not   come to be 
 a Cabinet Minister , and one never read anything about he in the 
 newspaper . I shall expect to see your name , very often , and I shall 
 always look for it . ' Mr. Phineas Finn pair off with Mr. Mildmay . ' 
 what be the meaning of pair off ? " 
 " I will explain it all to you when I come back , after learn my 
 lesson . " 
 " mind you do come back . but I do not   suppose you ever will . you will 
 be go somewhere to see Lady Laura Standish when you be not want 
 in Parliament . " 
 " Lady Laura Standish ! " 
 " and why should not   you ? of course , with your prospect , you should 
 go as much as possible among people of that sort . be Lady Laura very 
 pretty ? " 
 " she be about six foot high . " 
 " Nonsense . I do not   believe that . " 
 " she would look as though she be , stand by you . " 
 " because I be so insignificant and small . " 
 " because your figure be perfect , and because she be straggle . she 
 be as unlike you as possible in everything . she have thick lumpy red 
 hair , while yours be all silk and softness . she have large hand and 
 foot , and - " 
 " why , Phineas , you be make she out to be an ogress , and yet I know

 " so I do , because she possess such an appearance of power . and 
 after all , in spite of the lumpy hair , and in spite of large hand 
 and straggle figure , she be handsome . one can not   tell what it be . 
 one can see that she be quite content with herself , and intend to 
 make other content with she . and so she do . " 
 " I see you be in love with she , Phineas . " 
 " no ; not in love , - not with she at least . of all man in the world , I 
 suppose that I be the last that have a right to be in love . I daresay 
 I shall marry some day . " 
 " I be sure I hope you will . " 
 " but not till I be forty or perhaps fifty year old . if I be not fool 
 enough to have what man call a high ambition I might venture to be in 
 love now . " 
 " I be sure I be very glad that you have   get a high ambition . it be what 
 every man ought to have ; and I have   no doubt that we shall hear of your 
 marriage soon , - very soon . and then , - if she can help you in your

 Phineas do not say a word far then . perhaps some commotion among 
 the party break up the little private conversation in the corner . and

 Flood Jones be finish some important narrative to his mother . it

 from pass , and so give he the opportunity which he abuse .

 love - making beyond what the reader have hear , - " one kiss before we

 " no , Phineas , no ! " but the kiss have be take and give before she 
 have even answer he . " oh , Phineas , you should not ! " 
 " I should . why should not   I ? and , Mary , I will have one morsel of your

 " Bother ! do you go to bed , and do not   trouble yourself about such 
 trifle . but mind you be up , old girl , to see I off in the morning . " 
 everybody be up to see he off in the morning , to give he coffee

 sake to be careful about his money . his mother tell he always to

 usual . and Barbara in a last whisper beg he never to forget dear 
 Mary Flood Jones . 
 CHAPTER III 
 Phineas Finn take his Seat 
 Phineas have many serious , almost solemn thought on his journey 
 towards London . I be sorry I must assure my female reader that very 
 few of they have reference to Mary Flood Jones . he have , however , very 
 carefully pack up the tress , and could bring that out for proper 
 act of erotic worship at season in which his mind might be less 
 engage with affair of state than it be at present . would he make a 
 failure of this great matter which he have take in hand ? he could not 
 but tell himself that the chance be twenty to one against he . now 
 that he look near at it all , the difficulty loom large than 
 ever , and the reward seem to be less , more difficult of approach , 
 and more evanescent . how many member be there who could never get 
 a hearing ! how many who only speak to fail ! how many , who speak well , 
 who could speak to no effect as far as their own worldly prospect 
 be concern ! he have already know many member of Parliament to 
 whom no outward respect or sign of honour be ever give by any one ; 
 and it seem to he , as he think over it , that irish member of 
 Parliament be generally treat with more indifference than any 
 other . there be o'b - - and O'C - - and O'D - - , for whom no one 
 care a straw , who could hardly get man to dine with they at the 
 club , and yet they be genuine member of Parliament . why should he 
 ever be well than O'B - - , or O'C - - , or O'D - - ? and in what way 
 should he begin to be well ? he have an idea of the fashion after 
 which it would be his duty to strive that he might excel those 
 gentleman . he do not give any of they credit for much earnestness 
 in their country 's behalf , and he be minded to be very earnest . he 
 would go to his work honestly and conscientiously , determine to do 
 his duty as good he might , let the result to himself be what they 
 would . this be a noble resolution , and might have be pleasant to 
 he , - have he not remember that smile of derision which have come 
 over his friend Erle 's face when he declare his intention of do 
 his duty to his country as a Liberal , and not of support a party . 
 o'b - - and O'C - - and O'D - - be keen enough to support their 
 party , only they be sometimes a little astray at know which 
 be their party for the nonce . he know that Erle and such man would 
 despise he if he do not fall into the regular groove , - and if the 
 Barrington Erles despise he , what would then be leave for he ? 
 his moody thought be somewhat dissipate when he find one 
 Laurence Fitzgibbon , - the Honourable Laurence Fitzgibbon , - a special 
 friend of his own , and a very clever fellow , on board the boat as it 
 steam out of Kingston harbour . Laurence Fitzgibbon have also just 
 be over about his election , and have be return as a matter of 
 course for his father 's county . Laurence Fitzgibbon have sit in the 
 House for the last fifteen year , and be yet well - nigh as young a 
 man as any in it . and he be a man altogether different from the 
 O'B - - s , O'C - - s , and O'D - - s. Laurence Fitzgibbon could always 
 get the ear of the House if he choose to speak , and his friend 
 declare that he might have be high up in office long since if he 
 would have take the trouble to work . he be a welcome guest at the 
 house of the very good people , and be a friend of whom any one 
 might be proud . it have for two year be a feather in the cap of 
 Phineas that he know Laurence Fitzgibbon . and yet people say that 
 Laurence Fitzgibbon have nothing of his own , and man wonder how he 
 live . he be the young son of Lord Claddagh , an irish peer with a 
 large family , who could do nothing for Laurence , his favourite child , 
 beyond find he a seat in Parliament . 
 " well , Finn , my boy , " say Laurence , shake hand with the young 
 member on board the steamer , ' so you have   make it all right at 
 Loughshane . " then Phineas be begin to tell all the story , 
 the wonderful story , of George Morris and the Earl of Tulla , - how 
 the man of Loughshane have elect he without opposition ; how he 
 have be support by conservative as well as Liberals ; - how 
 unanimous Loughshane have be in elect he , Phineas Finn , as its 
 representative . but Mr. Fitzgibbon seem to care very little about 
 all this , and go so far as to declare that those thing be 
 accident which fall out sometimes one way and sometimes another , 
 and be altogether independent of any merit or demerit on the part 
 of the candidate himself . and it be marvellous and almost painful 
 to Phineas that his friend Fitzgibbon should accept the fact of his 
 membership with so little of congratulation , - with absolutely no 
 blowing of trumpet whatever . have he be elect a member of the 
 municipal corporation of Loughshane , instead of its representative in 
 the British Parliament , Laurence Fitzgibbon could not have make less 
 fuss about it . Phineas be disappoint , but he take the cue from his 
 friend too quickly to show his disappointment . and when , half an hour 
 after their meeting , Fitzgibbon have to be remind that his companion 
 be not in the House during the last session , Phineas be able to 
 make the remark as though he think as little about the House as do 
 the old - accustomed member himself . 
 " as far as I can see as yet , " say Fitzgibbon , " we be sure to have 
 seventeen . " 
 " Seventeen ? " say Phineas , not quite understand the meaning of the 
 number quote . 
 " a majority of seventeen . there be four irish county and three 
 Scotch which have not   return as yet ; but we know pretty well what 
 they will do . there be a doubt about Tipperary , of course , but whichever 
 get in of the seven who be stand , it will be a vote on our side . 
 now the Government can not   live against that . the uphill strain be too 
 much for they . " 
 " accord to my idea , nothing can justify they in try to live 
 against a majority . " 
 " that be gammon . when the thing be so equal , anything be fair . but you 
 see they do not   like it . of course there be some among they as hungry 
 as we be ; and Dubby would give his toe and finger to remain in . " 
 Dubby be the ordinary name by which , among friend and foe , Mr. 
 Daubeny be know : Mr. Daubeny , who at that time be the leader of 
 the conservative party in the House of Commons . " but most of they , " 
 continue Mr. Fitzgibbon , " prefer the other game , and if you do not 
 care about money , upon my word it be the pleasanter game of the two . " 
 " but the country get nothing do by a Tory Government . " 
 " as to that , it be six of one and half a dozen of the other . I never 
 know a government yet that want to do anything . give a government 
 a real strong majority , as the Tories use to have half a century 
 since , and as a matter of course it will do nothing . why should 
 it ? do thing , as you call it , be only bid for power , - for 
 patronage and pay . " 
 " and be the country to have no service do ? " 
 " the country get quite as much service as it pay for , - and perhaps 
 a little more . the clerk in the office work for the country . and 
 the Ministers work too , if they have   get anything to manage . there be 
 plenty of work do ; - but of work in Parliament , the less the well , 
 accord to my idea . it be very little that ever be do , and that 
 little be generally too much . " 
 " but the people - " 
 " come down and have a glass of brandy - and - water , and leave the people 
 alone for the present . the people can take care of themselves a great 
 deal well than we can take care of they . " Mr. Fitzgibbon 's doctrine 
 as to the commonwealth be very different from that of Barrington 
 Erle , and be still less to the taste of the new member . Barrington 
 Erle consider that his leader , Mr. Mildmay , should be intrust to 
 make all necessary change in the law , and that an obedient House of 
 Commons should implicitly obey that leader in authorise all change 
 propose by he ; - but accord to Barrington Erle , such change 
 should be numerous and of great importance , and would , if duly pass 
 into law at his lord 's behest , gradually produce such a Whig Utopia 
 in England as have never yet be see on the face of the earth . 
 now , accord to Mr. Fitzgibbon , the present Utopia would be good 
 enough , - if only he himself might be once more put into possession 
 of a certain semi - political place about the Court , from which he have 
 heretofore draw L1,000 per annum , without any work , much to his 
 comfort . he make no secret of his ambition , and be chagrin simply 
 at the prospect of have to return to his elector before he could 
 enjoy those good thing which he expect to receive from the 
 undoubted majority of seventeen , which have be , or would be , 
 achieve . 
 " I hate all change as a rule , " say Fitzgibbon ; " but , upon my word , 
 we ought to alter that . when a fellow have get a crumb of comfort , 
 after wait for it year and year , and perhaps spend thousand 
 in election , he have to go back and try his hand again at the last 
 moment , merely in obedience to some antiquated prejudice . look at 
 poor Jack Bond , - the good friend I ever have in the world . he be 
 wreck upon that rock for ever . he spend every shilling he have in 
 contest Romford three time run , - and three time run 
 he get in . then they make he Vice - Comptroller of the Granaries , 
 and I be shoot if he do not   get spilt at Romford on stand for his 
 re - election ! " 
 " and what become of he ? " 
 " God know . I think I hear that he marry an old woman and settle 
 down somewhere . I know he never come up again . now , I call that a 
 confound shame . I suppose I be safe down in Mayo , but there be no 
 know what may happen in these day . " 
 as they part at Euston Square , Phineas ask his friend some little 
 nervous question as to the good mode of make a first entrance into 
 the House . would Laurence Fitzgibbon see he through the difficulty 
 of the oath - taking ? but Laurence Fitzgibbon make very little of the 
 difficulty . " oh ; - you just come down , and there will be a rush of 
 fellow , and you will know everybody . you will have to hang about for an 
 hour or so , and then you will get push through . there be not   time for 
 much ceremony after a general election . " 
 Phineas reach London early in the morning , and go home to bed 
 for an hour or so . the House be to meet on that very day , and he 
 intend to begin his parliamentary duty at once if he should find 
 it possible to get some one to accompany he ; he feel that he should 
 lack courage to go down to Westminster Hall alone , and explain to 
 the policeman and door - keeper that he be the man who have just be 
 elect member for Loughshane . so about noon he go into the Reform 
 Club , and there he find a great crowd of man , among whom there be a 
 plentiful sprinkling of member . Erle see he in a moment , and come 
 to he with congratulation . 
 " so you be all right , Finn , " say he . 
 " yes ; I be all right , - I do not   have much doubt about it when I go 
 over . " 
 " I never hear of a fellow with such a run of luck , " say Erle . " it be 
 just one of those fluke that occur once in a dozen election . any 
 one on earth might have get in without spend a shilling . " 
 Phineas do not   at all like this . " I do not   think any one could have 
 get in , " say he , " without know Lord Tulla . " 
 " Lord Tulla be nowhere , my dear boy , and could have nothing to say 
 to it . but never mind that . you meet I in the lobby at two . there will 
 be a lot of we there , and we will go in together . have you see 
 Fitzgibbon ? " then Barrington Erle go off to other business , and 
 Finn be congratulate by other man . but it seem to he that the 
 congratulation of his friend be not hearty . he speak to some man , 
 of whom he think that he know they would have give their eye 
 to be in Parliament ; - and yet they speak of his success as be a 
 very ordinary thing . " well , my boy , I hope you like it , " say one 
 middle - aged gentleman whom he have know ever since he come up to 
 London . " the difference be between work for nothing and work 
 for money . you will have to work for nothing now . " 
 " that be about it , I suppose , " say Phineas . 
 " they say the House be a comfortable club , " say the middle - aged 
 friend , " but I confess that I should not   like be ring away from my 
 dinner myself . " 
 at two punctually Phineas be in the lobby at Westminster , and then 
 he find himself take into the House with a crowd of other man . the 
 old and young , and they who be neither old nor young , be mingle 
 together , and there seem to be very little respect of person . on 
 three or four occasion there be some cheer when a popular man or 
 a great leader come in ; but the work of the day leave but little clear 
 impression on the mind of the young member . he be confused , half 
 elated , half disappointed , and have not his wit about he . he find 
 himself constantly regret that he be there ; and as constantly 
 tell himself that he , hardly yet twenty - five , without a shilling 
 of his own , have achieve an entrance into that assembly which by the 
 consent of all man be the great in the world , and which many of 
 the rich magnate of the country have in vain spend heap of treasure 
 in their endeavour to open to their own footstep . he try hard to 
 realise what he have gain , but the dust and the noise and the crowd 
 and the want of something august to the eye be almost too strong 
 for he . he manage , however , to take the oath early among those who 
 take it , and hear the Queen s speech read and the Address move and 
 seconded . he be seat very uncomfortably , high up on a back seat , 
 between two man whom he do not know ; and he find the speech to be 
 very long . he have be in the habit of see such speech report 
 in about a column , and he think that these speech must take at 
 least four column each . he sit out the debate on the Address till 
 the House be adjourn , and then he go away to dine at his club . 
 he do go into the dining - room of the House , but there be a crowd 
 there , and he find himself alone , - and to tell the truth , he be 
 afraid to order his dinner . 
 the near approach to a triumph which he have in London come to he 
 from the glory which his election reflect upon his landlady . she

 CHAPTER IV 
 Lady Laura Standish

 the House have meet on a Friday , again on the Saturday morning , and

 Mr. and Mrs. Low

 aspiration with infirm purpose ; - or , as we may say , strong body
