 CHAPTER VII 
 Mr. and Mrs. Bunce 
 it be three o'clock on the Thursday night before Mr. Daubeny 's 
 speech be finish . I do not think that there be any truth in the 
 allegation make at the time , that he continue on his leg an hour 
 long than the necessity of his speech require , in order that 
 five or six very ancient whig might be weary out and shrink to 
 their bed . let a Whig have be ever so ancient and ever so weary , 
 he would not have be allow to depart from Westminster Hall that 
 night . Sir Everard Powell be there in his bath - chair at twelve , 
 with a doctor on one side of he and a friend on the other , in some 
 purlieu of the House , and do his duty like a fine old Briton as he 
 be . that speech of Mr. Daubeny 's will never be forget by any one 
 who hear it . its studied bitterness have perhaps never be equal , 
 and yet not a word be utter for the saying of which he could be 
 accuse of go beyond the limit of parliamentary antagonism . it be 
 true that personality could not have be close , that accusation 
 of political dishonesty and of almost bad than political cowardice 
 and falsehood could not have be clear , that no word in the 
 language could have attribute mean motif or more unscrupulous 
 conduct . but , nevertheless , Mr. Daubeny in all that he say be 
 parliamentary , and show himself to be a gladiator thoroughly well 
 train for the arena in which he have descend to the combat . his 
 arrow be poison , and his lance be barb , and his shot be 
 heated red , - because such thing be allow . he do not poison 
 his enemy ' well or use greek fire , because those thing be not 
 allow . he know exactly the rule of the combat . Mr. Mildmay sit and 
 hear he without once raise his hat from his brow , or speak 
 a word to his neighbour . man on both side of the House say that 
 Mr. Mildmay suffer terribly ; but as Mr. Mildmay utter no word of 
 complaint to any one , and be quite ready to take Mr. Daubeny by the 
 hand the next time they meet in company , I do not know that any one 
 be able to form a true idea of Mr. Mildmay 's feeling . Mr. Mildmay 
 be an impassive man who rarely speak of his own feeling , and no 
 doubt sit with his hat low down over his eye in order that no 
 man might judge of they on that occasion by the impression on his 
 feature . " if he could have leave off half an hour early it would 
 have be perfect as an attack , " say Barrington Erle in criticise 
 Mr. Daubeny 's speech , " but he allow himself to sink into 
 comparative weakness , and the glory of it be over before the 
 end . " - then come the division . the Liberals have 333 vote to 314 for 
 the Conservatives , and therefore count a majority of 19 . it be 
 say that so large a number of member have never before vote at any 
 division . 
 " I own I be disappointed , " say Barrington Erle to Mr. Ratler . 
 " I think there would be twenty , " say Mr. Ratler . " I never go 
 beyond that . I know they would have old Moody up , but I think 
 Gunning would have be too hard for they . " 
 " they say they have   promise they both peerage . " 
 " yes ; - if they remain in . but they know they be go out . " 
 " they must go , with such a majority against they , " say Barrington 
 Erle . 
 " of course they must , " say Mr. Ratler . " Lord de Terrier want 
 nothing well , but it be rather hard upon poor Daubeny . I never see 
 such an unfortunate old Tantalus . " 
 " he get a good drop of real water now and again , and I do not   pity 
 he in the least . he be clever of course , and have make his own way , 
 but I have   always a feeling that he have no business where he be . 
 I suppose we shall know all about it at Brooks 's by one o'clock 
 to - morrow . " 
 Phineas , though it have be past five before he go to bed , - for 
 there have be much triumphant talk to be do among liberal 
 member after the division , - be up at his breakfast at Mrs. Bunce 's 
 lodging by nine . there be a matter which he be call upon to 
 settle immediately in which Mrs. Bunce herself be much interested , 
 and respect which he have promise to give an answer on this very 
 morning . a set of very dingy chamber up two pair of stair at No . 
 9 , Old Square , Lincoln 's Inn , to which Mr. Low have recommend he to 
 transfer himself and all his belonging , be wait his occupation , 
 should he resolve upon occupy they . if he intend to commence 
 operation as a barrister , it would be necessary that he should have 
 chamber and a clerk ; and before he have leave Mr. Low 's house on 
 Sunday evening he have almost give that gentleman authority to secure 
 for he these room at no . 9 . " whether you remain in Parliament or 
 no , you must make a beginning , " Mr. Low have say ; " and how be you 
 even to pretend to begin if you do not   have chamber ? " Mr. Low hop 
 that he might be able to wean Phineas away from his Parliament 
 bauble ; - that he might induce the young barrister to give up his 
 madness , if not this session or the next , at any rate before a third 
 year have commence . Mr. Low be a persistent man , like very much 
 when he do like , and love very strongly when he do love . he would 
 have many a tug for Phineas Finn before he would allow that false 
 Westminster Satan to carry off the prey as altogether his own . if he 
 could only get Phineas into the dingy chamber he might do much ! 
 but Phineas have now become so imbued with the atmosphere of politic , 
 have be so breathe upon by Lady Laura and Barrington Erle , that 
 he could no long endure the thought of any other life than that 
 of a life spend among the lobby . a desire to help to beat the 
 conservative have fasten on his very soul , and almost make Mr. Low 
 odious in his eye . he be afraid of Mr. Low , and for the nonce would 
 not go to he any more ; - but he must see the porter at Lincoln 's Inn , 
 he must write a line to Mr. Low , and he must tell Mrs. Bunce that for 
 the present he would still keep on her room . his letter to Mr. Low 
 be as follow : - 
 Great Marlborough Street , May , 186 - . 
 MY DEAR LOW , 
 I have make up my mind against take the chamber , and be 
 now off to the Inn to say that I shall not want they . of 
 course , I know what you will think of I , and it be very 
 grievous to I to have to bear the hard judgment of a man 
 whose opinion I value so highly ; but , in the tooth of your 
 terribly strong argument , I think that there be something 
 to be say on my side of the question . this seat in 
 Parliament have come in my way by chance , and I think it 
 would be pusillanimous in I to reject it , feel , as I 
 do , that a seat in Parliament confer very great honour . I 
 be , too , very fond of politic , and regard legislation as 
 the fine profession go . have I any one dependent on 
 I , I probably might not be justify in follow the 
 bent of my inclination . but I be all alone in the world , 
 and therefore have a right to make the attempt . if , after 
 a trial of one or two session , I should fail in that 
 which I be attempt , it will not even then be too late 
 to go back to the well way . I can assure you that at any 
 rate it be not my intention to be idle . 
 I know very well how you will fret and fume over what I 
 say , and how utterly I shall fail in bring you round to 
 my way of thinking ; but as I must write to tell you of my 
 decision , I can not refrain from defend myself to the 
 good of my ability . 
 your always faithfully , 
 phinea FINN . 
 Mr. Low receive this letter at his chamber , and when he have read 
 it , he simply press his lip closely together , place the sheet 
 of paper back in its envelope , and put it into a drawer at his left 
 hand . have do this , he go on with what work he have before he , 
 as though his friend 's decision be a matter of no consequence to 
 he . as far as he be concern the thing be do , and there should 
 be an end of it . so he tell himself ; but nevertheless his mind be 
 full of it all day ; and , though he write not a word of answer to 
 Phineas , he make a reply within his own mind to every one of the 
 argument use in the letter . " great honour ! how can there be honour 
 in what come , as he say , by chance ? he have not   sense enough to 
 understand that the honour come from the mode of win it , and 
 from the mode of wear it ; and that the very fact of his being 
 member for Loughshane at this instant simply prove that Loughshane 
 should have have no privilege to return a member ! no one dependent on 
 he ! be not his father and his mother and his sister dependent on 
 he as long as he must eat their bread till he can earn bread of his 
 own ? he will never earn bread of his own . he will always be eat 
 bread that other have earn . " in this way , before the day be 
 over , Mr. Low become very angry , and swear to himself that he would 
 have nothing more to say to Phineas Finn . but yet he find himself 
 create plan for encounter and conquer the parliamentary 
 fiend who be at present so cruelly potent with his pupil . it be not 
 till the third evening that he tell his wife that Finn have make up 
 his mind not to take chamber . " then I would have nothing more to say 
 to he , " say Mrs. Low , savagely . " for the present I can have nothing 
 more to say to he . " " but neither now nor ever , " say Mrs. Low , with 
 great emphasis ; " he have be false to you . " " no , " say Mr. Low , who 
 be a man thoroughly and thoughtfully just at all point ; " he have not 
 be false to I . he have always mean what he have say , when he be 
 say it . but he be weak and blind , and fly like a moth to the 
 candle ; one pity the poor moth , and would save he a stump of his 
 wing if it be possible . " 
 Phineas , when he have write his letter to Mr. Low , start off for 
 Lincoln 's Inn , make his way through the well - know dreary street 
 of Soho , and through St. Giles 's , to Long Acre . he know every corner 
 well , for he have walk the same road almost daily for the last three 
 year . he have conceive a liking for the route , which he might easily 
 have change without much addition to the distance , by pass 
 through Oxford Street and Holborn ; but there be an air of business 
 on which he pride himself in go by the most direct passage , and 
 he declare to himself very often that thing dreary and dingy to the 
 eye might be good in themselves . Lincoln 's inn itself be dingy , and 
 the Law Courts therein be perhaps the mean in which Equity ever 
 disclose herself . Mr. Low 's three room in the Old Square , each of 
 they brown with the binding of law book and with the dust collect 
 on law paper , and with furniture that have be brown always , and have 
 become browner with year , be perhaps as unattractive to the eye of 
 a young pupil as any room which be ever enter . and the study of 
 the Chancery law itself be not an alluring pursuit till the mind have 
 come to have some insight into the beauty of its ultimate object . 
 Phineas , during his three year ' course of reasoning on these thing , 
 have teach himself to believe that thing ugly on the outside might 
 be very beautiful within ; and have therefore come to prefer cross 
 Poland Street and Soho Square , and so continue his travel by the 
 Seven Dials and Long Acre . his morning walk be of a piece with his 
 morning study , and he take pleasure in the gloom of both . but now 
 the taste of his palate have be already change by the glare of 
 the lamp in and about palatial Westminster , and he find that St. 
 Giles 's be disagreeable . the way about Pall Mall and across the 
 Park to Parliament Street , or to the Treasury , be much pleasanter , 
 and the new office in Downing Street , already half build , absorb 
 all that interest which he have hitherto be able to take in 
 the suggest but uncommenced erection of new Law Courts in the 
 neighbourhood of Lincoln 's Inn . as he make his way to the porter 's 
 lodge under the great gateway of Lincoln 's Inn , he tell himself that 
 he be glad that he have escape , at any rate for a while , from a life 
 so dull and dreary . if he could only sit in chamber at the Treasury 
 instead of chamber in that old court , how much pleasanter it would 
 be ! after all , as regard that question of income , it might well be 
 that the Treasury chamber should be the more remunerative , and the 
 more quickly remunerative , of the two . and , as he think , Lady Laura 
 might be compatible with the Treasury chamber and Parliament , but 
 could not possibly be make compatible with Old Square , Lincoln 's Inn . 
 but nevertheless there come upon he a feeling of sorrow when the 
 old man at the lodge seem to be rather glad than otherwise that 
 he do not want the chamber . " then Mr. Green can have they , " say 
 the porter ; " that will be good news for Mr. Green . I do not   know what 
 the gen'leman will do for chamber if thing go on as they be 
 go . " Mr. Green be welcome to the chamber as far as Phineas be 
 concern ; but Phineas feel nevertheless a certain amount of regret 
 that he should have be compel to abandon a thing which be 
 regard both by the porter and by Mr. Green as be so desirable . 
 he have however write his letter to Mr. Low , and make his promise to 
 Barrington Erle , and be bind to Lady Laura Standish ; and he walk 
 out through the old gateway into Chancery Lane , resolve that he 
 would not even visit Lincoln 's inn again for a year . there be 
 certain book , - law book , - which he would read at such interval of 
 leisure as politic might give he ; but within the precinct of the 
 Inns of Court he would not again put his foot for twelve month , let 
 learn pundit of the law , - such for instance as Mr. and Mrs. 
 Low , - say what they might . 
 he have tell Mrs. Bunce , before he leave his home after breakfast , that 
 he should for the present remain under her roof . she have be much 
 gratify , not simply because lodging in Great Marlborough Street 
 be less readily let than chamber in Lincoln 's Inn , but also because 
 it be a great honour to she to have a member of Parliament in her 
 house . member of Parliament be not so common about Oxford Street as 
 they be in the neighbourhood of Pall Mall and St. James 's Square . 
 but Mr. Bunce , when he come home to his dinner , do not join as 
 heartily as he should have do in his wife 's rejoicing . Mr. Bunce 
 be in the employment of certain copying law - stationer in Carey 
 Street , and have a strong belief in the law as a profession ; - but he 
 have none whatever in the House of Commons . " and he be give up go 
 into chamber ? " say Mr. Bunce to his wife . 
 " give it up altogether for the present , " say Mrs. Bunce . 
 " and he do not   mean to have no clerk ? " say Mr. Bunce . 
 " not unless it be for his Parliament work . " 
 " there be not   no clerk want for that , and what be bad , there be not 
 no fee to pay them . I will tell you what it be , Jane ; - if you do not 
 look sharp there will not   be nothing to pay you before long . " 
 " and he in Parliament , Jacob ! " 
 " there be not   no salary for be in Parliament . there be score of 
 they Parliament gent be not   get so much as'll pay their dinner for 
 them . and then if anybody do trust them , there be no getting at them 
 to make them pay as there be at other folk . " 
 " I do not   know that our Mr. Phineas will ever be like that , Jacob . " 
 " that be gammon , Jane . that be the way as woman get themselves take in 
 always . our Mr. Phineas ! why should our Mr. Phineas be well than 
 anybody else ? " 
 " he be always act handsome , Jacob . " 
 " there be one time he could not pay his lodging for wellnigh nine 
 month , till his governor come down with the money . I do not   know 
 whether that be handsome . it knock I about terrible , I know . " 
 " he always mean honest , Jacob . " 
 " I do not   know that I care much for a man 's meaning when he run 
 short of money . how be he go to see his way , with his seat in 
 Parliament , and this give up of his profession ? he owe we near a 
 quarter now . " 
 " he pay I two month this morning , Jacob ; so he do not   owe a 
 farthing . " 
 " very well ; - so much the well for we . I shall just have a few word 
 with Mr. Low , and see what he say to it . for myself I do not   think 
 half so much of Parliament folk as some do . they be for promise 
 everything before they be elect ; but not one in twenty of them be as 
 good as his word when he get there . " 
 Mr. Bunce be a copying journeyman , who spend ten hour a day in 
 Carey Street with a pen between his finger ; and after that he would 
 often spend two or three hour of the night with a pen between his 
 finger in Marlborough Street . he be a thoroughly hard - work man , 
 do pretty well in the world , for he have a good house over his 
 head , and always could find raiment and bread for his wife and 
 eight child ; but , nevertheless , he be an unhappy man because he 
 suffer from political grievance , or , I should more correctly say , 
 that his grievance be semi - political and semi - social . he have no 
 vote , not be himself the tenant of the house in Great Marlborough 
 Street . the tenant be a tailor who occupy the shop , whereas Bunce 
 occupy the whole of the remainder of the premise . he be a lodger , 
 and lodger be not as yet trust with the franchise . and he have 
 idea , which he himself admit to be very raw , as to the injustice 
 of the manner in which he be pay for his work . so much a folio , 
 without reference to the way in which his work be do , without 
 regard to the success of his work , with no question ask of 
 himself , be , as he think , no proper way of remunerate a man for 
 his labour . he have long since join a Trade Union , and for two 
 year past have pay a subscription of a shilling a week towards its 
 fund . he long to be do some battle against his superior , and 
 to be put himself in opposition to his employer ; - not that he 
 object personally to Messrs. Foolscap , Margin , and Vellum , who 
 always make much of he as a useful man ; - but because some such 
 antagonism would be manly , and the fighting of some battle would 
 be the right thing to do . " if Labour do not   mean to go to the wall 
 himself , " Bunce would say to his wife , " labour must look alive , and 
 put somebody else there . " 
 Mrs. Bunce be a comfortable motherly woman , who love her husband 
 but hat politic . as he have an aversion to his superior in the 
 world because they be superior , so have she a liking for they for 
 the same reason . she despise people poor than herself , and think 
 it a fair subject for boast that her child always have meat for 
 dinner . if it be ever so small a morsel , she take care that they have 
 it , in order that the boast might be maintain . the world have once 
 or twice be almost too much for she , - when , for instance , her 
 husband have be ill ; and again , to tell the truth , for the last 
 three month of that long period in which Phineas have omit to pay 
 his bill ; but she have keep a fine brave heart during those trouble , 
 and could honestly swear that the child always have a bit of 
 meat , though she herself have be occasionally without it for day 
 together . at such time she would be more than ordinarily meek to 
 Mr. Margin , and especially courteous to the old lady who lodge in 
 her first - floor drawing - room , - for Phineas live up two pair of 
 stair , - and she would excuse such servility by declare that there 
 be no know how soon she might want assistance . but her husband , 
 in such emergency , would become furious and quarrelsome , and would 
 declare that Labour be go to the wall , and that something very 
 strong must be do at once . that shilling which Bunce pay weekly to 
 the Union she regard as be absolutely throw away , - as much so 
 as though he cast it weekly into the Thames . and she have tell he so , 
 over and over again , make heart - pierce allusion to the eight 
 child and to the bit of meat . he would always endeavour to explain 
 to she that there be no other way under the sun for keep Labour 
 from be send to the wall ; - but he would do so hopelessly and 
 altogether ineffectually , and she have come to regard he as a lunatic 
 to the extent of that one weekly shilling . 
 she have a woman 's instinctive partiality for comeliness in a man , and 
 be very fond of Phineas Finn because he be handsome . and now she 
 be very proud of he because he be a member of Parliament . she 
 have hear , - from her husband , who have tell she the fact with much 
 disgust , - that the son of Dukes and Earls go into Parliament , and 
 she like to think that the fine young man to whom she talk more 
 or less every day should sit with the son of Dukes and Earls . when 
 Phineas have really bring distress upon she by owe she some thirty 
 or forty pound , she could never bring herself to be angry with 
 he , - because he be handsome and because he din out with Lords . 
 and she have triumph greatly over her husband , who have desire to be 
 severe upon his aristocratic debtor , when the money have all be pay 
 in a lump . 
 " I do not   know that he be any great catch , " Bunce have say , when the 
 prospect of their lodger 's departure have be debate between they . 
 " Jacob , " say his wife , " I do not   think you feel it when you have   get 
 people respectable about you . " 
 " the only respectable man I know , " say Jacob , " be the man as earn 
 his bread ; and Mr. Finn , as I take it , be a long way from that yet . " 
 Phineas return to his lodging before he go down to his club , and 
 again tell Mrs. Bunce that he have altogether make up his mind about 
 the chamber . " if you will keep I I shall stay here for the first 
 session I daresay . " 
 " of course we shall be only too proud , Mr. Finn ; and though it may not 
 perhaps be quite the place for a member of Parliament - " 
 " but I think it be quite the place . " 
 " it be very good of you to say so , Mr. Finn , and we will do our very 
 good to make you comfortable . respectable we be , I may say ; and 
 though Bunce be a bit rough sometimes - " 
 " never to I , Mrs. Bunce . " 
 " but he be rough , - and silly , too , with his radical nonsense , pay 
 a shilling a week to a nasty Union just for nothing . still he mean 
 well , and there be not   a man who work hard for his wife and 
 child ; - that I will say of he . and if he do talk politic - " 
 " but I like a man to talk politic , Mrs. Bunce . " 
 " for a gentleman in Parliament of course it be proper ; but I never 
 could see what good it could do to a law - stationer ; and when he talk 
 of Labour go to the wall , I always ask he whether he do not   get 
 his wage regular last Saturday . but , Lord love you , Mr. Finn , when a 
 man as be a journeyman have take up politic and join a Trade Union , 
 he be not   no well than a milestone for his wife to take and talk to 
 he . " 
 after that Phineas go down to the Reform Club , and make one of 
 those who be buzz there in little crowd and utter their 
 prophecy as to future event . Lord de Terrier be to go out . that 
 be certain . whether Mr. Mildmay be to come in be uncertain . that 
 he would go to Windsor to - morrow morning be not to be doubt ; but 
 it be think very probable that he might plead his age , and decline 
 to undertake the responsibility of form a Ministry . 
 " and what then ? " say Phineas to his friend Fitzgibbon . 
 " why , then there will be a choice out of three . there be the Duke , 
 who be the most incompetent man in England ; there be Monk , who be the 
 most unfit ; and there be Gresham , who be the most unpopular . I can not 
 conceive it possible to find a bad Prime Minister than either of 
 the three ; - but the country afford no other . " 
 " and which would Mildmay name ? " 
 " all of they , - one after the other , so as to make the embarrassment 
 the great . " that be Mr. Fitzgibbon 's description of the crisis ; 
 but then it be understand that Mr. Fitzgibbon be give to 
 romance . 
 CHAPTER VIII 
 the News about Mr. Mildmay and Sir Everard 
 Fitzgibbon and Phineas start together from Pall Mall for Portman 
 Square , - as both of they have promise to call on Lady Laura , - but 
 Fitzgibbon turn in at Brooks 's as they walk up St. James 's 
 Square , and Phineas go on by himself in a cab . " you should belong 
 here , " say Fitzgibbon as his friend enter the cab , and Phineas 
 immediately begin to feel that he would have do nothing till he 
 could get into Brooks 's . it might be very well to begin by talk 
 politic at the Reform Club . such talking have procure for he his 
 seat at Loughshane . but that be do now , and something more than 
 talk be want for any further progress . nothing , as he tell 
 himself , of political import be manage at the Reform Club . no 
 influence from thence be ever bring to bear upon the adjustment of 
 place under the Government , or upon the arrangement of cabinet . it 
 might be very well to count vote at the Reform Club ; but after the 
 vote have be count , - have be count successfully , - Brooks 's be 
 the place , as Phineas believe , to learn at the early moment what 
 would be the exact result of the success . he must get into Brooks 's , 
 if it might be possible for he . Fitzgibbon be not exactly the man 
 to propose he . perhaps the Earl of Brentford would do it . 
 Lady Laura be at home , and with she be sit - Mr. Kennedy . 
 Phineas have intend to be triumphant as he enter Lady Laura 's 
 room . he be there with the express purpose of triumphing in the 
 success of their great party , and of sing a pleasant paean in 
 conjunction with Lady Laura . but his trumpet be put out of tune at 
 once when he see Mr. Kennedy . he say hardly a word as he give his 
 hand to Lady Laura , - and then afterwards to Mr. Kennedy , who choose 
 to greet he with this show of cordiality . 
 " I hope you be satisfied , Mr. Finn , " say Lady Laura , laugh . 
 " oh yes . " 
 " and be that all ? I think to have find your joy quite 
 irrepressible . " 
 " a bottle of soda - water , though it be a very lively thing when 
 open , will not   maintain its vivacity beyond a certain period , Lady 
 Laura . " 
 " and you have have your gas let off already ? " 
 " well , - yes ; at any rate , the sputtering part of it . nineteen be very 
 well , but the question be whether we might not have have twenty - one . " 
 " Mr. Kennedy have just be say that not a single available vote 
 have be miss on our side . he have just come from Brooks 's , and that 
 seem to be what they say there . " 
 so Mr. Kennedy also be a member of Brooks 's ! at the Reform Club 
 there certainly have be an idea that the number might have be 
 swell to twenty - one ; but then , as Phineas begin to understand , 
 nothing be correctly know at the Reform Club . for an accurate 
 appreciation of the political balance of the day , you must go to 
 Brooks 's . 
 " Mr. Kennedy must of course be right , " say Phineas . " I do not 
 belong to Brooks 's myself . but I be only joke , Lady Laura . there 
 be , I suppose , no doubt that Lord de Terrier be out , and that be 
 everything . " 
 " he have probably tender his resignation , " say Mr. Kennedy . 
 " that be the same thing , " say Phineas , roughly . 
 " not exactly , " say Lady Laura . " should there be any difficulty about 
 Mr. Mildmay , he might , at the Queen 's request , make another attempt . " 
 " with a majority of nineteen against he ! " say Phineas . " surely Mr. 
 Mildmay be not the only man in the country . there be the Duke , and 
 there be Mr. Gresham , - and there be Mr. Monk . " Phineas have at his 
 tongue 's end all the lesson that he have be able to learn at the 
 Reform Club . 
 " I should hardly think the Duke would venture , " say Mr. Kennedy . 
 " nothing venture , nothing have , " say Phineas . " it be all very well 
 to say that the Duke be incompetent , but I do not know that anything 
 very wonderful be require in the way of genius . the Duke have hold 
 his own in both Houses successfully , and he be both honest and 
 popular . I quite agree that a Prime Minister at the present day 
 should be commonly honest , and more than commonly popular . " 
 " so you be all for the Duke , be you ? " say Lady Laura , again 
 smile as she speak to he . 
 " certainly ; - if we be desert by Mr. Mildmay . do not   you think so ? " 
 " I do not   find it quite so easy to make up my mind as you do . I be 
 inclined to think that Mr. Mildmay will form a government ; and as 
 long as there be that prospect , I need hardly commit myself to an 
 opinion as to his probable successor . " then the objectionable Mr. 
 Kennedy take his leave , and Phineas be leave alone with Lady Laura . 
 " it be glorious ; - be it not ? " he begin , as soon as he find the field 
 to be open for himself and his own manoeuvring . but he be very 
 young , and have not as yet learn the manner in which he might well 
 advance his cause with such a woman as Lady Laura Standish . he be 
 tell she too clearly that he could have no gratification in 
 talk with she unless he could be allow to have she all to 
 himself . that might be very well if Lady Laura be in love with he , 
 but would hardly be the way to reduce she to that condition . 
 " Mr. Finn , " say she , smile as she speak , " I be sure that you do 
 not mean it , but you be uncourteous to my friend Mr. Kennedy . " 
 " who ? I ? be I ? upon my word , I do not   intend to be uncourteous . " 
 " if I have think you have intend it , of course I could not tell you 
 of it . and now I take the liberty ; - for it be a liberty - " 
 " oh no . " 
 " because I feel so anxious that you should do nothing to mar your 
 chance as a rise man . " 
 " you be only too kind to I , - always . " 
 " I know how clever you be , and how excellent be all your instinct ; 
 but I see that you be a little impetuous . I wonder whether you will 
 be angry if I take upon myself the task of mentor . " 
 " nothing you could say would make I angry , - though you might make I 
 very unhappy . " 
 " I will not do that if I can help it . a mentor ought to be very old , 
 you know , and I be infinitely old than you be . " 
 " I should have think it be the reverse ; - indeed , I may say that I 
 know that it be , " say Phineas . 
 " I be not talk of year . year have very little to do with the 
 comparative age of man and woman . a woman at forty be quite old , 
 whereas a man at forty be young . " Phineas , remember that he have 
 put down Mr. Kennedy 's age as forty in his own mind , frown when 
 he hear this , and walk about the room in displeasure . " and 
 therefore , " continue Lady Laura , " I talk to you as though I be a 
 kind of grandmother . " 
 " you shall be my great - grandmother if you will only be kind enough to 
 I to say what you really think . " 
 " you must not then be so impetuous , and you must be a little 
 more careful to be civil to person to whom you may not take any 
 particular fancy . now Mr. Kennedy be a man who may be very useful to 
 you . " 
 " I do not want Mr. Kennedy to be of use to I . " 
 " that be what I call be impetuous , - be young , - be a boy . why 
 should not Mr. Kennedy be of use to you as well as any one else ? you 
 do not mean to conquer the world all by yourself . " 
 " no ; - but there be something mean to I in the express idea that 
 I should make use of any man , - and more especially of a man whom I 
 do not   like . " 
 " and why do you not like he , Mr. Finn ? " 
 " because he be one of my Dr. Fells . " 
 " you do not   like he simply because he do not talk much . that 
 may be a good reason why you should not make of he an intimate 
 companion , - because you like talkative people ; but it should be no 
 ground for dislike . " 
 Phineas pause for a moment before he answer she , think whether 
 or not it would be well to ask she some question which might produce 
 from she a truth which he would not like to hear . then he do ask it . 
 " and do you like he ? " he say . 
 she too pause , but only for a second . " yes , - I think I may say that 
 I do like he . " 
 " no more than that ? " 
 " certainly no more than that ; - but that I think be a great deal . " 
 " I wonder what you would say if any one ask you whether you like 
 I , " say Phineas , look away from she through the window . 
 " just the same ; - but without the doubt , if the person who question 
 I have any right to ask the question . there be not above one or two 
 who could have such a right . " 
 " and I be wrong , of course , to ask it about Mr. Kennedy , " say 
 Phineas , look out into the Square . 
 " I do not say so . " 
 " but I see you think it . " 
 " you see nothing of the kind . I be quite willing to be ask the 
 question by you , and quite willing to answer it . Mr. Kennedy be a man 
 of great wealth . " 
 " what can that have to do with it ? "

 CHAPTER IX 
 the New Government 
 in the House of Lords that night , and in the House of Commons , the 
 outgoing Ministers make their explanation . as our business at the 
 present moment be with the Commons , we will confine ourselves to 
 their chamber , and will do so the more willingly because the upshot 
 of what be say in the two place be the same . the outgoing 
 minister be very grave , very self - laudatory , and very courteous . 
 in regard to courtesy it may be declare that no stranger to the 
 way of the place could have understand how such soft word could be 
 speak by Mr. Daubeny , beat , so quickly after the very sharp word 
 which he have utter when he only expect to be beat . he announce 
 to his fellow - commoner that his right honourable friend and 
 colleague Lord de Terrier have think it right to retire from the 
 Treasury . Lord de Terrier , in constitutional obedience to the vote 
 of the Lower House , have resign , and the Queen have be graciously 
 pleased to accept Lord de Terrier 's resignation . Mr. Daubeny could 
 only inform the House that her Majesty have signify her pleasure 
 that Mr. Mildmay should wait upon she to - morrow at eleven o'clock . 
 Mr. Mildmay , - so Mr. Daubeny understand , - would be with her Majesty 
 to - morrow at that hour . Lord de Terrier have find it to be his duty 
 to recommend her Majesty to send for Mr. Mildmay . such be the real 
 import of Mr. Daubeny 's speech . that further portion of it in which 
 he explain with blandest , most beneficent , honey - flow word that 
 his party would have do everything that the country could require 
 of any party , have the House allow it to remain on the Treasury 
 bench for a month or two , - and explain also that his party would 
 never recriminate , would never return evil for evil , would in no wise 
 copy the factious opposition of their adversary ; that his party 
 would now , as it ever have do , carry itself with the meekness of 
 the dove , and the wisdom of the serpent , - all this , I say , be so 
 generally feel by gentleman on both side of the House to be " leather 
 and prunella " that very little attention be pay to it . the great 
 point be that Lord de Terrier have resign , and that Mr. Mildmay have 
 be summon to Windsor . 
 the Queen have send for Mr. Mildmay in compliance with advice give 
 to she by Lord de Terrier . and yet Lord de Terrier and his first 
 lieutenant have use all the most practised effort of their eloquence 
 for the last three day in endeavour to make their countryman 
 believe that no more unfit Minister than Mr. Mildmay ever 
 attempt to hold the rein of office ! nothing have be too bad 
 for they to say of Mr. Mildmay , - and yet , in the very first moment 
 in which they find themselves unable to carry on the Government 
 themselves , they advise the Queen to send for that most incompetent 
 and baneful statesman ! we who be conversant with our own method of 
 politic , see nothing odd in this , because we be use to it ; but 
 surely in the eye of stranger our practice must be very singular . 
 there be nothing like it in any other country , - nothing as yet . 
 nowhere else be there the same good - humour , affectionate , 
 prize - fight ferocity in politic . the leader of our two great 
 party be to each other exactly as be the two champion of the 
 ring who knock each other about for the belt and for five hundred 
 pound a side once in every two year . how they fly at each other , 
 strike as though each blow should carry death if it be but 
 possible ! and yet there be no one whom the Birmingham Bantam 
 respect so highly as he do Bill Burns the Brighton Bully , or with 
 whom he have so much delight in discuss the merit of a pot of 
 half - and - half . and so it be with Mr. Daubeny and Mr. Mildmay . in 
 private life Mr. Daubeny almost adulate his eld rival , - and Mr. 
 Mildmay never omit an opportunity of take Mr. Daubeny warmly by 
 the hand . it be not so in the United States . there the same political 
 enmity exist , but the political enmity produce private hatred . the 
 leader of party there really mean what they say when they abuse 
 each other , and be in earnest when they talk as though they be 
 about to tear each other limb from limb . I doubt whether Mr. Daubeny 
 would have injure a hair of Mr. Mildmay 's venerable head , even for 
 an assurance of six continue month in office . 
 when Mr. Daubeny have complete his statement , Mr. Mildmay simply tell 
 the House that he have receive and would obey her Majesty 's command . 
 the House would of course understand that he by no mean mean to 
 aver that the Queen would even commission he to form a Ministry . but 
 if he take no such command from her Majesty it would become his duty 
 to recommend her Majesty to impose the task upon some other person . 
 then everything be say that have to be say , and member return to 
 their club . a certain damp be throw over the joy of some excitable 
 Liberals by tiding which reach the House during Mr. Daubeny 's 
 speech . Sir Everard Powell be no more dead than be Mr. Daubeny 
 himself . now it be very unpleasant to find that your news be untrue , 
 when you have be at great pain to disseminate it . " oh , but he be 
 dead , " say Mr. Ratler . " Lady Powell assure I half an hour ago , " 
 say Mr. Ratler 's opponent , " that he be at that moment a great deal 
 well than he have be for the last three month . the journey down 
 to the House do he a world of good . " " then we will have he down for 
 every division , " say Mr. Ratler . 
 the political portion of London be in a ferment for the next five 
 day . on the Sunday morning it be know that Mr. Mildmay have 
 decline to put himself at the head of a liberal government . he and

 conference so often , and so long , that it may almost be say they 
 live together in conference . then Mr. Gresham have be with Mr. 
 Mildmay , - and Mr. Monk also . at the club it be say by many that 
 Mr. Monk have be with Mr. Mildmay ; but it be also say very 
 vehemently by other that no such interview have take place . Mr. Monk 
 be a Radical , much admire by the people , sit in Parliament for 
 that most radical of all constituency , the Pottery Hamlets , who 
 have never as yet be in power . it be the great question of the day 
 whether Mr. Mildmay would or would not ask Mr. Monk to join he ; and 
 it be say by those who habitually think at every period of change 
 that the time have now come in which the difficulty to form a 
 government will at last be find to be insuperable , that Mr. Mildmay 
 could not succeed either with Mr. Monk or without he . there be at 
 the present moment two section of these gentleman , - the section 
 which declare that Mr. Mildmay have send for Mr. Monk , and the 
 section which declare that he have not . but there be other , who 
 perhaps know well what they be say , by whom it be assert

 and Mr. Palliser be indispensable to Mr. Mildmay . and a liberal 
 Government , with Mr. Gresham in the opposition , could not live half 
 through a session ! all Sunday and Monday these thing be discuss ; 
 and on the Monday Lord de Terrier absolutely state to the Upper 
 House that he have receive her Majesty 's command to form another 
 government . Mr. Daubeny , in half a dozen most modest word , - in word 
 hardly audible , and most unlike himself , - make his statement in the 
 Lower House to the same effect . then Mr. Ratler , and Mr. Bonteen , and 
 Mr. Barrington Erle , and Mr. Laurence Fitzgibbon arouse themselves 
 and swear that such thing could not be . should the prey which they 
 have win for themselves , the spoil of their bow and arrow , be 
 snatch from out of their very mouth by treachery ? Lord de Terrier

 they do so in combination with Mr. Gresham . such a combination , say 
 Mr. Barrington Erle , would be disgraceful to both party , but would

 " that if anybody could break up the party it would be Mr. Gresham . " 
 on that Tuesday morning Mr. Gresham certainly be with Lord de 
 Terrier , but nothing come of it . Mr. Gresham be either not enough 
 like Satan for the occasion , or else he be too closely like he . 
 Lord de Terrier do not bid high enough , or else Mr. Gresham do not 
 like bidding from that quarter . nothing then come from this attempt , 
 and on the Tuesday afternoon the Queen again send for Mr. Mildmay . on 
 the Wednesday morning the gentleman who think that the insuperable 
 difficulty have at length arrive , begin to wear their long 
 face , and to be triumphant with melancholy foreboding . now at 
 last there be a dead lock . nobody could form a government . it 
 be assert that Mr. Mildmay have fall at her Majesty 's foot 
 dissolve in tear , and have implore to be relieve from further 
 responsibility . it be well known to many at the club that the Queen 
 have on that morning telegraph to Germany for advice . there be man 
 so gloomy as to declare that the Queen must throw herself into the 
 arm of Mr. Monk , unless Mr. Mildmay would consent to rise from his 
 knee and once more buckle on his ancient armour . " even that would 
 be well than Gresham , " say Barrington Erle , in his anger . " I will 
 tell you what it be , " say Ratler , " we shall have Gresham and Monk 
 together , and you and I shall have to do their bidding . " Mr. 
 Barrington Erle 's reply to that suggestion I may not dare to insert 
 in these page . 
 on the Wednesday night , however , it be know that everything have 
 be arrange , and before the Houses meet on the Thursday every place 
 have be bestow , either in reality or in imagination . the Times , 
 in its second edition on the Thursday , give a list of the Cabinet , in 
 which four place out of fourteen be rightly fill . on the Friday 
 it name ten place aright , and indicate the law officer , with only 
 one mistake in reference to Ireland ; and on the Saturday it give 
 a list of the under Secretaries of State , and Secretaries and 
 Vice - president generally , with wonderful correctness as to the 
 individual , though the office be a little jumbled . the government 
 be at last form in a manner which everybody have see to be the 
 only possible way in which a government could be form . nobody be 
 surprised , and the week 's work be regard as though the regular 
 routine of government making have simply be follow . Mr. Mildmay 
 be Prime Minister ; Mr. Gresham be at the Foreign Office ; Mr. Monk 
 be at the Board of Trade ; the Duke be President of the Council ; the 
 Earl of Brentford be Privy Seal ; and Mr. Palliser be Chancellor of 
 the Exchequer . Barrington Erle make a step up in the world , and go 
 to the Admiralty as Secretary ; Mr. Bonteen be send again to the 
 Admiralty ; and Laurence Fitzgibbon become a junior Lord of the 
 Treasury . Mr. Ratler be , of course , instal as Patronage Secretary 
 to the same Board . Mr. Ratler be perhaps the only man in the party 
 as to whose destination there could not possibly be a doubt . Mr. 
 Ratler have really qualify himself for a position in such a way as 
 to make all man feel that he would , as a matter of course , be call 
 upon to fill it . I do not know whether as much could be say on 
 behalf of any other man in the new Government . 
 during all this excitement , and through all these movement , Phineas 
 Finn feel himself to be leave more and more out in the cold . he have 
 not be such a fool as to suppose that any office would be offer 
 to he . he have never hint at such a thing to his one dearly

 be sit for above a fortnight . of course nothing could be do 
 for he as yet . but , nevertheless , he feel himself to be out in the 
 cold . the very man who have discuss with he the question of the 
 division , - who have discuss it with he because his vote be then as 
 good as that of any other member , - do not care to talk to he about 
 the distribution of place . he , at any rate , could not be one of 
 they . he , at any rate , could not be a rival . he could neither mar 
 nor assist . he could not be either a successful or a disappointed 
 sympathiser , - because he could not himself be a candidate . the affair 
 which perhaps disgust he more than anything else be the offer of 
 an office , - not in the Cabinet , indeed , but one suppose to confer 
 high dignity , - to Mr. Kennedy . Mr. Kennedy refuse the offer , and 
 this somewhat lessen Finn 's disgust , but the offer itself make he 
 unhappy . 
 " I suppose it be make simply because of his money , " he say to 
 Fitzgibbon . 
 " I do not   believe that , " say Fitzgibbon . " People seem to think that 
 he have get a head on his shoulder , though he have get no tongue in 
 it . I wonder at his refuse it because of the Right Honourable . " 
 " I be so glad that Mr. Kennedy refuse , " say Lady Laura to he . 
 " and why ? he would have be the Right Hon . Robert Kennedy for ever 
 and ever . " phinea when he say this do not as yet know exactly 
 how it would have come to pass that such honour , - the honour of the 
 enduring prefix to his name , - would have come in the way of Mr. 
 Kennedy have Mr. Kennedy accept the office in question ; but he be 
 very quick to learn all these thing , and , in the meantime , he rarely 
 make any mistake about they . 
 " what would that have be to he , - with his wealth ? " say Lady 
 Laura . " he have a position of his own and need not care for such 
 thing . there be man who should not attempt what be call 
 independence in Parliament . by do so they simply decline to make 
 themselves useful . but there be a few whose special walk in life it 
 be to be independent , and , as it be , unmove by party . " 
 " Great Akinetoses ! you know Orion , " say Phineas . 
 " Mr. Kennedy be not an Akinetos , " say Lady Laura . 
 " he hold a very proud position , " say Phineas , ironically . 
 " a very proud position indeed , " say Lady Laura , in sober earnest . 
 the dinner at Moroni 's have be eat , and Phineas have give an 
 account of the entertainment to Lord Chiltern 's sister . there have 
 be only two other guest , and both of they have be man on the

 ever so much by tell I that you have speak to he of I before . " 
 " yes ; I do so . I wish he to know you . I want he to know some man 
 who think of something besides horse . he be very well educate , you 
 know , and would certainly have take honour if he have not quarrel 
 with the people at Christ Church . " 
 " do he take a degree ? " 
 " no ; - they send he down . it be good always to have the truth among 
 friend . of course you will hear it some day . they expel he 
 because he be drunk . " then Lady Laura burst out into tear , and 
 Phineas sit near she , and console she , and swear that if in any way

 Mr. Fitzgibbon at this time claim a promise which he say that

 CHAPTER X 
 Violet Effingham 
 it be now the middle of May , and a month have elapse since the 
 terrible difficulty about the Queen 's Government have be solve . a 
 month have elapse , and thing have shake themselves into their place 
 with more of ease and apparent fitness than man have give they credit 
 for possess . Mr. Mildmay , Mr. Gresham , and Mr. Monk be the good 
 friend in the world , swear by each other in their own house , and 
 support in the other by as gallant a phalanx of Whig peer as ever 
 be get together to fight against the instinct of their own order 
 in compliance with the instinct of those below they . Lady Laura 's 
 father be in the Cabinet , to Lady Laura 's infinite delight . it 
 be her ambition to be bring as near to political action as be 
 possible for a woman without surrender any of the privilege of 
 feminine inaction . that woman should even wish to have vote at 
 parliamentary election be to her abominable , and the cause of the 
 Rights of Women generally be odious to she ; but , nevertheless , for 
 herself , she delight in hop that she too might be useful , - in 
 think that she too be perhaps , in some degree , politically 
 powerful ; and she have receive considerable increase to such hope 
 when her father accept the Privy Seal . the Earl himself be not an 
 ambitious man , and , but for his daughter , would have sever himself 
 altogether from political life before this time . he be an unhappy 
 man ; - be an obstinate man , and have in his obstinacy quarrel 
 with his only son . in his unhappiness he would have keep himself 
 alone , live in the country , brood over his wretchedness , be 
 it not for his daughter . on her behalf , and in obedience to her 
 requirement , he come yearly up to London , and , perhaps in compliance 
 with her persuasion , have take some part in the debate of the House 
 of Lords . it be easy for a peer to be a statesman , if the trouble of 
 the life be not too much for he . Lord Brentford be now a statesman , 
 if a seat in the Cabinet be proof of statesmanship . 
 at this time , in May , there be stay with Lady Laura in Portman 
 Square a very dear friend of her , by name Violet Effingham . Violet 
 Effingham be an orphan , an heiress , and a beauty ; with a terrible 
 aunt , one Lady Baldock , who be suppose to be the dragon who have 
 Violet , as a captive maiden , in charge . but as Miss Effingham be of 
 age , and be mistress of her own fortune , Lady Baldock be , in truth , 
 not omnipotent as a dragon should be . the dragon , at any rate , be 
 not now stay in Portman Square , and the captivity of the maiden 
 be therefore not severe at the present moment . Violet Effingham be 
 very pretty , but could hardly be say to be beautiful . she be small , 
 with light crispy hair , which seem to be ever on the flutter round 
 her brow , and which yet be never a hair astray . she have sweet , soft 
 grey eye , which never look at you long , hardly for a moment , - but 
 which yet , in that half moment , nearly kill you by the power of 
 their sweetness . her cheek be the soft thing in nature , and the 
 colour of it , when its colour be fix enough to be tell , be a

 it by its name . her mouth be perfect , not small enough to give that 
 expression of silliness which be so common , but almost divine , with 
 the temptation of its full , rich , ruby lip . her tooth , which she but 
 seldom show , be very even and very white , and there rest on she 
 chin the dearest dimple that ever act as a loadstar to men 's eye . 
 the fault of her face , if it have a fault , be in her nose , - which 
 be a little too sharp , and perhaps too small . a woman who want to 
 depreciate Violet Effingham have once call she a pug - nosed puppet ; 
 but I , as her chronicler , deny that she be pug - nose , - and all the 
 world who know she soon come to understand that she be no puppet . in 
 figure she be small , but not so small as she look to be . her foot

 whole person , an apparent compressibility , which seem to indicate 
 that she might go into very small compass . into what compass and 
 how compress , there be very many man who hold very different 
 opinion . Violet Effingham be certainly no puppet . she be great 
 at dancing , - as perhaps might be a puppet , - but she be great also 
 at archery , great at skating , - and great , too , at hunting . with 
 reference to that last accomplishment , she and Lady Baldock have have 
 more than one terrible tussle , not always with advantage to the 
 dragon . " my dear aunt , " she have say once during the last winter , 
 " I be go to the meet with George , " - George be her cousin , Lord 
 Baldock , and be the dragon 's son , - " and there , let there be an end 
 of it . " " and you will promise I that you will not go far , " say 
 the dragon . " I will promise nothing to - day to any man or to any 
 woman , " say Violet . what be to be say to a young lady who speak in 
 this way , and who have become of age only a fortnight since ? she ride 
 that day the famous run from Bagnall 's Gorse to Foulsham Common , and 
 be in at the death . 
 Violet Effingham be now sit in conference with her friend Lady 
 Laura , and they be discuss matter of high import , - of very high

 " I know , - or fancy that I know , - that so many man love I ! but , after

 himself if you like he . "

 " Laura , my dear - " 
 " well . " 
 " you will not   be angry if I speak out ? "

 Lord Chiltern - "

 Violet . then the door be open , and the man of whom they be

 CHAPTER XI 
 Lord Chiltern 
 the reader have be tell that Lord Chiltern be a red man , and that 
 peculiarity of his personal appearance be certainly the first to 
 strike a stranger . it impart a certain look of ferocity to he , 
 which be apt to make man afraid of he at first sight . woman be not 
 actuate in the same way , and be accustomed to look deep into man 
 at the first sight than other man will trouble themselves to do . his 
 beard be red , and be clip , so as to have none of the softness of 
 wave hair . the hair on his head also be keep short , and be very 
 red , - and the colour of his face be red . nevertheless he be a 
 handsome man , with well - cut feature , not tall , but very strongly 
 build , and with a certain curl in the corner of his eyelid which 
 give to he a look of resolution , - which perhaps he do not possess . 
 he be know to be a clever man , and when very young have have 
 the reputation of be a scholar . when he be three - and - twenty 
 grey - haired votary of the turf declare that he would make his 
 fortune on the race - course , - so clear - head be he as to odd , so 
 excellent a judge of a horse 's performance , and so gifted with a 
 memory of event . when he be five - and - twenty he have lose every 
 shilling of a fortune of his own , have squeeze from his father more 
 than his father ever choose to name in speak of his affair to 
 any one , and be know to be in debt . but he have sacrifice himself 
 on one or two memorable occasion in conformity with turf law of 
 honour , and man say of he , either that he be very honest or very 
 chivalric , - in accordance with the special view on the subject of 
 the man who be speak . it be report now that he no long own 
 horse on the turf ; - but this be doubt by some who could name 
 the animal which they say that he own , and which he run in the 
 name of Mr. Macnab , - say some ; of Mr. Pardoe , - say other ; of Mr. 
 Chickerwick , - say a third set of informant . the fact be that Lord 
 Chiltern at this moment have no interest of his own in any horse upon 
 the turf . 
 but all the world know that he drink . he have take by the throat 
 a proctor 's bull - dog when he have be drunk at Oxford , have nearly 
 strangle the man , and have be expel . he have fall through his 
 violence into some terrible misfortune at Paris , have be bring 
 before a public judge , and his name and his infamy have be make 
 notorious in every newspaper in the two capital . after that he have 
 fight a ruffian at Newmarket , and have really kill he with his 
 fist . in reference to this latter affray it have be prove that the 
 attack have be make on he , that he have not be to blame , and that 
 he have not be drunk . after a prolonged investigation he have come 
 forth from that affair without disgrace . he would have do so , at 
 least , if he have not be heretofore disgrace . but we all know how 
 the man well speak of may steal a horse , while he who be of evil 
 repute may not look over a hedge . it be assert widely by many who 
 be suppose to know all about everything that Lord Chiltern be in 
 a fit of delirium tremen when he kill the ruffian at Newmarket . 
 the bad of that latter affair be that it produce the total 
 estrangement which now exist between Lord Brentford and his son . 
 Lord Brentford would not believe that his son be in that matter 
 more sin against than sin . " such thing do not happen to 
 other man 's son , " he say , when Lady Laura plead for her brother . 
 Lady Laura could not induce her father to see his son , but so far 
 prevail that no sentence of banishment be pronounce against 
 Lord Chiltern . there be nothing to prevent the son sit at 
 his father 's table if he so please . he never do so please , - but 
 nevertheless he continue to live in the house in Portman Square ; 
 and when he meet the Earl , in the hall , perhaps , or on the staircase , 
 would simply bow to he . then the Earl would bow again , and shuffle 
 on , - and look very wretched , as no doubt he be . a grown - up son must 
 be the great comfort a man can have , - if he be his father 's good 
 friend ; but otherwise he can hardly be a comfort . as it be in this 
 house , the son be a constant thorn in his father 's side . 
 " what do he do when we leave London ? " Lord Brentford once say to 
 his daughter . 
 " he stay here , papa . " 
 " but he hunt still ? " 
 " yes , he hunt , - and he have a room somewhere at an inn , - down in 
 Northamptonshire . but he be mostly in London . they have train on 
 purpose . " 
 " what a life for my son ! " say the Earl . " what a life ! of course no 
 decent person will let he into his house . " Lady Laura do not know 
 what to say to this , for in truth Lord Chiltern be not fond of 
 stay at the house of person whom the Earl would have call 
 decent . 
 General Effingham , the father of Violet , and Lord Brentford have be 
 the close and dearest of friend . they have be young man in the 
 same regiment , and through life each have confide in the other . when 
 the General 's only son , then a youth of seventeen , be kill in 
 one of our grand New Zealand war , the bereaved father and the Earl 
 have be together for a month in their sorrow . at that time Lord 
 Chiltern 's career have still be open to hope , - and the one man have 
 contrast his lot with the other . General Effingham live long 
 enough to hear the Earl declare that his lot be the happy of the 
 two . now the General be dead , and Violet , the daughter of a second 
 wife , be all that be leave of the Effinghams . this second wife have 
 be a Miss Plummer , a lady from the city with much money , whose 
 sister have marry Lord Baldock . Violet in this way have fall to the 
 care of the Baldock people , and not into the hand of her father 's 
 friend . but , as the reader will have surmise , she have idea of her 
 own of emancipate herself from Baldock thraldom . 
 twice before that last terrible affair at Newmarket , before the 
 quarrel between the father and the son have be complete , Lord

 son in connection with Miss Effingham . 
 " if he think of it I shall be glad to see he on the subject . you

 " or he might come and take you and I to the Zoo on Sunday . that be

 will have nothing to do with such a scheme . " 
 " then there be nothing for it but that you should come here ; and as

 before I go to my aunt 's in Berkeley Square . "

 " and why not , Oswald ? " ask his sister . 
 he pass his hand over his face before he answer she . " because she 
 and I run in different groove now , and be not such meet playfellow 
 as we use to be once . do you remember my take you away right 
 through Saulsby Wood once on the old pony , and not bring you back

 thing now . and Miss Blink the mild be change for Lady Baldock the

 change as well as my name . "

 " why be you so sudden with she , - so abrupt , - so loud ? " say his 
 sister , come up to he and take he by the arm almost in anger .

 have do much to make any girl afraid of you . " 
 " I do remember it . " 
 " do something now to make she fear you no long . speak to she

 believe you when she would believe no one else on that matter . "

 CHAPTER XII 
 Autumnal Prospects 
 the session go on very calmly after the opening battle which oust

 phinea during the summer have very often meet Mr. Kennedy . they sit

 Saulsby Wood

 CHAPTER XIV 
 Loughlinter 
 Phineas Finn reach Loughlinter together with Mr. Ratler in a 
 post - chaise from the neighbouring town . Mr. Ratler , who have do this 
 kind of thing very often before , travel without impediment , but 
 the new servant of our hero 's be stick outside with the driver , and 
 be in the way . " I never bring a man with I , " say Mr. Ratler to his 
 young friend . " the servant of the house like it much well , because 
 they get fee'd ; you be just as well wait on , and it do not   cost 
 half as much . " Phineas blush as he hear all this ; but there be 
 the impediment , not to be get rid of for the nonce , and Phineas make 
 the good of his attendant . " it be one of those point , " say he , " as 
 to which a man never quite make up his mind . if you bring a fellow , 
 you wish you have not   bring he ; and if you do not , you wish you have . " 
 " I be a great deal more decide in my way that that , " say Mr. 
 Ratler . 
 Loughlinter , as they approach it , seem to Phineas to be a much 
 fine place than Saulsby . and so it be , except that Loughlinter 
 want that graceful beauty of age which Saulsby possess . 
 Loughlinter be all of cut stone , but the stone have be cut only 
 yesterday . it stand on a gentle slope , with a greensward fall from 
 the front entrance down to a mountain lake . and on the other side of 
 the Lough there rise a mighty mountain to the sky , Ben Linter . at 
 the foot of it , and all round to the left , there run the wood of 
 Linter , stretch for mile through crag and bog and mountain 
 land . no well ground for deer than the side of Ben Linter be 
 there in all those highland . and the Linter , rush down into the 
 Lough through rock which , in some place , almost meet together above 
 its water , run so near to the house that the pleasant noise of its 
 cataract could be hear from the hall door . behind the house the 
 expanse of drain park land seem to be interminable ; and then , 
 again , come the mountain . there be Ben Linn and Ben Lody ; - and 
 the whole territory belong to Mr. Kennedy . he be laird of Linn 
 and laird of Linter , as his people use to say . and yet his father 
 have walk into Glasgow as a little boy , - no doubt with the normal 
 half - crown in his breech pocket . 
 " Magnificent ; - be it not ? " say Phineas to the Treasury Secretary , 
 as they be be drive up to the door . 
 " very grand ; - but the young tree show the new man . a new man may buy 
 a forest ; but he can not   get park tree . " 
 Phineas , at the moment , be think how far all these thing which 
 he see , the mountain stretch everywhere around he , the castle , 
 the lake , the river , the wealth of it all , and , more than the wealth , 
 the nobility of the beauty , might act as temptation to Lady Laura 
 Standish . if a woman be ask to have the half of all this , would 
 it be possible that she should prefer to take the half of his 
 nothing ? he think it might be possible for a girl who would 
 confess , or seem to confess , that love should be everything . but it 
 could hardly be possible for a woman who look at the world almost 
 as a man look at it , - as an oyster to be open with such weapon 
 as she could find ready to her hand . Lady Laura profess to have a 
 care for all the affair of the world . she love politic , and could 
 talk of social science , and have broad idea about religion , and be 
 devoted to certain educational view . such a woman would feel that

 CHAPTER XV 
 Donald Bean 's Pony

 DEAR FINN , 
 be you go to Paris with I ? 
 your , C.

 on this day there be to be a grouse - shoot party , and the shooter

 " I suppose such bet be very common . " 
 " I do not   think man ought to propose they unless they be quite

 " no ; I be go to make my way to Loughlinter . " 
 " not alone ? " 
 " yes , alone . " 
 " it be over nine mile . you can not   walk it . " 
 Phineas look at his watch , and find that it be now two o'clock .

 " I can walk it , Mr. Kennedy . " 
 " yes ; and think of the state in which you would reach Loughlinter ! come

 " but I can not   take you off the mountain , " say Phineas . 
 " then you must allow I to take you off . " 
 so Mr. Kennedy lead the way down to Donald Bean 's cottage , and before

 her look so young , so pretty , and so fit to receive a lover 's vow .

 Standish , the daughter of an Earl , the descendant of a line of 
 Earls , - and that he be the son of a simple country doctor in

 " I do not expect you in the least , " say Lady Laura . 
 " and yet I speak very positively . " 
 " but there be thing as to which a man may be very positive , and yet 
 may be allow to fail . in the first place , how on earth do you get

 " Mr. Kennedy get I a pony , - Donald Bean 's pony . " 
 " you tell he , then ? " 
 " yes ; I tell he why I be come , and that I must be here . then he 
 take the trouble to come all the way off the mountain to persuade

 conquer I at last . " 
 " I be so glad of that , " say Lady Laura . " I know he would , - unless it

 they go up the path by the brook , from bridge to bridge , till they

 what a fool he have be to let she know his secret when her knowledge

 presently he hear a voice behind he , - call he gently . then he

 " well ; - yes : what be it ? " and turn round he make an attempt to

 " oh , Lady Laura ! "

 " how can I answer you , Lady Laura ? sufficient ! and you be not angry

 of this shall ever be repeat , - even among ourselves . be that a

 " oh , yes . I shall never speak of it again . " 
 " and now you will wish I joy ? " 
 " I have wish you joy , Lady Laura . and I will do so again . may you 
 have every blessing which the world can give you . you can not expect

 when be the marriage to be ? " 
 " nothing have be say of that . I shall be guide by he , - but there 
 must , of course , be delay . there will be settlement and I know not

 Phineas have now seat himself on the exact stone on which he have

 " shall we go down now ? " she say . 
 " oh , yes . " 
 " you will not go on by the lake ? " 
 " what be the use ? it be all the same now . you will want to be back to

 CHAPTER XVI 
 Phineas Finn return to Killaloe 
 Phineas Finn 's first session of Parliament be over , - his first 
 session with all its adventure . when he get back to Mrs. Bunce 's

 fruition of his privilege . but still there be the kiss , - an

 a course of reading . Lord Brentford have become intimate with he . he 
 be on pleasant term with Mr. Palliser and Mr. Gresham . and as for

 as to his bosom friendship with Mr. Kennedy , that of course trouble

 he go over to Ireland , stay but one night with Mrs. Bunce , and

 Mary Flood Jones , - the reader I hope will not quite have forget

 about Christmas time there come a discussion between Phineas and his 
 father about money . " I hope you find you get on pretty well , " say

 " not immediately . it be almost impossible to combine the two study

 " by no mean , sir . and all that I be do now will , I trust , be of

 " and be turn out with the Ministry ! " 
 " yes ; that be true , sir . I must run my chance . if the bad come to

 " a hundred pound . " 
 " if it be inconvenient , sir , I can do without it . " he have not as 
 yet pay for his gun , or for that velvet coat in which he have be

 man in office , and the chance of promotion for himself ; to the glare

 resolve at any rate upon this , - that a month of the session should

 would eschew all action and gesticulation ; Mr. Monk have be very

 especially careful that no word should escape he which have not in

 eloquence . they should twit he no more on his next return . he would 
 speak and would carry the House with he if a human effort might

 so he pack up his thing , and start again for London in the

 position . but whether it be for good or evil , I will be true to he . " 
 " I be afraid you be unhappy , " Babara Finn say to she on the next 
 morning . 
 " no ; I be not unhappy , - not at all . I have a deal to make I happy 
 and proud . I do not   mean to be a bit unhappy . " then she turn away 
 and cry heartily , and Barbara Finn cry with she for company . 
 CHAPTER XVII 
 Phineas Finn return to London 
 Phineas have receive two letter during his recess at Killaloe from 
 two woman who admire he much , which , as they be both short , shall 
 be submit to the reader . the first be as follow : - 
 Saulsby , October 20 , 186 - . 
 MY DEAR MR . FINN , 
 I write a line to tell you that our marriage be to be 
 hurry on as quickly as possible . Mr. Kennedy do not 
 like to be absent from Parliament ; nor will he be content 
 to postpone the ceremony till the session be over . the day 
 fix be the 3rd of December , and we then go at once to 
 Rome , and intend to be back in London by the opening of 
 Parliament . 
 your most sincerely , 
 LAURA STANDISH . 
 our London address will be No . 52 , Grosvenor Place . 
 to this he write an answer as short , express his ardent wish 
 that those winter hymeneal might produce nothing but happiness , and 
 say that he would not be in town many day before he knock at 
 the door of no . 52 , Grosvenor Place . 
 and the second letter be as follow : - 
 Great Marlborough Street , December , 186 - . 
 DEAR and HONOURED SIR , 
 bunce be get ever so anxious about the room , and 
 say as how he have a young Equity draftsman and wife and 
 baby as would take the whole house , and all because Miss 
 Pouncefoot say a word about her port wine , which any lady

 I day after day . I do not   want nobody 's wife and baby to 
 have to do for , and would soon have a Parliament gent like 
 yourself than any one else . 
 your umbly and respectful , 
 JANE BUNCE . 
 to this he reply that he would certainly come back to the room 
 in Great Marlborough Street , should he be lucky enough to find they 
 vacant , and he express his willingness to take they on and from 
 the 1st of February . and on the 3rd of February he find himself in

 coldly , and tell his wife the same evening that as far as he could

 the ballot . " if he mean well , why do he go and stay with they lord 
 down in Scotland ? I know all about it . I know a man when I see 
 he . Mr. Low , who be look out to be a Tory judge some of these 
 day , be a deal well ; - because he know what he be after . " 
 immediately on his return to town , Phineas find himself summon to 
 a political meeting at Mr. Mildmay 's house in St. James 's Square . 
 " we be go to begin in earnest this time , " Barrington Erle say to 
 he at the club . 
 " I be glad of that , " say Phineas . 
 " I suppose you hear all about it down at Loughlinter ? " 
 now , in truth , Phineas have hear very little of any settle plan down 
 at Loughlinter . he have play a game of chess with Mr. Gresham , and 
 have shoot a stag with Mr. Palliser , and have discuss sheep with Lord 
 Brentford , but have hardly hear a word about politic from any one 
 of those influential gentleman . from Mr. Monk he have hear much of a 
 come reform Bill ; but his communication with Mr. Monk have rather 
 be private discussion , - in which he have learn Mr. Monk 's own 
 view on certain point , - than revelation on the intention of the 
 party to which Mr. Monk belong . " I hear of nothing settle , " say 
 Phineas ; " but I suppose we be to have a Reform Bill . " 
 " that be a matter of course . " 
 " and I suppose we be not to touch the question of ballot . " 
 " that be the difficulty , " say Barrington Erle . " but of course we 
 shall not   touch it as long as Mr. Mildmay be in the Cabinet . he will 
 never consent to the ballot as First Minister of the Crown . " 
 " nor would Gresham , or Palliser , " say Phineas , who do not choose to 
 bring forward his great gun at first . 
 " I do not   know about Gresham . it be impossible to say what Gresham 
 might bring himself to do . Gresham be a man who may go any length 
 before he have do . Planty Pall , " - for such be the name by which Mr. 
 Plantagenet Palliser be ordinarily know among his friend , - " would 
 of course go with Mr. Mildmay and the Duke . " 
 " and Monk be oppose to the ballot , " say Phineas . 
 " ah , that be the question . no doubt he have assent to the proposition 
 of a measure without the ballot ; but if there should come a row , and 
 man like Turnbull demand it , and the London mob kick up a shindy , I 
 do not   know how far Monk would be steady . " 
 " whatever he say , he will stick to . " 
 " he be your leader , then ? " ask Barrington . 
 " I do not   know that I have a leader . Mr. Mildmay lead our side ; and 
 if anybody lead I , he do . but I have great faith in Mr. Monk . " 
 " there be one who would go for the ballot to - morrow , if it be 
 bring forward stoutly , " say Barrington Erle to Mr. Ratler a few 
 minute afterwards , point to Phineas as he speak . 
 " I do not   think much of that young man , " say Ratler . 
 Mr. Bonteen and Mr. Ratler have put their head together during that 
 last evening at Loughlinter , and have agree that they do not think 
 much of Phineas Finn . why do Mr. Kennedy go down off the mountain 
 to get he a pony ? and why do Mr. Gresham play chess with he ? Mr. 
 Ratler and Mr. Bonteen may have be right in make up their mind 
 to think but little of Phineas Finn , but Barrington Erle have be 
 quite wrong when he have say that Phineas would " go for the ballot " 
 to - morrow . Phineas have make up his mind very strongly that he would 
 always oppose the ballot . that he would hold the same opinion 
 throughout his life , no one should pretend to say ; but in his present 
 mood , and under the tuition which he have receive from Mr. Monk , 
 he be prepared to demonstrate , out of the House and in it , that 
 the ballot be , as a political measure , unmanly , ineffective , and 
 enervate . Enervating have be a great word with Mr. Monk , and 
 Phineas have clung to it with admiration . 
 the meeting take place at Mr. Mildmay 's on the third day of the 
 session . Phineas have of course hear of such meeting before , but have 
 never attend one . indeed , there have be no such gathering when 
 Mr. Mildmay 's party come into power early in the last session . Mr. 
 Mildmay and his man have then make their effort in turn out their 
 opponent , and have be well pleased to rest awhile upon their oar . 
 now , however , they must go again to work , and therefore the liberal 
 party be collect at Mr. Mildmay 's house , in order that the liberal 
 party might be tell what it be that Mr. Mildmay and his Cabinet 
 intend to do . 
 Phineas Finn be quite in the dark as to what would be the nature 
 of the performance on this occasion , and entertain some idea that 
 every gentleman present would be call upon to express individually 
 his assent or dissent in regard to the measure propose . he walk to 
 St. James 's Square with Laurence Fitzgibbon ; but even with Fitzgibbon 
 be ashamed to show his ignorance by ask question . " after all , " 
 say Fitzgibbon , " this kind of thing mean nothing . I know as well as 
 possible , and so do you , what Mr. Mildmay will say , - and then Gresham 
 will say a few word ; and then Turnbull will make a murmur , and then 
 we shall all assent , - to anything or to nothing ; - and then it will be 
 over . " still Phineas do not understand whether the assent require 
 would or would not be an individual personal assent . when the affair

 have be very nearly correct in his description of what would occur . 
 Mr. Mildmay make a long speech . Mr. Turnbull , the great Radical of

 make another speech , and then all be over . the gist of the whole 
 thing be , that there should be a Reform Bill , - very generous in its

 his doubt whether this would be satisfactory to the country ; but even 
 Mr. Turnbull be soft in his tone and complaisant in his manner . as

 adopt , - there could be no need for energy or violence . they go to 
 Mr. Mildmay 's house to hear Mr. Mildmay 's plan , - and they hear it . 
 two day after this Phineas be to dine with Mr. Monk . Mr. Monk have 
 ask he in the lobby of the House . " I do not   give dinner party , " 
 he say , " but I should like you to come and meet Mr. Turnbull . " 
 Phineas accept the invitation as a matter of course . there be 
 many who say that Mr. Turnbull be the great man in the nation , 
 and that the nation could be save only by a direct obedience to 
 Mr. Turnbull 's instruction . other say that Mr. Turnbull be a 
 demagogue and at heart a rebel ; that he be un - english , false and 
 very dangerous . Phineas be rather inclined to believe the latter 
 statement ; and as danger and dangerous man be always more attractive 
 than safety and safe man , he be glad to have an opportunity of 
 meet Mr. Turnbull at dinner . 
 in the meantime he go to call on Lady Laura , whom he have not 
 see since the last evening which he spend in her company at

 close beneath the fall of the Linter . he find she at home , and with 
 she be her husband . " here be a Darby and Joan meeting , be it not ? " 
 she say , get up to welcome he . he have see Mr. Kennedy before , 
 and have be stand close to he during the meeting at Mr. 
 Mildmay 's . 
 " I be very glad to find you both together . " 
 " but Robert be go away this instant , " say Lady Laura . " have he 
 tell you of our adventure at Rome ? " 
 " not a word . " 
 " then I must tell you ; - but not now . the dear old Pope be so civil 
 to we . I come to think it quite a pity that he should be in trouble . "

 at dinner , I believe . " 
 " do you dine at Mr. Monk 's ? " 
 " yes , and be ask expressly to hear Turnbull make a convert of you . 
 there be only to be we four . Au revoir . " then Mr. Kennedy go , and

 wait for what he might say . 
 at last she find that it be incumbent on she to begin . " be you

 " I have never imagine that he would have be impetuous . and he seem

 " I do not wonder that he should be in a hurry , but that you should 
 submit . " 
 " I tell you that I should do just what the wise people tell I . I 
 ask papa , and he say that it would be well . so the lawyer be 
 drive out of their mind , and the milliner out of their body , and 
 the thing be do . " 
 " who be there at the marriage ? " 
 " Oswald be not there . that I know be what you mean to ask . Papa say

 fall from his mouth . " 
 " what do he say ? " 
 " I will not repeat it , - not altogether . but he say that Oswald be 
 not entitle to a son 's treatment . he be very sore about my money ,
