 between they be as wide as ever . " 
 " and where be chiltern now ? " say Phineas . 
 " down in Northamptonshire , stay at some inn from whence he hunt . 
 he tell I that he be quite alone , - that he never din out , never 
 have any one to dine with he , that he hunt five or six day a 
 week , - and read at night . " 
 " that be not a bad sort of life . " 
 " not if the reading be any good . but I can not bear that he should be 
 so solitary . and if he break down in it , then his companion will 
 not be fit for he . do you ever hunt ? " 
 " oh yes , - at home in county Clare . all Irishmen hunt . " 
 " I wish you would go down to he and see he . he would be delighted 
 to have you . " 
 Phineas think over the proposition before he answer it , and then

 Laura , - but that I have no money for hunt in England . " 
 " alas , alas ! " say she , smile . " how that hit one on every side ! " 
 " I might manage it , - for a couple of day , - in March . " 
 " do not do what you think you ought not to do , " say Lady Laura . 
 " no ; certainly . but I should like it , and if I can I will . " 
 " he could mount you , I have no doubt . he have no other expense now , 
 and keep a stable full of horse . I think he have seven or eight . and 
 now tell I , Mr. Finn ; when be you go to charm the House ? or be

 he blush , - he know that he blush as he answer . " oh , I suppose I 
 shall make some sort of attempt before long . I can not   bear the idea of 
 be a bore . " 
 " I think you ought to speak , Mr. Finn . " 
 " I do not know about that , but I certainly mean to try . there will be 
 lot of opportunity about the new Reform Bill . of course you know 
 that Mr. Mildmay be go to bring it in at once . you hear all that 
 from Mr. Kennedy . " 
 " and papa have tell I . I still see papa almost every day . you must 
 call upon he . mind you do . " Phineas say that he certainly would . 
 " Papa be very lonely now , and I sometimes feel that I have be 
 almost cruel in desert he . and I think that he have a horror of 
 the house , - especially later in the year , - always fancy that he 
 will meet Oswald . I be so unhappy about it all , Mr. Finn . " 
 " why do not   your brother marry ? " say Phineas , know nothing as 
 yet of Lord Chiltern and Violet Effingham . " if he be to marry well , 
 that would bring your father round . " 
 " yes , - it would . " 
 " and why should he not ? " 
 Lady Laura pause before she answer ; and then she tell the whole 
 story . " he be violently in love , and the girl he love have refuse 
 he twice . " 
 " be it with Miss Effingham ? " ask Phineas , guess the truth at 
 once , and remember what Miss Effingham have say to he when rid 
 in the wood . 
 " yes ; - with Violet Effingham ; my father 's pet , his favourite , whom he 
 love next to myself , - almost as well as myself ; whom he would really 
 welcome as a daughter . he would gladly make her mistress of his 
 house , and of Saulsby . everything would then go smoothly . " 
 " but she do not like Lord Chiltern ? " 
 " I believe she love he in her heart ; but she be afraid of he . as 
 she say herself , a girl be bind to be so careful of herself . with 
 all her seem frolic , Violet Effingham be very wise . " 
 Phineas , though not conscious of anything akin to jealousy , be 
 annoy at the revelation make to he . since he have hear that Lord 
 Chiltern be in love with Miss Effingham , he do not like Lord 
 Chiltern quite as well as he have do before . he himself have simply 
 admire Miss Effingham , and have take pleasure in her society ; but , 
 though this have be all , he do not like to hear of another man 
 want to marry she , and he be almost angry with Lady Laura for 
 say that she believe Miss Effingham love her brother . if Miss

 sufficient . it be not that Phineas be in love with Miss Effingham 
 himself . as he be still violently in love with Lady Laura , any other

 find fault with she for her decision . " 
 " I find no fault ; - but I think my brother would make she happy . " 
 Lady Laura , when she be leave alone , at once revert to the tone in 
 which Phineas Finn have answer her remark about Miss Effingham . 
 Phineas be very ill able to conceal his thought , and wear his heart 
 almost upon his sleeve . " can it be possible that he care for her 
 himself ? " that be the nature of Lady Laura 's first question to 
 herself upon the matter . and in ask herself that question , she 
 think nothing of the disparity in rank or fortune between Phineas 
 Finn and Violet Effingham . nor do it occur to she as at all 
 improbable that Violet might accept the love of he who have so lately 
 be her own lover . but the idea grate against her wish on two 
 side . she be most anxious that Violet should ultimately become her 
 brother 's wife , - and she could not be pleased that Phineas should be 
 able to love any woman . 
 I must beg my reader not to be carry away by those last word 
 into any erroneous conclusion . they must not suppose that Lady Laura 
 Kennedy , the lately married bride , indulge a guilty passion for the 
 young man who have love she . though she have probably think often 
 of Phineas Finn since her marriage , her thought have never be of 
 a nature to disturb her rest . it have never occur to she even to 
 think that she regard he with any feeling that be an offence 
 to her husband . she would have hat herself have any such idea 
 present itself to her mind . she pride herself on be a pure 
 high - principle woman , who have keep so strong a guard upon herself as 
 to be nearly free from the danger of those rock upon which other 
 woman make shipwreck of their happiness . she take pride in this , and 
 would then blame herself for her own pride . but though she so blame 
 herself , it never occur to she to think that to she there might be 
 danger of such shipwreck . she have put away from herself the idea of 
 love when she have first perceive that Phineas have regard she with 
 more than friendship , and have accept Mr. Kennedy 's offer with an 
 assure conviction that by do so she be act well for her own 
 happiness and for that of all those concern . she have feel the 
 romance of the position to be sweet when Phineas have stand with she 
 at the top of the fall of the Linter , and have tell she of the hope 
 which he have dare to indulge . and when at the bottom of the fall he 
 have presume to take she in his arm , she have forgive he without 
 difficulty to herself , tell herself that that would be the alpha 
 and the omega of the romance of her life . she have not feel herself 
 bind to tell Mr. Kennedy of what have occur , - but she have feel 
 that he could hardly have be angry even have he be tell . and she 
 have often think of her lover since , and of his love , - tell 
 herself that she too have once have a lover , never regard her 
 husband in that light ; but her thought have not frighten she as 
 guilty thought will do . there have come a romance which have be 
 pleasant , and it be go . it have be soon banish , - but it 
 have leave to she a sweet flavour , of which she love to taste the 
 sweetness though she know that it be go . and the man should be her 
 friend , but especially her husband 's friend . it should be her care to 
 see that his life be successful , - and especially her husband 's care . 
 it be a great delight to she to know that her husband like the man . 
 and the man would marry , and the man 's wife should be her friend . all 
 this have be very pure and very pleasant . now an idea have flit 
 across her brain that the man be in love with some one else , - and 
 she do not like it ! 
 but she do not therefore become afraid of herself , or in the least 
 realise at once the danger of her own position . her immediate glance 
 at the matter do not go beyond the falseness of man . if it be so , 
 as she suspect , - if Phineas have in truth transfer his affection 
 to Violet Effingham , of how little value be the love of such a man ! 
 it do not occur to she at this moment that she also have transfer 
 her to Robert Kennedy , or that , if not , she have do bad . but she 
 do remember that in the autumn this young Phoebus among man have 
 turn his back upon she out upon the mountain that he might hide 
 from she the agony of his heart when he learn that she be to be 
 the wife of another man ; and that now , before the winter be over , he 
 could not hide from she the fact that his heart be elsewhere ! and 
 then she speculate , and count up fact , and satisfy herself that 
 Phineas could not even have see Violet Effingham since they two have 
 stand together upon the mountain . how false be man ! - how false and 
 how weak of heart ! 
 " chiltern and Violet Effingham ! " say Phineas to himself , as he 
 walk away from Grosvenor Place . " be it fair that she should be 
 sacrifice because she be rich , and because she be so win and so 
 fascinating that Lord Brentford would receive even his son for the 
 sake of receive also such a daughter - in - law ? " Phineas also like

 he ; have look forward to his regeneration , hop , perhaps , that he

 this . if Miss Effingham have refuse Lord Chiltern twice , surely that 
 ought to be sufficient . it do not occur to he that the love of such

 himself , he be still in love , - hopelessly in love , with Lady Laura 
 Kennedy ! 
 CHAPTER XVIII 
 Mr. Turnbull 
 it be a Wednesday evening and there be no House ; - and at seven 
 o'clock Phineas be at Mr. Monk 's hall door . he be the first of the 
 guest , and he find Mr. Monk alone in the dining - room . " I be do 
 butler , " say Mr. Monk , who have a brace of decanter in his hand , 
 which he proceed to put down in the neighbourhood of the fire . 
 " but I have finish , and now we will go up - stair to receive the 
 two great man properly . " 
 " I beg your pardon for come too early , " say Finn . 
 " not a minute too early . seven be seven , and it be I who be too late . 
 but , Lord bless you , you do not   think I be ashamed of be find in 
 the act of decant my own wine ! I remember Lord Palmerston say 
 before some committee about salary , five or six year ago now , I 
 daresay , that it would not   do for an English Minister to have his hall 
 door open by a maid - servant . now , I be an English Minister , and 
 I have   get nobody but a maid - servant to open my hall door , and I be 
 oblige to look after my own wine . I wonder whether it be improper ? I 
 should not   like to be the mean of injure the British Constitution . " 
 " perhaps if you resign soon , and if nobody follow your example , 
 grave evil result may be avoid . " 
 " I sincerely hope so , for I do love the British Constitution ; and I 
 love also the respect in which member of the English Cabinet be 
 hold . now Turnbull , who will be here in a moment , hat it all ; but 
 he be a rich man , and have more powdered footman hang about his 
 house than ever Lord Palmerston have himself . " 
 " he be still in business . " 
 " oh yes ; - and make his thirty thousand a year . here he be . how be 
 you , Turnbull ? we be talk about my maid - servant . I hope she 
 open the door for you properly . " 
 " certainly , - as far as I perceive , " say Mr. Turnbull , who be 
 well at a speech than a joke . " a very respectable young woman I 
 should say . " 
 " there be not one more so in all London , " say Mr. Monk ; " but Finn 
 seem to think that I ought to have a man in livery . " 
 " it be a matter of perfect indifference to I , " say Mr. Turnbull . 
 " I be one of those who never think of such thing . " 
 " nor I either , " say Mr. Monk . then the laird of Loughlinter be 
 announce , and they all go down to dinner . 
 Mr. Turnbull be a good - looking robust man about sixty , with long 
 grey hair and a red complexion , with hard eye , a well - cut nose , and 
 full lip . he be nearly six foot high , stand quite upright , and 
 always wear a black swallow - tail coat , black trouser , and a black 
 silk waistcoat . in the House , at least , he be always so dressed , and 
 at dinner table . what difference there might be in his costume when 
 at home at Staleybridge few of those who see he in London have the 
 mean of know . there be nothing in his face to indicate special 
 talent . no one look at he would take he to be a fool ; but there 
 be none of the fire of genius in his eye , nor be there in the line 
 of his mouth any of that play of thought or fancy which be generally 
 to be find in the face of man and woman who have make themselves 
 great . Mr. Turnbull have certainly make himself great , and could 
 hardly have do so without force of intellect . he be one of the 
 most popular , if not the most popular politician in the country . poor 
 man believe in he , think that he be their most honest public 
 friend ; and man who be not poor believe in his power , think 
 that his counsel must surely prevail . he have obtain the ear of the 
 House and the favour of the reporter , and open his voice at no 
 public dinner , on no public platform , without a conviction that the 
 word speak by he would be read by thousand . the first necessity 
 for good speaking be a large audience ; and of this advantage Mr. 
 Turnbull have make himself sure . and yet it could hardly be say that 
 he be a great orator . he be gift with a powerful voice , with 
 strong , and I may , perhaps , call they broad conviction , with perfect 
 self - reliance , with almost unlimited power of endurance , with hot 
 ambition , with no keen scruple , and with a moral skin of great 
 thickness . nothing say against he pain he , no attack wound 
 he , no raillery touch he in the least . there be not a sore spot 
 about he , and probably his first thought on wake every morning 
 tell he that he , at least , be totus tere atque rotundus . he be , 
 of course , a thorough Radical , - and so be Mr. Monk . but Mr. Monk 's 
 first wake thought be probably exactly the reverse of those 
 of his friend . Mr. Monk be a much hotter man in debate than Mr. 
 Turnbull ; - but Mr. Monk be ever doubting of himself , and never 
 doubt of himself so much as when he have be most violent , and 
 also most effective , in debate . when Mr. Monk jeer at himself for 
 be a Cabinet Minister and keep no attendant grander than a 
 parlour - maid , there be a substratum of self - doubt under the joke . 
 Mr. Turnbull be certainly a great Radical , and as such enjoy a 
 great reputation . I do not think that high office in the State have 
 ever be offer to he ; but thing have be say which justify 
 he , or seem to himself to justify he , in declare that in 
 no possible circumstance would he serve the Crown . " I serve the 
 people , " he have say , " and much as I respect the servant of the 
 Crown , I think that my own office be the high . " he have be greatly 
 call to task for this speech ; and Mr. Mildmay , the present Premier , 
 have ask he whether he do not recognise the so - call servant of 
 the Crown as the most hard - work and true servant of the people . 
 the House and the press have support Mr. Mildmay , but to all that 
 Mr. Turnbull be quite indifferent ; and when an assertion make by he 
 before three or four thousand person at Manchester , to the effect 
 that he , - he specially , - be the friend and servant of the people , 
 be receive with acclamation , he feel quite satisfied that he have 
 gain his point . progressive reform in the franchise , of which 
 manhood suffrage should be the acknowledged and not far distant end , 
 equal electoral district , ballot , tenant right for England as well 
 as Ireland , reduction of the stand army till there should be no 
 stand army to reduce , utter disregard of all political movement 
 in Europe , an almost idolatrous admiration for all political 
 movement in America , free trade in everything except malt , and 
 an absolute extinction of a State Church , - these be among the 
 principal article in Mr. Turnbull 's political catalogue . and I 
 think that when once he have learn the art of arrange his word 
 as he stand upon his leg , and have so master his voice as to 
 have obtain the ear of the House , the work of his life be not 
 difficult . have nothing to construct , he could always deal with 
 generality . be free from responsibility , he be not call upon 
 either to study detail or to master even great fact . it be his 
 business to inveigh against existing evil , and perhaps there be 
 no easy business when once the privilege of an audience have be 
 attain . it be his work to cut down forest - tree , and he have 
 nothing to do with the subsequent cultivation of the land . Mr. 
 Monk have once tell Phineas Finn how great be the charm of that 
 inaccuracy which be permit to the Opposition . Mr. Turnbull no 
 doubt enjoy these charm to the full , though he would soon have 
 put a padlock on his mouth for a month than have own as much . upon 
 the whole , Mr. Turnbull be no doubt right in resolve that he would 
 not take office , though some reticence on that subject might have 
 be more become to he . 
 the conversation at dinner , though it be altogether on political 
 subject , have in it nothing of special interest as long as the girl 
 be there to change the plate ; but when she be go , and the door 
 be close , it gradually open out , and there come on to be a 
 pleasant sparring match between the two great Radicals , - the Radical 
 who have join himself to the govern power , and the Radical who 
 stand aloof . Mr. Kennedy barely say a word now and then , and Phineas 
 be almost as silent as Mr. Kennedy . he have come there to hear some 
 such discussion , and be quite willing to listen while gun of such 
 great calibre be be fire off for his amusement . 
 " I think Mr. Mildmay be make a great step forward , " say Mr. 
 Turnbull . 
 " I think he be , " say Mr. Monk . 
 " I do not believe that he would ever live to go so far . it will 
 hardly suffice even for this year ; but still come from he , it be 
 a great deal . it only show how far a man may be make to go , if only 
 the proper force be apply . after all , it matter very little who 
 be the Ministers . " 
 " that be what I have always declare , " say Mr. Monk . 
 " very little indeed . we do not   mind whether it be Lord de Terrier , or 
 Mr. Mildmay , or Mr. Gresham , or you yourself , if you choose to get 
 yourself make First Lord of the Treasury . " 
 " I have no such ambition , Turnbull . " 
 " I should have think you have . if I go in for that kind of thing 
 myself , I should like to go to the top of the ladder . I should feel 
 that if I could do any good at all by become a Minister , I could 
 only do it by become first Minister . " 
 " you would not   doubt your own fitness for such a position ? " 
 " I doubt my fitness for the position of any Minister , " say Mr. 
 Turnbull . 
 " you mean that on other ground , " say Mr. Kennedy . 
 " I mean it on every ground , " say Mr. Turnbull , rise on his leg 
 and stand with his back to the fire . " of course I be not fit to 
 have diplomatic intercourse with man who would come to I simply with 
 the desire of deceive I . of course I be unfit to deal with member 
 of Parliament who would flock around I because they want place . 
 of course I be unfit to answer every man 's question so as to give no 
 information to any one . " 
 " could you not answer they so as to give information ? " say Mr. 
 Kennedy . 
 but Mr. Turnbull be so intent on his speech that it may be doubt 
 whether he hear this interruption . he take no notice of it as he 
 go on . " of course I be unfit to maintain the propriety of a 
 seem confidence between a Crown all - powerless and a people 
 all - powerful . no man recognise his own unfitness for such work more 
 clearly than I do , Mr. Monk . but if I take in hand such work at all , 
 I should like to be the leader , and not the lead . tell we fairly , now , 
 what be your conviction worth in Mr. Mildmay 's Cabinet ? " 
 " that be a question which a man may hardly answer himself , " say Mr. 
 Monk . 
 " it be a question which a man should at least answer for himself 
 before he consent to sit there , " say Mr. Turnbull , in a tone of 
 voice which be almost angry . 
 " and what reason have you for suppose that I have omit that 
 duty ? " say Mr. Monk . 
 " simply this , - that I can not reconcile your known opinion with the 
 practice of your colleague . " 
 " I will not tell you what my conviction may be worth in Mr. 
 Mildmay 's Cabinet . I will not take upon myself to say that they be 
 worth the chair on which I sit when I be there . but I will tell you

 shall judge of their worth . I think that they might possibly leaven

 absent myself . I think that when I be ask to join Mr. Mildmay 
 and Mr. Gresham , the very fact of that request indicate liberal 
 progress , and that if I refuse the request I should be decline to 
 assist in good work . " 
 " you could have support they , if anything be propose worthy of

 " yes ; but I could not have be so effective in take care that 
 some measure be propose worthy of support as I may possibly be now . 
 I think a good deal about it , and I believe that my decision be 
 right . " 
 " I be sure you be right , " say Mr. Kennedy . 
 " there can be no juster object of ambition than a seat in the 
 Cabinet , " say Phineas . 
 " Sir , I must dispute that , " say Mr. Turnbull , turn round upon our 
 hero . " I regard the position of our high Ministers as most 
 respectable . " 
 " thank you for so much , " say Mr. Monk . but the orator go on again , 
 regardless of the interruption : - 
 " the position of gentleman in inferior office , - of gentleman who 
 attend rather to the nod and wink of their superior in Downing 
 Street than to the interest of their constituent , - I do not regard 
 as be highly respectable . " 
 " a man can not begin at the top , " say Phineas . 
 " our friend Mr. Monk have begin at what you be pleased to call the 
 top , " say Mr. Turnbull . " but I will not profess to think that even 
 he have raise himself by go into office . to be an independent 
 representative of a really popular commercial constituency be , in my 
 estimation , the high object of an Englishman 's ambition . " 
 " but why commercial , Mr. Turnbull ? " say Mr. Kennedy . 
 " because the commercial constituency really do elect their own 
 member in accordance with their own judgment , whereas the county 
 and the small town be coerce either by individual or by a 
 combination of aristocratic influence . " 
 " and yet , " say Mr. Kennedy , " there be not half a dozen 
 conservative return by all the county in Scotland . " 
 " Scotland be very much to be honour , " say Mr. Turnbull . 
 Mr. Kennedy be the first to take his departure , and Mr. Turnbull 
 follow he very quickly . phinea get up to go at the same time , but 
 stay at his host 's request , and sit for awhile smoke a cigar . 
 " turnbull be a wonderful man , " say Mr. Monk . 
 " do he not domineer too much ? " 
 " his fault be not arrogance , so much as ignorance that there be , 
 or should be , a difference between public and private life . in the

 dictatorial assurance . he be always address , not the House only ,

 Turnbull and the little Turnbulls have of it ? " 
 Phineas , as he go home , make up his mind that Mrs. Turnbull and 
 the little Turnbulls must probably have a bad time of it .

 Lord Chiltern rid his Horse Bonebreaker 
 it be know that whatever might be the detail of Mr. Mildmay 's

 CHAPTER XX 
 the Debate on the Ballot 
 Phineas take his seat in the House with a consciousness of much 
 inward trepidation of heart on that night of the ballot debate . after 
 leave Lord Chiltern he go down to his club and din alone . three 
 or four man come and speak to he ; but he could not talk to they at 
 his ease , nor do he quite know what they be say to he . he 
 be go to do something which he long to achieve , but the very 
 idea of which , now that it be so near to he , be a terror to he . 
 to be in the House and not to speak would , to his thinking , be a 
 disgraceful failure . indeed , he could not continue to keep his seat 
 unless he speak . he have be put there that he might speak . he would 
 speak . of course he would speak . have he not already be conspicuous 
 almost as a boy orator ? and yet , at this moment he do not know 
 whether he be eat mutton or beef , or who be stand opposite to 
 he and talk to he , so much be he in dread of the ordeal which 
 he have prepare for himself . as he go down to the House after 
 dinner , he almost make up his mind that it would be a good thing to 
 leave London by one of the night mail train . he feel himself to be 
 stiff and stilte as he walk , and that his clothe be uneasy to 
 he . when he turn into Westminster Hall he regret more keenly 
 than ever he have do that he have secede from the keeping of Mr. 
 Low . he could , he think , have speak very well in court , and would 
 there have learn that self - confidence which now fail he so 
 terribly . it be , however , too late to think of that . he could only 
 go in and take his seat . 
 he go in and take his seat , and the chamber seem to he to be 
 mysteriously large , as though bench be crowd over bench , and 
 gallery over gallery . he have be long enough in the House to 
 have lose the original awe inspire by the Speaker and the clerk of 
 the House , by the row of Ministers , and by the unequalled importance 
 of the place . on ordinary occasion he could saunter in and out , and 
 whisper at his ease to a neighbour . but on this occasion he go 
 direct to the bench on which he ordinarily sit , and begin at once to 
 rehearse to himself his speech . he have in truth be do this all 
 day , in spite of the effort that he have make to rid himself of all 
 memory of the occasion . he have be collect the head of his 
 speech while Mr. Low have be talk to he , and refresh his 
 quotation in the presence of Lord Chiltern and the dumb - bell . he 
 have tax his memory and his intellect with various task , which , 
 as he fear , would not adjust themselves one with another . he have 
 learn the heading of his speech , - so that one head might follow 
 the other , and nothing be forget . and he have learn verbatim the 
 word which he intend to utter under each heading , - with a hope 
 that if any one compact part should be destroy or injure in its 
 compactness by treachery of memory , or by the course of the debate , 
 each other compact part might be there in its entirety , ready for 
 use ; - or at least so many of the compact part as treachery of 
 memory and the accident of the debate might leave to he ; so 
 that his speech might be like a vessel , watertight in its various 
 compartment , that would float by the buoyancy of its stern and bow , 
 even though the hold should be waterlogge . but this use of his 
 compose word , even though he should be able to carry it through , 
 would not complete his work ; - for it would be his duty to answer in 
 some sort those who have go before he , and in order to do this he 
 must be able to insert , without any prearrangement of word or idea , 
 little intercalatory part between those compact mass of argument 
 with which he have be occupy himself for many laborious hour . as 
 he look round upon the House and perceive that everything be dim 
 before he , that all his original awe of the House have return , and 
 with it a present quake fear that make he feel the pulsation 
 of his own heart , he become painfully aware that the task he have 
 prepare for himself be too great . he should , on this the occasion

 a short general speech , which could indeed have do little for his 
 credit in the House , but which might have serve to carry off the 
 novelty of the thing , and have introduce he to the sound of his own 
 voice within those wall , - or he should have trust to what his wit 
 and spirit would produce for he on the spur of the moment , and not 
 have burden himself with a huge exercise of memory . during the

 intend to satisfy the most eager of his friend , and to astound his

 the debate begin , and if the leisure afford by a long and tedious

 it be not for he to ask the Speaker to count the House , but he

 be no malicious member then present , or else it be know that Lords 
 of the Treasury and Lords of the Admiralty would flock in during

 ballot - lover 's verbosity be permit to evolve itself without 
 interruption . at eight o'clock he have complete his catalogue of 
 illustration , and immediately Mr. Monk rise from the Treasury bench

 support the motion before the House . 
 Phineas be aware that Mr. Monk intend to speak , and be aware also 
 that his speech would be very short . " my idea be , " he have say to

 have and to express a political opinion of his own ; that otherwise 
 the franchise be not worth have ; and that man will learn that when 
 all so dare , no evil can come from such daring . as the ballot would 
 make any courage of that kind unnecessary , I dislike the ballot . I 
 shall confine myself to that , and leave the illustration to young 
 debater . " Phineas also have be inform that Mr. Turnbull would

 not command his opportunity . there be the chapter of accident to 
 which he must accommodate himself ; but such have be his programme

 Mr. Monk make his speech , - and though he be short , he be very fiery 
 and energetic . quick as lightning word of wrath and scorn fly from 
 he , in which he paint the cowardice , the meanness , the falsehood

 then Mr. Turnbull rise slowly from the bench below the gangway . with

 of enemy . Mr. Monk be taunt with his office , taunt with his

 just after this , while Mr. Turnbull be still go on with a variety

 the chamber seem to swim round before our hero 's eye . Mr. Turnbull

 approach to contempt for listening opponent , and nothing of fear

 Mr. Turnbull continue speak quite long enough to give poor

 Mr. Bonteen 's speech be in no way remarkable . Mr. Monk , he say , have

 he leave the House alone , carefully avoid all speech with any one .

 he think that Lady Laura Kennedy would be at home , and probably

 " yes ; it be over . " 
 " and what have they do , - those leviathan of the people ? " 
 then Phineas tell she what be the majority .

 at he suddenly . " be you not well ? " 
 " yes ; I be very well . "

 " I have simply be the great idiot , the great coward , the most

 this be the kind of sympathy which he want , she draw her chair

 " I have very little doubt but that you be right in allow Mr.

 " I do not care what Mr. Kennedy may think . "

 " he would not be too kind to you . he be never too kind to any one . he

 " I do not   care twopence about the ballot , " say Violet , as she put out

 that be the question . "

 " oh , Mr. Kennedy ! Mr. Kennedy never tell one anything . I doubt

 good - night , Mr. Finn . " 
 " what a beautiful creature she be ! " say Phineas .

 it will be remember that this be say on the day before Lord

 at any rate , he have be comfort . 
 CHAPTER XXI 
 " do be punctual " 
 on the very morning after his failure in the House of Commons , when 
 Phineas be read in the Telegraph , - he take the Telegraph not 
 from choice but for economy , - the word of that debate which he have 
 hear and in which he should have take a part , a most unwelcome 
 visit be pay to he . it be near eleven , and the breakfast thing 
 be still on the table . he be at this time on a Committee of the 
 House with reference to the use of potted pea in the army and 
 navy , at which he have sit once , - at a preliminary meeting , - and in 
 reference to which he have already resolve that as he have fail so 
 frightfully in debate , he would certainly do his duty to the utmost 
 in the more easy but infinitely more tedious work of the Committee 
 Room . the Committee meet at twelve , and he intend to walk down to 
 the Reform Club , and then to the House . he have just complete his 
 reading of the debate and of the leader in the Telegraph on the 
 subject . he have tell himself how little the writer of the article 
 know about Mr. Turnbull , how little about Mr. Monk , and how little 
 about the people , - such be his own idea as to the qualification 
 of the writer of that leading article , - and be about to start . but 
 Mrs. Bunce arrest he by tell he that there be a man below who 
 want to see he . 
 " what sort of a man , Mrs. Bunce ? " 
 " he be not   a gentleman , sir . " 
 " do he give his name ? " 
 " he do not , sir ; but I know it be about money . I know the way of 
 they so well . I have   see this one 's face before somewhere . " 
 " you have well show he up , " say Phineas . he know well the business 
 on which the man be come . the man want money for that bill which 
 Laurence Fitzgibbon have send afloat , and which Phineas have endorse . 
 Phineas have never as yet fall so deeply into trouble of money as 
 to make it necessary that he need refuse himself to any caller on 
 that score , and he do not choose to do so now . nevertheless he most 
 heartily wish that he have leave his lodging for the club before the 
 man have come . this be not the first he have hear of the bill be 
 overdue and unpaid . the bill have be bring to he note a month 
 since , and then he have simply tell the youth who bring it that he 
 would see Mr. Fitzgibbon and have the matter settle . he have speak 
 to his friend Laurence , and Laurence have simply assure he that all 
 should be make right in two day , - or , at furth , by the end of 
 a week . since that time he have observe that his friend have be 
 somewhat shy of speak to he when no other be with they . 
 Phineas would not have allude to the bill have he and Laurence be 
 alone together ; but he have be quick enough to guess from his 
 friend 's manner that the matter be not settle . now , no doubt , 
 serious trouble be about to commence . 
 the visitor be a little man with grey hair and a white cravat , some 
 sixty year of age , dress in black , with a very decent hat , - which , 
 on enter the room , he at once put down on the near chair , - with 
 reference to whom , any judge on the subject would have concur at 
 first sight in the decision pronounce by Mrs. Bunce , though none 
 but a judge very well use to sift the cause of his own conclusion 
 could have give the reason for that early decision . " he be not   a 
 gentleman , " Mrs. Bunce have say . and the man certainly be not a 
 gentleman . the old man in the white cravat be very neatly dress , 
 and carry himself without any of that humility which betray one 
 class of uncertified aspirant to gentility , or of that assume 
 arrogance which be at once fatal to another class . but , nevertheless , 
 Mrs. Bunce have see at a glance that he be not a gentleman , - have 
 see , moreover , that such a man could have come only upon one 
 mission . she be right there too . this visitor have come about money . 
 " about this bill , Mr. Finn , " say the visitor , proceed to take 
 out of his breast coat - pocket a rather large leathern case , as he 
 advance up towards the fire . " my name be Clarkson , Mr. Finn . if I 
 may venture so far , I will take a chair . " 
 " certainly , Mr. Clarkson , " say Phineas , get up and point to 
 a seat . 
 " Thankye , Mr. Finn , thankye . we shall be more comfortable do 
 business sitting , shall not   we ? " whereupon the horrid little man draw 
 himself close in to the fire , and spread out his leathern case 
 upon his knee , begin to turn over one suspicious bit of paper after 
 another , as though he be uncertain in what part of his portfolio 
 lie this identical bit which he be seek . he seem to be quite 
 at home , and to feel that there be no ground whatever for hurry 
 in such comfortable quarter . Phineas hat he at once , - with a 
 hatred altogether unconnected with the difficulty which his friend 
 Fitzgibbon have bring upon he . 
 " here it be , " say Mr. Clarkson at last . " oh , dear I , dear I ! the 
 third of November , and here we be in March ! I do not   think it be 
 so bad as this ; - I do not   indeed . this be very bad , - very bad ! and 
 for Parliament gent , too , who should be more punctual than anybody , 
 because of the privilege . should not   they now , Mr. Finn ? " 
 " all man should be punctual , I suppose , " say Phineas . 
 " of course they should ; of course they should . I always say to my 
 gent , ' be punctual , and I will do anything for you . ' but , perhaps , Mr. 
 Finn , you can hand I a cheque for this amount , and then you and I 
 will begin square . " 
 " indeed I can not , Mr. Clarkson . " 
 " not hand I a cheque for it ! " 
 " upon my word , no . " 
 " that be very bad ; - very bad indeed . then I suppose I must take the 
 half , and renew for the remainder , though I do not   like it ; - I do not 
 indeed . " 
 " I can pay no part of that bill , Mr. Clarkson . " 
 " pay no part of it ! " and Mr. Clarkson , in order that he might the 
 well express his surprise , arrest his hand in the very act of 
 poke his host 's fire . 
 " if you will allow I , I will manage the fire , " say Phineas , put out 
 his hand for the poker . 
 but Mr. Clarkson be fond of poking fire , and would not surrender 
 the poker . " pay no part of it ! " he say again , hold the poker away 
 from Phineas in his left hand . " do not   say that , Mr. Finn . pray do not 
 say that . do not   drive I to be severe . I do not   like to be severe with 
 my gent . I will do anything , Mr. Finn , if you will only be punctual . " 
 " the fact be , Mr. Clarkson , I have never have one penny of 
 consideration for that bill , and - " 
 " oh , Mr. Finn ! oh , Mr. Finn ! " and then Mr. Clarkson have his will of 
 the fire . 
 " I never have one penny of consideration for that bill , " continue 
 Phineas . " of course , I do not   deny my responsibility . " 
 " no , Mr. Finn ; you can not   deny that . here it be ; - Phineas Finn ; - and 
 everybody know you , because you be a Parliament gent . " 
 " I do not   deny it . but I have no reason to suppose that I should 
 be call upon for the money when I accommodate my friend , Mr. 
 Fitzgibbon , and I have not get it . that be the long and the short 
 of it . I must see he and take care that arrangement be make . " 
 " arrangement ! " 
 " yes , arrangement for settle the bill . " 
 " he have not   get the money , Mr. Finn . you know that as well as I do . " 
 " I know nothing about it , Mr. Clarkson . " 
 " oh yes , Mr. Finn ; you know ; you know . " 
 " I tell you I know nothing about it , " say Phineas , wax angry . 
 " as to Mr. Fitzgibbon , he be the pleasantest gent that ever live . 
 be not   he now ? I have   know'd he these ten year . I do not   suppose that 
 for ten year I have   be without his name in my pocket . but , bless 
 you , Mr. Finn , there be an end to everything . I should not   have look 
 at this bit of paper if it have not   be for your signature . of course 
 not . you be just begin , and it be natural you should want a little 
 help . you will find I always ready , if you will only be punctual . " 
 " I tell you again , sir , that I never have a shilling out of that for 
 myself , and do not want any such help . " here Mr. Clarkson smile 
 sweetly . " I give my name to my friend simply to oblige he . " 
 " I like you irish gent because you do hang together so close , " say 
 Mr. Clarkson . 
 " simply to oblige he , " continue Phineas . " as I say before , I know 
 that I be responsible ; but , as I say before also , I have not the 
 mean of take up that bill . I will see Mr. Fitzgibbon , and let 
 you know what we propose to do . " then Phineas get up from his seat 
 and take his hat . it be full time that he should go down to his 
 Committee . but Mr. Clarkson do not get up from his seat . " I be afraid 
 I must ask you to leave I now , Mr. Clarkson , as I have business down 
 at the House . " 
 " business at the House never press , Mr. Finn , " say Mr. Clarkson . 
 " that be the good of Parliament . I have   know Parliament gent this 
 thirty year and more . would you believe it - I have   have a Prime 
 Minister 's name in that portfolio ; that I have ; and a Lord 
 Chancellor 's ; that I have ; - and an Archbishop 's too . I know 
 what Parliament be , Mr. Finn . come , come ; do not   put I off with 
 Parliament . " 
 there he sit before the fire with his pouch open before he , and 
 Phineas have no power of move he . could Phineas have pay he the 
 money which be manifestly due to he on the bill , the man would of 
 course have go ; but fail in that , Phineas could not turn he 
 out . there be a black cloud on the young member 's brow , and great 
 anger at his heart , - against Fitzgibbon rather than against the man 
 who be sit there before he . " Sir , " he say , " it be really 
 imperative that I should go . I be pledge to an appointment at the 
 House at twelve , and it want now only a quarter . I regret that your 
 interview with I should be so unsatisfactory , but I can only promise 
 you that I will see Mr. Fitzgibbon . " 
 " and when shall I call again , Mr. Finn ? " 
 " perhaps I have well write to you , " say Phineas . 
 " oh dear , no , " say Mr. Clarkson . " I should much prefer to look in . 
 look in be always best . we can get to understand one another in 
 that way . let I see . I daresay you be not particular . suppose I say 
 Sunday morning . " 
 " really , I could not see you on Sunday morning , Mr. Clarkson . " 
 " parliament gent be not   generally particular , - ' speciaily not among 
 the Catholics , " plead Mr. Clarkson . 
 " I be always engage on Sundays , " say Phineas . 
 " suppose we say Monday , - or Tuesday . Tuesday morning at eleven . and 
 do be punctual , Mr. Finn . at Tuesday morning I will come , and then no 
 doubt I shall find you ready . " Whereupon Mr. Clarkson slowly put up

 it be now twelve , and Phineas rush off to a cab . he be in such

 advocate of the potted pea on the other . the potter of the pea , 
 who want to sell their article to the Crown , declare that an 
 extensive , - perhaps we may say , an unlimited , - use of the article 
 would save the whole army and navy from the scourge of scurvy , 
 dyspepsia , and rheumatism , would be the good safeguard against

 be give in German , and have to be translate into English . and

 CHAPTER XXII 
 Lady Baldock at home

 CHAPTER XXIII 
 Sunday in Grosvenor Place 
 Mr. Kennedy , though he be a most scrupulously attentive member of 
 Parliament , be a man very punctual to hour and rule in his own 
 house , - and like that his wife should be as punctual as himself . 
 Lady Laura , who in marry he have firmly resolve that she would do 
 her duty to he in all way , even though the way might sometimes be 
 painful , - and have be perhaps more punctilious in this respect than 
 she might have be have she love he heartily , - be not perhaps 
 quite so fond of accurate regularity as her husband ; and thus , by 
 this time , certain habit of his have become rather bond than habit 
 to she . he always have prayer at nine , and breakfast at a quarter 
 past nine , let the hour on the night before have be as late as 
 they might before the time for rest have come . after breakfast he 
 would open his letter in his study , but he like she to be with 
 he , and desire to discuss with she every application he get from 
 a constituent . he have his private secretary in a room apart , but he 
 think that everything should be filter to his private secretary 
 through his wife . he be very anxious that she herself should 
 superintend the account of their own private expenditure , and have 
 take some trouble to teach she an excellent mode of book - keeping . 
 he have recommend to she a certain course of reading , - which be 
 pleasant enough ; lady like to receive such recommendation ; but Mr. 
 Kennedy , have draw out the course , seem to expect that his wife 
 should read the book he have name , and , bad still , that she should 
 read they in the time he have allocate for the work . this , I think , 
 be tyranny . then the Sundays become very wearisome to Lady Laura . 
 go to church twice , she have learn , would be a part of her duty ; 
 and though in her father 's household attendance at church have never 
 be very strict , she have make up her mind to this cheerfully . but 
 Mr. Kennedy expect also that he and she should always dine together 
 on Sundays , that there should be no guest , and that there should be 
 no evening company . after all , the demand be not very severe , but 
 yet she find that it operate injuriously upon her comfort . the 
 Sundays be very wearisome to she , and make she feel that her lord 
 and master be - her lord and master . she make an effort or two to 
 escape , but the effort be all in vain . he never speak a cross word 
 to she . he never give a stern command . but yet he have his way . " I 
 will not   say that read a novel on a Sunday be a sin , " he say ; " but 
 we must at any rate admit that it be a matter on which man disagree , 
 that many of the good of man be against such occupation on Sunday , 
 and that to abstain be to be on the safe side . " so the novel be 
 put away , and Sunday afternoon with the long evening become rather 
 a stumble - block to Lady Laura . 
 those two hour , moreover , with her husband in the morning become 
 very wearisome to she . at first she have declare that it would be her 
 great ambition to help her husband in his work , and she have read 
 all the letter from the MacNabs and MacFies , ask to be make 
 gauger and landing - waiter , with an assumed interest . but the work 
 pal upon she very quickly . her quick intellect discover soon 
 that there be nothing in it which she really do . it be all form 
 and verbiage , and pretence at business . her husband go through it 
 all with the utmost patience , read every word , give order as 
 to every detail , and conscientiously do that which he conceive 
 he have undertake to do . but Lady Laura want to meddle with high 
 politic , to discuss reform bill , to assist in put up Mr. this 
 and put down my Lord that . why should she waste her time in 
 do that which the lad in the next room , who be call a private 
 secretary , could do as well ? 
 still she would obey . let the task be as hard as it might , she would 
 obey . if he counsel she to do this or that , she would follow his 
 counsel , - because she owe he so much . if she have accept the half 
 of all his wealth without love he , she owe he the more on that 
 account . but she know , - she could not but know , - that her intellect 
 be bright than his ; and might it not be possible for she to lead 
 he ? then she make effort to lead her husband , and find that he be 
 as stiff - neck as an ox . Mr. Kennedy be not , perhaps , a clever man ; 
 but he be a man who know his own way , and who intend to keep it . 
 " I have get a headache , Robert , " she say to he one Sunday after 
 luncheon . " I think I will not go to church this afternoon . " 
 " it be not serious , I hope . " 
 " oh dear no . do not   you know how one feel sometimes that one have get 
 a head ? and when that be the case one 's armchair be the good place . " 
 " I be not sure of that , " say Mr. Kennedy . 
 " if I go to church I should not attend , " say Lady Laura . 
 " the fresh air would do you more good than anything else , and we 
 could walk across the park . " 
 " thank you ; - I will not   go out again to - day . " this she say with 
 something almost of crossness in her manner , and Mr. Kennedy go to 
 the afternoon service by himself . 
 Lady Laura when she be leave alone begin to think of her position . 
 she be not more than four or five month marry , and she be 
 become very tired of her life . be it not also true that she be 
 become tired of her husband ? she have twice tell Phineas Finn that 
 of all man in the world she esteem Mr. Kennedy the most . she do 
 not esteem he less now . she know no point or particle in which 
 he do not do his duty with accuracy . but no person can live 
 happily with another , - not even with a brother or a sister or a 
 friend , - simply upon esteem . all the virtue in the calendar , 
 though they exist on each side , will not make a man and woman happy 
 together , unless there be sympathy . Lady Laura be begin to 
 find out that there be a lack of sympathy between herself and her 
 husband . 
 she think of this till she be tired of thinking of it , and then , 
 wish to divert her mind , she take up the book that be lie 
 near to her hand . it be a volume of a new novel which she have 
 be read on the previous day , and now , without much thought about 
 it , she go on with her reading . there come to she , no doubt , some 
 dim , half - form idea that , as she be free from go to church by 
 the plea of a headache , she be also absolve by the same plea from 
 other Sunday hindrance . a child , when it be ill , have butter toast 
 and a picture - book instead of bread - and - milk and lesson . in this 
 way , Lady Laura conceive herself to be entitle to her novel . 
 while she be read it , there come a knock at the door , and 
 Barrington Erle be show upstairs . Mr. Kennedy have give no order 
 against Sunday visitor , but have simply say that Sunday visit be 
 not to his taste . Barrington , however , be Lady Laura 's cousin , and 
 people must be very strict if they can not   see their cousin on Sunday . 
 Lady Laura soon lose her headache altogether in the animation 
 of discuss the chance of the new Reform Bill with the Prime 
 Minister 's private secretary ; and have leave her chair , and be 
 stand by the table with the novel in her hand , protest this 
 and deny that , express infinite confidence in Mr. Monk , and 
 violently denounce Mr. Turnbull , when her husband return from 
 church and come up into the drawing - room . Lady Laura have forget 
 her headache altogether , and have in her composition none of that 
 thoughtfulness of hypocrisy which would have teach she to moderate 
 her political feeling at her husband 's return . 
 " I do declare , " she say , " that if Mr. Turnbull oppose the 
 Government measure now , because he can not   have his own way in 
 everything , I will never again put my trust in any man who call 
 himself a popular leader . " 
 " you never should , " say Barrington Erle . 
 " that be all very well for you , Barrington , who be an aristocratic 
 Whig of the old official school , and who call yourself a Liberal 
 simply because Fox be a Liberal a hundred year ago . my heart be in 
 it . " 
 " heart should never have anything to do with politic ; should it ? " 
 say Erle , turn round to Mr. Kennedy . 
 Mr. Kennedy do not wish to discuss the matter on a Sunday , nor yet 
 do he wish to say before Barrington Erle that he think it wrong 
 to do so . and he be desirous of treat his wife in some way 
 as though she be an invalid , - that she thereby might be , as it 
 be , punish ; but he do not wish to do this in such a way that 
 Barrington should be aware of the punishment . 
 " Laura have well not disturb herself about it now , " he say . 
 " how be a person to help be disturb ? " say Lady Laura , laugh . 
 " well , well ; we will not   mind all that now , " say Mr. Kennedy , turn 
 away . then he take up the novel which Lady Laura have just lay down 
 from her hand , and , have look at it , carry it aside , and place 
 it on a book - shelf which be remote from they . Lady Laura watch he 
 as he do this , and the whole course of her husband 's thought on the 
 subject be open to she at once . she regret the novel , and she 
 regret also the political discussion . soon afterwards Barrington 
 Erle go away , and the husband and wife be alone together . 
 " I be glad that your head be so much well , " say he . he do not 
 intend to be severe , but he speak with a gravity of manner which 
 almost amount to severity . 
 " yes ; it be , " she say , " Barrington 's come in cheer I up . " 
 " I be sorry that you should have want cheer . " 
 " do not   you know what I mean , Robert ? " 
 " no ; I do not think that I do , exactly . " 
 " I suppose your head be strong . you do not get that feeling 
 of daze , helpless imbecility of brain , which hardly amount to 
 headache , but which yet - be almost as bad . " 
 " imbecility of brain may be bad than headache , but I do not   think it 
 can produce it . " 
 " well , well ; - I do not   know how to explain it . " 
 " Headache come , I think , always from the stomach , even when produce 
 by nervous affection . but imbecility of the brain - " 
 " oh , Robert , I be so sorry that I use the word . " 
 " I see that it do not prevent your reading , " he say , after a pause . 
 " not such reading as that . I be up to nothing well . " 
 then there be another pause . 
 " I will not   deny that it may be a prejudice , " he say , " but I confess 
 that the use of novel in my own house on Sundays be a pain to I . 
 my mother 's idea on the subject be very strict , and I can not think 
 that it be bad for a son to hang on to the teaching of his mother . " 
 this he say in the most serious tone which he could command . 
 " I do not   know why I take it up , " say Lady Laura . " simply , I believe , 
 because it be there . I will avoid do so for the future . " 
 " do , my dear , " say the husband . " I shall be oblige and grateful if 
 you will remember what I have say . " then he leave she , and she sit 
 alone , first in the dusk and then in the dark , for two hour , do 
 nothing . be this to be the life which she have procure for herself 
 by marry Mr. Kennedy of Loughlinter ? if it be harsh and 
 unendurable in London , what would it be in the country ? 
 CHAPTER XXIV 
 the Willingford Bull 
 Phineas leave London by a night mail train on Easter Sunday , and find 
 himself at the Willingford Bull about half an hour after midnight . 
 Lord Chiltern be up and wait for he , and supper be on the 
 table . the Willingford Bull be an English inn of the old stamp , 
 which have now , in these latter year of railway travel , cease 
 to have a road business , - for there be no traveller on the road , 
 and but little posting - but have acquire a new trade as a depot for 
 hunter and hunt man . the landlord let out horse and keep hunt 
 stable , and the house be generally fill from the beginning of 
 November till the middle of April . then it become a desert in the 
 summer , and no guest be see there , till the pink coat flock 
 down again into the shire . 
 " how many day do you mean to give we ? " say Lord Chiltern , as he 
 help his friend to a devilled leg of turkey . 
 " I must go back on Wednesday , " say Phineas . 
 " that mean Wednesday night . I will tell you what we will do . we have   the 
 Cottesmore to - morrow . we will get into Tailby 's country on Tuesday , and 
 Fitzwilliam will be only twelve mile off on Wednesday . we shall be 
 rather short of horse . " 
 " pray do not   let I put you out . I can hire something here , I 
 suppose ? " 
 " you will not   put I out at all . there will be three between we each day , 
 and we will run our luck . the horse have go on to Empingham for 
 to - morrow . Tailby be rather a way off , - at Somerby ; but we will manage 
 it . if the bad come to the bad , we can get back to Stamford by 
 rail . on Wednesday we shall have everything very comfortable . they be 
 out beyond Stilton and will draw home our way . I have   plan it all 
 out . I have   a trap with a fast stepper , and if we start to - morrow at 
 half - past nine , we shall be in plenty of time . you shall ride Meg 
 Merrilies , and if she do not   carry you , you may shoot she . " 
 " be she one of the pull one ? " 
 " she be heavy in hand if you be heavy at she , but leave her mouth 
 alone and she will go like flow water . you would well not ride more 
 in a crowd than you can help . now what will you drink ? " 
 they sit up half the night smoking and talking , and Phineas learn 
 more about Lord Chiltern then than ever he have learn before . there 
 be brandy and water before they , but neither of they drank . Lord 
 Chiltern , indeed , have a pint of beer by his side from which he sip 
 occasionally . " I have   take to beer , " he say , " as be the good drink 
 go . when a man hunt six day a week he can afford to drink beer . 
 I be on an allowance , - three pint a day . that be not too much . " 
 " and you drink nothing else ? " 
 " nothing when I be alone , - except a little cherry - brandy when I be out . 
 I never care for drink ; - never in my life . I do like excitement , and 
 have be less careful than I ought to have be as to what it have 
 come from . I could give up drink to - morrow , without a struggle , - if 
 it be worth my while to make up my mind to do it . and it be the same 
 with gambling . I never do gamble now , because I have   get no money ; but 
 I own I like it well than anything in the world . while you be at 
 it , there be life in it . " 
 " you should take to politic , Chiltern . " 
 " and I would have do so , but my father would not help I . never 
 mind , we will not talk about he . how do Laura get on with her 
 husband ? " 
 " very happily , I should say . " 
 " I do not   believe it , " say Lord Chiltern . " her temper be too much 
 like mine to allow she to be happy with such a log of wood as Robert 
 Kennedy . it be such man as he who drive I out of the pale of decent 
 life . if that be decency , I would soon be indecent . you mark my word . 
 they will come to grief . she will never be able to stand it . " 
 " I should think she have her own way in everything , " say Phineas . 
 " no , no . though he be a prig , he be a man ; and she will not find it 
 easy to drive he . " 
 " but she may bend he . " 
 " not an inch ; - that be if I understand his character . I suppose you 
 see a good deal of they ? " 
 " yes , - pretty well . I be not there so often as I use to be in the 
 Square . " 
 " you get sick of it , I suppose . I should . do you see my father 
 often ? " 
 " only occasionally . he be always very civil when I do see he . " 
 " he be the very pink of civility when he please , but the most unjust 
 man I ever meet . " 
 " I should not have think that . " 
 " yes , he be , " say the Earl 's son , " and all from lack of judgment to 
 discern the truth . he make up his mind to a thing on insufficient 
 proof , and then nothing will turn he . he think well of you , - would 
 probably believe your word on any indifferent subject without thought 
 of a doubt ; but if you be to tell he that I do not   get drunk every 
 night of my life and spend most of my time in thrash policeman , he 
 would not believe you . he would smile incredulously and make you a 
 little bow . I can see he do it . " 
 " you be too hard on he , Chiltern . " 
 " he have be too hard on I , I know . be Violet Effingham still in 
 Grosvenor Place ? " 
 " no ; she be with Lady Baldock . " 
 " that old grandmother of evil have come to town , - have she ? poor 
 Violet ! when we be young together we use to have such fun about 
 that old woman . " 
 " the old woman be an ally of mine now , " say Phineas . 
 " you make ally everywhere . you know Violet Effingham of course ? " 
 " oh yes . I know she . " 
 " do not   you think she very charming ? " say Lord Chiltern . 
 " exceedingly charming . " 
 " I have ask that girl to marry I three time , and I shall never 
 ask she again . there be a point beyond which a man should not   go . 
 there be many reason why it would be a good marriage . in the first 
 place , her money would be serviceable . then it would heal matter in 
 our family , for my father be as prejudiced in her favour as he be 
 against I . and I love she dearly . I have   love she all my life , - since 
 I use to buy cake for she . but I shall never ask she again . " 
 " I would if I be you , " say Phineas , - hardly know what it might 
 be good for he to say . 
 " no ; I never will . but I will tell you what . I shall get into some 
 desperate scrape about she . of course she will marry , and that soon . 
 then I shall make a fool of myself . when I hear that she be engage I 
 shall go and quarrel with the man , and kick he , - or get kick . all 
 the world will turn against I , and I shall be call a wild beast . " 
 " a dog in the manger be what you should be call . " 
 " exactly ; - but how be a man to help it ? if you love a girl , could 
 you see another man take she ? " Phineas remember of course that he 
 have lately come through this ordeal . " it be as though he be to come 
 and put his hand upon I , and want my own heart out of I . though 
 I have no property in she at all , no right to she , - though she never 
 give I a word of encouragement , it be as though she be the most 
 private thing in the world to I . I should be half mad , and in my 
 madness I could not master the idea that I be be rob . I should 
 resent it as a personal interference . " 
 " I suppose it will come to that if you give she up yourself , " say 
 Phineas . 
 " it be no question of give up . of course I can not make her marry 
 I . light another cigar , old fellow . " 
 Phineas , as he light the other cigar , remember that he owe a certain 
 duty in this matter to Lady Laura . she have commission he to 
 persuade her brother that his suit with Violet Effingham would not be 
 hopeless , if he could only restrain himself in his mode of conduct 
 it . Phineas be dispose to do his duty , although he feel it to be 
 very hard that he should be call upon to be eloquent against his 
 own interest . he have be think for the last quarter of an hour 
 how he must bear himself if it might turn out that he should be the 
 man whom Lord Chiltern be resolve to kick . he look at his friend 
 and host , and become aware that a kicking - match with such a one would 
 not be pleasant pastime . nevertheless , he would be happy enough to be 
 subject to Lord Chiltern 's wrath for such a reason . he would do his 
 duty by Lord Chiltern ; and then , when that have be adequately do , 
 he would , if occasion serve , fight a battle for himself . 
 " you be too sudden with she , Chiltern , " he say , after a pause . 
 " what do you mean by too sudden ? " say Lord Chiltern , almost angrily . 
 " you frighten she by be so impetuous . you rush at she as though 
 you want to conquer she by a single blow . " 
 " so I do . " 
 " you should be more gentle with she . you should give her time to find 
 out whether she like you or not . " 
 " she have know I all her life , and have find that out long ago . not 
 but what you be right . I know you be right . if I be you , and have 
 your skill in pleasing , I should drop soft word into her ear till I 
 have catch she . but I have no gift in that way . I be as awkward as 
 a pig at what be call flirt . and I have an accursed pride which 
 stand in my own light . if she be in this house this moment , and 
 if I know she be to be have for ask , I do not   think I could bring 
 myself to ask again . but we will go to bed . it be half - past two , and we 
 must be off at half - past nine , if we be to be at Exton Park gate at 
 eleven . " 
 Phineas , as he go up - stair , assure himself that he have do his 
 duty . if there ever should come to be anything between he and Violet 
 Effingham , Lord Chiltern might quarrel with he , - might probably 
 attempt that kicking encounter to which allusion have be make , - but 
 nobody could justly say that he have not behave honourably to his 
 friend . 
 on the next morning there be a bustle and a scurry , as there always 
 be on such occasion , and the two man get off about ten minute after 
 time . but Lord Chiltern drive hard , and they reach the meet before 
 the master have move off . they have a fair day 's sport with the 
 Cottesmore ; and Phineas , though he find that Meg Merrilies do 
 require a good deal of riding , go through his day 's work with 
 credit . he have be rid since he be a child , as be the custom 
 with all boy in Munster , and have an Irishman 's natural aptitude for 
 jump . when they get back to the Willingford Bull he feel pleased 
 with the day and rather proud of himself . " it be not   fast , you know , " 
 say Chiltern , " and I do not   call that a stiff country . besides , Meg 
 be very handy when you have   get she out of the crowd . you shall ride 
 Bonebreaker to - morrow at Somerby , and you will find that well fun . " 
 " Bonebreaker ? have not   I hear you say he rush like mischief ? " 
 " well , he do rush . but , by George ! you want a horse to rush in that 
 country . when you have to go right through four or five foot of stiff 
 green wood , like a bullet through a target , you want a little force , 
 or you be apt to be leave up a tree . " 
 " and what do you ride ? " 
 " a brute I never put my leg on yet . he be send down to Wilcox here , 
 out of Lincolnshire , because they could not   get anybody to ride he 
 there . they say he go with his head up in the air , and will not   look 
 at a fence that be not   as high as his breast . but I think he will do 
 here . I never see a well make beast , or one with more power . do you 
 look at his shoulder . he be to be have for seventy pound , and these 
 be the sort of horse I like to buy . " 
 again they din alone , and Lord Chiltern explain to Phineas that 
 he rarely associate with the man of either of the hunt in which 
 he ride . " there be a set of fellow down here who be poison to I , 
 and there be another set , and I be poison to they . everybody be 
 very civil , as you see , but I have no associate . and gradually I be 
 get to have a reputation as though I be the devil himself . I 
 think I shall come out next year dress entirely in black . " 
 " be you not wrong to give way to that kind of thing ? " 
 " what the deuce be I to do ? I can not   make civil little speech . when 
 once a man get a reputation as an ogre , it be the most difficult 
 thing in the world to drop it . I could have a score of man here every 
 day if I like it , - my title would do that for I ; - but they would 
 be man I should loathe , and I should be sure to tell they so , 
 even though I do not mean it . Bonebreaker , and the new horse , 
 and another , go on at twelve to - day . you must expect hard work 
 to - morrow , as I daresay we shall not   be home before eight . " 
 the next day 's meet be in Leicestershire , not far from Melton , and 
 they start early . Phineas , to tell the truth of he , be rather 
 afraid of Bonebreaker , and look forward to the probability of an 
 accident . he have neither wife nor child , and nobody have a well 
 right to risk his neck . " we will put a gag on ' I m , " say the groom , 
 " and you will ride ' I m in a ring , - so that you may well - nigh break 
 his jaw ; but he be a rum un , sir . " " I will do my good , " say Phineas . 
 " he will take all that , " say the groom . " just let he have his own way 
 at everything , " say Lord Chiltern , as they move away from the meet 
 to Pickwell Gorse ; " and if you will only sit on his back , he will carry 
 you through as safe as a church . " Phineas could not help think 
 that the counsel of the master and of the groom be very different . 
 " my idea be , " continue Lord Chiltern , " that in hunt you should 
 always avoid a crowd . I do not   think a horse be worth rid that 
 will go in a crowd . it be just like yachting , - you should have plenty 
 of sea - room . if you be to pull your horse up at every fence till 
 somebody else be over , I think you would well come out on a donkey . " 
 and so they go away to Pickwell Gorse . 
 there be over two hundred man out , and Phineas begin to think that 
 it might not be so easy to get out of the crowd . a crowd in a fast 
 run no doubt quickly become small by degree and beautifully less ; 
 but it be very difficult , especially for a stranger , to free himself 
 from the rush at the first start . Lord Chiltern 's horse plunge about 
 so violently , as they stand on a little hill - side look down upon 
 the cover , that he be oblige to take he to a distance , and Phineas 
 follow he . " if he break down wind , " say Lord Chiltern , " we can not 
 be well than we be here . if he go up wind , he must turn before 
 long , and we shall be all right . " as he speak an old hound open 
 true and sharp , - an old hound whom all the pack believe , - and in a 
 moment there be no doubt that the fox have be find . " there be not 
 above eight or nine acre in it , " say Lord Chiltern , " and he can not 
 hang long . do you ever see such an uneasy brute as this in your 
 life ? but I feel certain he will go well when he get away . " 
 Phineas be too much occupy with his own horse to think much of 
 that on which Lord Chiltern be mount . Bonebreaker , the very moment 
 that he hear the old hound 's note , stretch out his head , and put 
 his mouth upon the bit , and begin to tremble in every muscle . " he be 
 a great deal more anxious for it than you and I be , " say Lord 
 Chiltern . " I see they have   give you that gag . but do not   you ride he 
 on it till he want it . give he lot of room , and he will go in the 
 snaffle . " all which caution make Phineas think that any insurance 
 office would charge very dear on his life at the present moment . 
 the fox take two ring of the gorse , and then he go , - up wind . 
 " it be not a vixen , I will swear , " say Lord Chiltern . " a vixen in cub 
 never go away like that yet . now then , Finn , my boy , keep to the 
 right . " and Lord Chiltern , with the horse out of Lincolnshire , go 
 away across the brow of the hill , leave the hound to the left , and 
 select , as his point of exit into the next field , a stiff rail , 
 which , have there be an accident , must have put a very wide margin 
 of ground between the rider and his horse . " go hard at your fence , 
 and then you will fall clear , " he have say to Phineas . I do not   think , 
 however , that he would have ride at the rail as he do , but 
 that there be no help for he . " the brute begin in his own way , 
 and carry on after in the same fashion all through , " he say 
 afterwards . Phineas take the fence a little low down , and what 
 it be at which he ride he never know . Bonebreaker sail over it , 
 whatever it be , and he soon find himself by his friend 's side . 
 the ruck of the man be low down than our two hero , and there 
 be other far away to the left , and other , again , who have be at 
 the end of the gorse , and be now behind . our friend be not near 
 the hound , not within two field of they , but the hound be below 
 they , and therefore could be see . " do not   be in a hurry , and they will 
 be round upon we , " Lord Chiltern say . " how the deuce be one to help 
 be in a hurry ? " say Phineas , who be do his very good to ride 
 Bonebreaker with the snaffle , but have already begin to feel that

 Lord Chiltern ride still a little in advance , and Phineas , know 
 his friend 's partiality for solitude when take his fence , keep a

 high - grow ox - fence , and by degree his rider come to feel that if

 the fox run up wind for a couple of mile or so , as Lord Chiltern have 
 prophesy , and then turn , - not to the right , as would well have

 far from each other , go in and out amidst a crowd of horse , and

 large pasture field , and Phineas begin to think that as long as

 that have be cut and lay , and these be not so pleasant . Force 
 be not sufficient for they , and they admit of a mistake . but the

 come without touch they . it might be all right yet , - unless the 
 beast should tire with he ; and then , Phineas think , a misfortune 
 might probably occur . he remember , as he fly over one such 
 impediment , that he ride a stone heavy than his friend . at the end 
 of forty - five minute Bonebreaker also might become aware of the 
 fact . 
 the hound be run well in sight to their right , and Phineas 
 begin to feel some of that pride which a man indulge when he become 
 aware that he have take his place comfortably , have leave the squad 
 behind , and be go well . there be man nearer the hound than he 
 be , but he be near enough even for ambition . there have already be 
 enough of the run to make he sure that it would be a " good thing " ,

 when a run be over , man be very apt to regret the termination , who 
 a minute or two before be anxiously long that the hound might 
 pull down their game . to finish well be everything in hunting . to 
 have lead for over an hour be nothing , let the pace and country have 
 be what they might , if you fall away during the last half mile . 
 therefore it be that those behind hope that the fox may make this 
 or that cover , while the forward man long to see he turn over in 
 every field . to ride to hound be very glorious ; but to have ride 
 to hound be more glorious still . they have now cross another road , 
 and a large one , and have get into a somewhat close country . the 
 field be not so big , and the fence be not so high . phinea get 
 a moment to look about he , and see Lord Chiltern rid without his

 there be brook in those part . the river Eye form itself 
 thereabout , or some of its tributary do so ; and these tributary , 
 though small as river , be considerable to man on one side who be 
 call by the exigency of the occasion to place themselves quickly 
 on the other . Phineas know nothing of these brook ; but Bonebreaker 
 have go gallantly over two , and now that there come a third in the 
 way , it be to be hop that he might go gallantly over that also . 
 Phineas , at any rate , have no power to decide otherwise . as long as

 close to he , still fight with his horse ; - but the farmer have 
 turn away . he think that Chiltern nod to he , as much as to 
 tell he to go on . on he go at any rate . the brook , when he come to 
 it , seem to be a huge black hole , yawn beneath he . the bank 
 be quite steep , and just where he be to take off there be an 
 ugly stump . it be too late to think of anything . he stick his knee 
 against his saddle , - and in a moment be on the other side . the 
 brute , who have take off a yard before the stump , know well the 
 danger of strike it with his foot , come down with a grunt , and do , 
 I think , begin to feel the weight of that extra stone . Phineas , as 
 soon as he be safe , look back , and there be Lord Chiltern 's horse 
 in the very act of his spring , - high up the rivulet , where it be 
 even broad . at that distance Phineas could see that Lord Chiltern 
 be wild with rage against the beast . but whether he wish to take

 animal rush at the brook , and in a moment the horse and horseman 
 be lose to sight . it be well then that that extra stone should 
 tell , as it enable Phineas to arrest his horse and to come back to 
 his friend . 
 the Lincolnshire horse have cheste the further bank , and of course 
 have fall back into the stream . when Phineas get down he find that 
 Lord Chiltern be wedge in between the horse and the bank , which be 
 well , at any rate , than be under the horse in the water . " all 
 right , old fellow , " he say , with a smile , when he see Phineas . " you 
 go on ; it be too good to lose . " but he be very pale , and seem to be 
 quite helpless where he lay . the horse do not move , - and never do 
 move again . he have smash his shoulder to piece against a stump on 
 the bank , and be afterwards shoot on that very spot . 
 when Phineas get down he find that there be but little water where 
 the horse lay . the depth of the stream have be on the side from 
 which they have take off , and the thick black mud lie within a foot 
 of the surface , close to the bank against which Lord Chiltern be 
 prop . " that be the bad one I ever be on , " say Lord Chiltern ; 
 " but I think he be gruelle now . " 
 " be you hurt ? " 
 " well ; - I fancy there be something amiss . I can not   move my arm ; and I

 CHAPTER XXV 
 Mr. Turnbull 's Carriage Stops the way 
 when Phineas get back to London , a day after his time , he find that 
 there be already a great political commotion in the metropolis . 
 he have know that on Easter Monday and Tuesday there be to be

 have be request by the Home Secretary to put aside this intention ,

 CHAPTER XXVI 
 " the First Speech " 
 on the following morning , which be Saturday , Phineas be early at

 who have be take up be take in batch before the magistrate ; 
 but as the soldier in the park have be maltreat , and a

 Street , there be a good deal of strong feeling against the mob , and 
 the magistrate be dispose to be severe . if decent man choose to go 
 out among such companion , and thereby get into trouble , decent man 
 must take the consequence . during the Saturday and Sunday a very 
 strong feeling grow up against Mr. Turnbull . the story of the 
 carriage be tell , and he be declare to be a turbulent demagogue , 
 only desirous of get popularity . and together with this feeling 
 there arise a general verdict of " serve they right " against all who 
 have come into contact with the police in the great Turnbull row ; and 
 thus it come to pass that Mr. Bunce have not be liberate up to

 declare her conviction that Mr. Bunce would be imprison for life . 
 Poor Phineas have an unquiet time with she on the morning of that day . 
 in every ecstasy of her grief she throw herself into his arm , either

 the moment , and express repeatedly an assured conviction that all 
 her child would die of starvation , and that she herself would be 
 pick up under the arch of one of the bridge . Phineas , who be 
 soft - hearted , do what he could to comfort she , and allow himself

 side , there be nothing in the way or arbitrary excess which be too 
 great for they . " this he say to Barrington Erle , who anger he and 
 increase the warmth of his feeling by declare that a little close
