 keep the mob down , the mob will keep we down , " say the Whig private 
 secretary . Phineas have no opportunity of answer this , but declare 
 to himself that Barrington Erle be no more a Liberal at heart than 
 be Mr. Daubeny . " he be bear on that side of the question , and have 
 be receive Whig wage all his life . that be the history of his 
 politic ! " 
 on the Sunday afternoon Phineas go to Lord Brentford 's in Portman 
 Square , intend to say a word or two about Lord Chiltern , and 
 meaning also to induce , if possible , the Cabinet Minister to take 
 part with he against the magistrate , - have a hope also , in which 
 he be not disappointed , that he might find Lady Laura Kennedy with 
 her father . he have come to understand that Lady Laura be not to be 
 visit at her own house on Sundays . so much indeed she have tell 
 he in so many word . but he have come to understand also , without 
 any plain telling , that she rebel in heart against this Sabbath 
 tyranny , - and that she would escape from it when escape be possible . 
 she have now come to talk to her father about her brother , and have 
 bring Violet Effingham with she . they have walk together across 
 the park after church , and intend to walk back again . Mr. Kennedy 
 do not like to have any carriage out on a Sunday , and to this 
 arrangement his wife make no objection . 
 Phineas have receive a letter from the Stamford surgeon , and be able 
 to report favourably of Lord Chiltern . " the man say that he have 
 well not be move for a month , " say Phineas . " but that mean 
 nothing . they always say that . " 
 " will it not be good for he to remain where he be ? " say the Earl . 
 " he have not a soul to speak to , " say Phineas . 
 " I wish I be with he , " say his sister . 
 " that be , of course , out of the question , " say the Earl . " they know 
 he at that inn , and it really seem to I well that he should stay 
 there . I do not think he would be so much at his ease here . " 
 " it must be dreadful for a man to be confine to his room without 
 a creature near he , except the servant , " say Violet . the Earl 
 frown , but say nothing far . they all perceive that as soon as 
 he have learn that there be no real danger as to his son 's life , he 
 be determined that this accident should not work he up to any show 
 of tenderness . " I do so hope he will come up to London , " continue 
 Violet , who be not afraid of the Earl , and be determine not to be 
 put down . 
 " you do not   know what you be talk about , my dear , " say Lord 
 Brentford . 
 after this Phineas find it very difficult to extract any sympathy 
 from the Earl on behalf of the man who have be lock up . he be 
 moody and cross , and could not be induce to talk on the great 
 subject of the day . Violet Effingham declare that she do not care 
 how many bunce be lock up ; nor for how long , - add , however , 
 a wish that Mr. Turnbull himself have be among the number of the 
 prisoner . Lady Laura be somewhat soft than this , and consent to 
 express pity in the case of Mr. Bunce himself ; but Phineas perceive 
 that the pity be award to he and not to the sufferer . the feeling 
 against Mr. Turnbull be at the present moment so strong among all 
 the upper class , that Mr. Bunce and his brother might have be 
 keep in durance for a week without commiseration from they . 
 " it be very hard certainly on a man like Mr. Bunce , " say Lady Laura . 
 " why do not Mr. Bunce stay at home and mind his business ? " say the 
 Earl . 
 Phineas spend the remainder of that day alone , and come to a 
 resolution that on the come occasion he certainly would speak in 
 the House . the debate would be resume on the Monday , and he would 
 rise to his leg on the very first moment that it become possible 
 for he to do so . and he would do nothing towards prepare a 
 speech ; - nothing whatever . on this occasion he would trust entirely 
 to such word as might come to he at the moment ; - ay , and to such 
 thought . he have before burden his memory with preparation , and 
 the very weight of the burden have be too much for his mind . he have 
 fear to trust himself to speak , because he have feel that he be 
 not capable of perform the double labour of say his lesson 
 by heart , and of face the House for the first time . there should 
 be nothing now for he to remember . his thought be full of his 
 subject . he would support Mr. Mildmay 's bill with all his eloquence , 
 but he would implore Mr. Mildmay , and the Home Secretary , and the 
 Government generally , to abstain from animosity against the populace 
 of London , because they desire one special boon which Mr. Mildmay 
 do not think that it be his duty to give they . he hop that idea 
 and word would come to he . idea and word have be free enough 
 with he in the old day of the Dublin debating society . if they 
 fail he now , he must give the thing up , and go back to Mr. Low . 
 on the Monday morning Phineas be for two hour at the police - court 
 in Westminster , and at about one on that day Mr. Bunce be liberate . 
 when he be bring up before the magistrate , Mr. Bunce speak his 
 mind very freely as to the usage he have receive , and declare his 
 intention of bring an action against the sergeant who have detain 
 he . the magistrate , of course , take the part of the police , and 
 declare that , from the evidence of two man who be examine , Bunce 
 have certainly use such violence in the crowd as have justify his 
 arrest . 
 " I use no violence , " say Bunce . 
 " accord to your own showing , you endeavour to make your way up 
 to Mr. Turnbull 's carriage , " say the magistrate . 
 " I be close to the carriage before the police even see I , " say 
 Bunce . 
 " but you try to force your way round to the door . " 
 " I use no force till a man have I by the collar to push I back ; and 
 I be not   violent , not then . I tell he I be do what I have a right

 Phineas , when he leave the indignant Bunce to go among his friend ,

 from the Committee Room , in which , alas ! he take no active part in

 Phineas Finn have sundry gift , a powerful and pleasant voice , which 
 he have learn to modulate , a handsome presence , and a certain

 CHAPTER XXVII 
 Phineas discuss 
 Lady Laura Kennedy hear two account of her friend 's speech , - and 
 both from man who have be present . her husband be in his place , in

 " and you think it be a failure ? " Lady Laura say to her husband . 
 " it certainly be not a success . there be nothing particular about

 after that she get the morning newspaper , and turn with great 
 interest to the report . Phineas Finn have be , as it be , adopt by 
 she as her own political offspring , - or at any rate as her political 
 godchild . she have make promise on his behalf to various personage 
 of high political standing , - to her father , to Mr. Monk , to the Duke 
 of St. Bungay , and even to Mr. Mildmay himself . she have thoroughly 
 intend that Phineas Finn should be a political success from the

 upon it than before . perhaps there be a feeling on her part that 
 have wrong he in one way , she would repay he in another . she 
 have become so eager for his success , - for a while scorn to conceal 
 her feeling , - that her husband have unconsciously begin to entertain

 understanding of the feeling of those with whom they live ; and now , 
 on that very occasion , Lady Laura perceive that her husband do not 
 take in good part her anxiety on behalf of her friend . she see that

 orator have well speak more slowly if he wish to be efficacious 
 either with the House or with the country . 
 " he seem to have be cheer a good deal , " say Lady Laura .

 now , " say Lady Laura . 
 " we may have one less liberal , - or rather less radical , - with few 
 man to support Mr. Turnbull . I do not see what else we can do . they 
 say that there be no less than twenty - seven man on our side of the

 Turnbull can form a Ministry . "

 " and will Mr. Finn lose his seat ? " ask Violet Effingham . " Most 
 probably , " say the Earl . " he only get it by an accident . "

 they speak again . then Lady Laura say something about her brother . 
 " if there be a dissolution , I hope Oswald will stand for Loughton . " 
 Loughton be a borough close to Saulsby , in which , as regard its 
 political interest , Lord Brentford be suppose to have considerable

 seldom go to the House now . " 
 " he be not your uncle , be he ? " 
 " no ; he be papa 's cousin ; but he be ever so much old than 
 papa ; - nearly eighty , I believe . "

 then Lady Laura become very serious . " Oswald would of course have a 
 well right to it than anybody else . " 
 " but would Lord Chiltern go into Parliament ? I have hear he declare 
 that he would not . "

 there be again silence for a few moment , after which Violet

 his not speak well last night . I do not   think that it be very much

 Lord Chiltern should have it if he want it , but I do not   think he 
 will stand in Mr. Finn 's way . " 
 " I be afraid it be out of the question , " say Lady Laura , gravely .

 the use of a little borough of his own , however , be a convenience to

 " those difficult thing have always to be talk of for a long while ,

 " I shall not try , at any rate . " 
 " because you do not   want to go to New Zealand ; - but you might try 
 about Loughton for poor Mr. Finn . " 
 " Violet , " say Lady Laura , after a moment 's pause ; - and she speak 
 sharply ; " Violet , I believe you be in love with Mr. Finn . "

 " I never make such an accusation against you before , or against

 " why should not   I be in love with he , if I like ? " 
 " I say nothing about that ; - only he have not get a penny . " 
 " but I have , my dear . " 
 " and I doubt whether you have any reason for suppose that he be in

 " that be my affair also . " 
 Lady Laura shrug her shoulder . " of course it be ; and if you tell 
 I to hold my tongue , of course I will do so . if you ask I whether I

 " I do not   tell you to hold your tongue , and I do not   ask you what you 
 think about the match . you be quite welcome to talk as much about I

 " I should not   talk to anybody but yourself . " 
 " I be grow to be quite indifferent as to what people say . Lady 
 Baldock ask I the other day whether I be go to throw myself

 " no ! " 
 " indeed she do . " 
 " and what do you answer ? "

 thing go . his manner be perfect ; - not Chesterfieldian , and yet

 should tell he to kiss I and be my brother . as to Mr. Phineas

 " what you say be very odd . "

 " simply because mine be the same . " 
 " be they the same ? I once think , Laura , that you do love 
 he ; - that you mean to be his wife . " 
 Lady Laura sit for a while without make any reply to this . she

 in the meantime Phineas be very wretched at home . when he reach

 come upon he , as he look round they , an idea that he have no

 go about the world under false pretence , and that he would never 
 set himself aright , even unto himself , till he have go through some 
 terrible act of humiliation . he have be a cheat even to Mr. Quintus 
 Slide of the Banner , in accept an invitation to come among 
 they . he have be a cheat to Lady Laura , in that he have induce 
 she to think that he be fit to live with she . he be a cheat to

 horse , and pretend to be a proper associate for a man of fortune . 
 why , - what be his income ? what his birth ? what his proper position ? 
 and now he have get the reward which all cheat deserve . then he go

 plight his troth to Mary , and then work like a slave under Mr. 
 Low 's auspex , - he would not have be a cheat . 
 it seem to he that he have hardly be asleep when the girl 
 come into his room in the morning . " Sir , " say she , " there be that 
 gentleman there . " 
 " what gentleman ? " 
 " the old gentleman . " 
 then Phineas know that Mr. Clarkson be in his sitting - room , and

 Nay , - Phineas be pretty sure that Mr. Clarkson would come into the

 CHAPTER XXIX 
 a Cabinet Meeting 
 and now will the Muses assist I while I sing an altogether new song ? 
 on the Tuesday the Cabinet meet at the First Lord 's official residence 
 in Downing Street , and I will attempt to describe what , accord to 
 the bewildered brain of a poor fictionist , be say or might have 
 be say , what be do or might have be do , on so august an 
 occasion . 
 the poor fictionist very frequently find himself to have be wrong 
 in his description of thing in general , and be tell so , roughly by 
 the critic , and tenderly by the friend of his bosom . he be move 
 to tell of thing of which he omit to learn the nature before he 
 tell of they - as should be do by a strictly honest fictionist . he 
 catch salmon in October ; or shoot his partridge in March . his 
 dahlia bloom in June , and his bird sing in the autumn . he open the 
 opera - house before Easter , and make Parliament sit on a Wednesday 
 evening . and then those terrible mesh of the Law ! how be a 
 fictionist , in these excited day , to create the needed bite 
 interest without legal difficulty ; and how again be he to steer his 
 little bark clear of so many rock , - when the rock and the shoal 
 have be purposely arrange to make the taking of a pilot on board a 
 necessity ? as to those law mesh , a benevolent pilot will , indeed , 
 now and again give a poor fictionist a help hand , - not use , 
 however , generally , with much discretion . but from whom be any 
 assistance to come in the august matter of a Cabinet assembly ? there 
 can be no such assistance . no man can tell aught but they who will 
 tell nothing . but then , again , there be this safety , that let the 
 story be ever so mistold , - let the fiction be ever so far remove 
 from the truth , no critic short of a Cabinet Minister himself can 
 convict the narrator of error . 
 it be a large dingy room , cover with a Turkey carpet , and 
 contain a dark polished mahogany dinner - table , on very heavy 
 carve leg , which an old messenger be prepare at two o'clock in 
 the day for the use of her Majesty 's Ministers . the table would have 
 be large enough for fourteen guest , and along the side far 
 from the fire , there be place some six heavy chair , good 
 comfortable chair , stuff at the back as well as the seat , - but on 
 the side nearer to the fire the chair be place irregularly ; and 
 there be four armchair , - two on one side and two on the other . 
 there be four window to the room , which look on to St. James 's 
 Park , and the curtain of the window be dark and heavy , - as become 
 the gravity of the purpose to which that chamber be appropriate . 
 in old day it have be the dining - room of one Prime Minister after 
 another . to Pitt it have be the abode of his own familiar prandial 
 Penates , and Lord Liverpool have be dull there among his dull 
 friend for long year after year . the Ministers of the present day 
 find it more convenient to live in private home , and , indeed , not 
 unfrequently carry their Cabinets with they . but , under Mr. Mildmay 's 
 rule , the meeting be generally hold in the old room at the 
 official residence . Thrice do the aged messenger move each armchair , 
 now a little this way and now a little that , and then look at they as 
 though something of the tendency of the come meeting might depend 
 on the comfort of its lead member . if Mr. Mildmay should find 
 himself to be quite comfortable , so that he could hear what be say 
 without a struggle to his ear , and see his colleague ' face clearly , 
 and feel the fire without burn his shin , it might be possible 
 that he would not insist upon resign . if this be so , how 
 important be the work now confide to the hand of that aged 
 messenger ! when his anxious eye have glance round the room some 
 half a dozen time , when he have touch each curtain , lay his 
 hand upon every chair , and dust certain paper which lie upon a 
 side - table , - and which have be lie there for two year , and at 
 which no one ever look or would look , - he gently creep away and 
 ensconce himself in an easy chair not far from the door of the 
 chamber . for it might be necessary to stop the attempt of a rash 
 intruder on those secret counsel . 
 very shortly there be hear the ring of various voice in the 
 passage , - the voice of man speak pleasantly , the voice of man 
 with whom it seem , from their tone , that thing be do well 
 in the world . and then a cluster of four or five gentleman enter 
 the room . at first sight they seem to be as ordinary gentleman as 
 you shall meet anywhere about Pall Mall on an afternoon . there be 
 nothing about their outward appearance of the august wiggery of 
 statecraft , nothing of the ponderous dignity of ministerial position . 
 that little man in the square - cut coat , - we may almost call it a 
 shooting - coat , - swing an umbrella and wear no glove , be no 
 less a person than the Lord Chancellor , - Lord Weazeling , - who make 
 a hundred thousand pound as Attorney - General , and be suppose 
 to be the good lawyer of his age . he be fifty , but he look to 
 be hardly over forty , and one might take he to be , from his 
 appearance , - perhaps a clerk in the War Office , well - to - do , and 
 popular among his brother - clerk . immediately with he be Sir Harry 
 Coldfoot , also a lawyer by profession , though he have never practise . 
 he have be in the House for nearly thirty year , and be now at the 
 Home Office . he be a stout , healthy , grey - haired gentleman , who 
 certainly do not wear the care of office on his face . perhaps , 
 however , no minister get more bully than he by the press , and man 
 say that he will be very willing to give up to some political enemy 
 the control of the police , and the onerous duty of judge in all 
 criminal appeal . behind these come our friend Mr. Monk , young Lord 
 Cantrip from the colony next door , than whom no smart young peer 
 now do honour to our hereditary legislature , and Sir Marmaduke 
 Morecombe , the Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster . why Sir 
 Marmaduke have always be place in Mr. Mildmay 's Cabinets nobody 
 ever know . as Chancellor of the Duchy he have nothing to do , - and be 
 there anything , he would not do it . he rarely speak in the House , 
 and then do not speak well . he be a handsome man , or would be but 
 for an assumption of grandeur in the carriage of his eye , give to

 be in the Guards when young , and have be in Parliament since he

 within two minute afterwards the Duke follow , with Plantagenet 
 Palliser . the Duke , as all the world know , be the Duke of St. 
 Bungay , the very front and head of the aristocratic old whig of the 
 country , - a man who have be thrice speak of as Prime Minister , and

 Cabinets for the last five - and - thirty year , and be even now not an

 then come Viscount Thrift by himself ; - the First Lord of the

 close behind he there be a ruck of Ministers , with the 
 much - honour grey - haired old Premier in the midst of they . there be

 Mildmay would fall , - to be wear , however , quite otherwise than Mr. 
 Mildmay have wear it . for Mr. Gresham be a man with no feeling

 CHAPTER XXXI 
 Finn for Loughton

 on this evening , the evening subsequent to that on which Mr. Kennedy

 " Lady Laura ! "

 of course Phineas be persuade before he leave Mr. Kennedy 's room .

 a few day after this Phineas be very much surprised at a visit

 the strange visitor be Miss Aspasia Fitzgibbon . " you will be very much

 CHAPTER XXXII 
 Lady Laura Kennedy 's Headache 
 Phineas go down to Loughlinter early in July , take Loughton in 
 his way . he stay there one night at the inn , and be introduce to 
 sundry influential inhabitant of the borough by Mr. Grating , the 
 ironmonger , who be know by those who know Loughton to be a very 
 strong supporter of the Earl 's interest . Mr. Grating and about half a 
 dozen other of the tradesman of the town come to the inn , and meet 
 Phineas in the parlour . he tell they he be a good sound liberal and 
 a supporter of Mr. Mildmay 's Government , of which their neighbour the 
 Earl be so conspicuous an ornament . this be almost all that be 
 say about the Earl out loud ; but each individual man of Loughton 
 then present take an opportunity during the meeting of whispering 
 into Mr. Finn 's ear a word or two to show that he also be admit 
 to the secret council of the borough , - that he too could see the 
 inside of the arrangement . " of course we must support the Earl , " one 
 say . " never mind what you hear about a Tory candidate , Mr. Finn , " 
 whisper a second ; " the Earl can do what he please here . " and it 
 seem to Phineas that it be think by they all to be rather a fine 
 thing to be thus hold in the hand by an english nobleman . Phineas 
 could not but reflect much upon this as he lie in his bed at the 
 Loughton inn . the great political question on which the political 
 world be engross up in London be the enfranchisement of 
 Englishmen , - of Englishmen down to the rank of artisan and 
 labourer ; - and yet when he find himself in contact with individual 
 Englishmen , with man even very much above the artisan and the 
 labourer , he find that they rather like be bind hand and foot , 
 and be keep as tool in the political pocket of a rich man . 
 every one of those Loughton tradesman be proud of his own personal 
 subjection to the Earl ! 
 from Loughton he go to Loughlinter , have promise to be back in 
 the borough for the election . Mr. Grating would propose he , and he 
 be to be second by Mr. Shortribs , the butcher and grazi . Mention 
 have be make of a conservative candidate , and Mr. Shortribs have 
 seem to think that a good stand - up fight upon english principle , 
 with a clear understanding , of course , that victory should prevail 
 on the liberal side , would be a good thing for the borough . but the 
 Earl 's man of business see Phineas on the morning of his departure , 
 and tell he not to regard Mr. Shortribs . " they would all like it , " say 
 the man of business ; " and I daresay they will have enough of it when 
 this Reform Bill be pass ; but at present no one will be fool enough 
 to come and spend his money here . we have they all in hand too well 
 for that , Mr. Finn ! " 
 he find the great house at Loughlinter nearly empty . Mr. Kennedy 's 
 mother be there , and Lord Brentford be there , and Lord Brentford 's 
 private secretary , and Mr. Kennedy 's private secretary . at present 
 that be the entire party . Lady Baldock be expect there , with 
 her daughter and Violet Effingham ; but , as well as Phineas could 
 learn , they would not be at Loughlinter until after he have leave it . 
 there have come up lately a rumour that there would be an autumn 
 session , - that the Houses would sit through October and a part of 
 November , in order that Mr. Mildmay might try the feeling of the new 
 Parliament . if this be to be so , Phineas have resolve that , in the 
 event of his election at Loughton , he would not return to Ireland 
 till after this autumn session should be over . he give an account to 
 the Earl , in the presence of the Earl 's son - in - law , of what have take 
 place at Loughton , and the Earl express himself as satisfied . it 
 be manifestly a great satisfaction to Lord Brentford that he should 
 still have a borough in his pocket , and the more so because there 
 be so very few nobleman leave who have such property belong to 
 they . he be very careful in his speech , never say in so many 
 word that the privilege of return a member be his own ; but his 
 meaning be not the less clear . 
 those be dreary day at Loughlinter . there be fish , - if Phineas 
 choose to fish ; and he be tell that he could shoot a deer if he be 
 minded to go out alone . but it seem as though it be the intention 
 of the host that his guest should spend their time profitably . Mr. 
 Kennedy himself be shut up with book and paper all the morning , 
 and always take up a book after dinner . the Earl also would read a 
 little , - and then would sleep a good deal . Old Mrs. Kennedy sleep 
 also , and Lady Laura look as though she would like to sleep if 
 it be not that her husband 's eye be upon she . as it be , she 
 administer tea , Mr. Kennedy not like the practice of have it 
 hand round by a servant when none be there but member of the 
 family circle , and she read novel . Phineas get hold of a stiff bit 
 of read for himself , and try to utilise his time . he take Alison 
 in hand , and work his way gallantly through a couple of volume . 
 but even he , more than once or twice , find himself on the very verge 
 of slumber . then he would wake up and try to think about thing . why 
 be he , Phineas Finn , an Irishman from Killaloe , live in that great 
 house of Loughlinter as though he be one of the family , strive to 
 kill the hour , and feel that he be in some way subject to the 
 dominion of his host ? would it not be well for he to get up and go 
 away ? in his heart of heart he do not like Mr. Kennedy , though he 
 believe he to be a good man . and of what service to he be it to 
 like Lady Laura , now that Lady Laura be a possession in the hand of 
 Mr. Kennedy ? then he would tell himself that he owe his position in 
 the world entirely to Lady Laura , and that he be ungrateful to feel 
 himself ever dull in her society . and , moreover , there be something 
 to be do in the world beyond make love and be merry . Mr. 
 Kennedy could occupy himself with a blue book for hour together 
 without wince . so Phineas go to work again with his Alison , and 
 read away till he nod . 
 in those day he often wander up and down the Linter and across the 
 moor to the Linn , and so down to the lake . he would take a book with 
 he , and would seat himself down on spot which he love , and would 
 pretend to read ; - but I do not think that he get much advantage 
 from his book . he be think of his life , and try to calculate 
 whether the wonderful success which he have achieve would ever be of 
 permanent value to he . would he be near to earn his bread when 
 he should be member for Loughton than he have be when he be member 
 for Loughshane ? or be there before he any slight probability 
 that he would ever earn his bread ? and then he think of Violet 
 Effingham , and be angry with himself for remember at that moment 
 that Violet Effingham be the mistress of a large fortune . 
 once before when he be sit beside the Linter he have make up his 
 mind to declare his passion to Lady Laura ; - and he have do so on the 
 very spot . now , within a twelvemonth of that time , he make up his 
 mind on the same spot to declare his passion to Miss Effingham , and 
 he think his good mode of carry his suit would be to secure the 
 assistance of Lady Laura . Lady Laura , no doubt , have be very anxious 
 that her brother should marry Violet ; but Lord Chiltern , as Phineas 
 know , have ask for Violet 's hand twice in vain ; and , moreover , 
 Chiltern himself have declare to Phineas that he would never ask 
 for it again . Lady Laura , who be always reasonable , would surely 
 perceive that there be no hope of success for her brother . that 
 Chiltern would quarrel with he , - would quarrel with he to the 
 knife , - he do not doubt ; but he feel that no fear of such a quarrel 
 as that should deter he . he love Violet Effingham , and he must 
 indeed be pusillanimous if , love she as he do , he be deter 
 from express his love from any fear of a suitor whom she do not 
 favour . he would not willingly be untrue to his friendship for Lady 
 Laura 's brother . have there be a chance for Lord Chiltern he would 
 have abstain from put himself forward . but what be the use 
 of his abstaining , when by do so he could in no wise benefit 
 his friend , - when the result of his do so would be that some 
 interloper would come in and carry off the prize ? he would explain 
 all this to Lady Laura , and , if the prize would be kind to he , he 
 would disregard the anger of Lord Chiltern , even though it might be 
 anger to the knife . 
 as he be think of all this Lady Laura stand before he where he 
 be sit at the top of the fall . at this moment he remember 
 well all the circumstance of the scene when he have be there with 
 she at his last visit to Loughlinter . how thing have change since 
 then ! then he have love Lady Laura with all his heart , and he have now 
 already bring himself to regard she as a discreet matron whom to 
 love would be almost as unreasonable as though he be to entertain 
 a passion for the Lord Chancellor . the reader will understand how 
 thorough have be the cure effect by Lady Laura 's marriage and the 
 interval of a few month , when the swain be already prepared to make 
 this lady the depositary of his confidence in another matter of love . 
 " you be often here , I suppose ? " say Lady Laura , look down upon 
 he as he sit upon the rock . 
 " well ; - yes ; not very often ; I come here sometimes because the view 
 down upon the lake be so fine . " 
 " it be the prettiest spot about the place . I hardly ever get here 
 now . indeed this be only the second time that I have be up since 
 we have be at home , and then I come to bring papa here . " there be 
 a little wooden seat near to the rock upon which Phineas have be 
 lie , and upon this Lady Laura sit down . Phineas , with his eye 
 turn upon the lake , be consider how he might introduce the 
 subject of his love for Violet Effingham ; but he do not find the 
 matter very easy . he have just resolve to begin by say that Violet 
 would certainly never accept Lord Chiltern , when Lady Laura speak a

 in the countenance of Mr. Kennedy , as he approach close , there be

 CHAPTER XXXIII 
 Mr. Slide 's Grievance 
 our hero be elect member for Loughton without any trouble to he 
 or , as far as he could see , to any one else . he make one speech from 
 a small raise booth that be call a platform , and that be all 
 that he be call upon to do . Mr. Grating make a speech in propose 
 he , and Mr. Shortribs another in second he ; and these be all 
 the speech that be require . the thing seem to be so very easy 
 that he be afterwards almost offend when he be tell that the bill 
 for so insignificant a piece of work come to L247 13s . 9d . he have 
 see no occasion for spend even the odd forty - seven pound . but 
 then he be member for Loughton ; and as he pass the evening alone 
 at the inn , have din in company with Messrs. Grating , Shortribs , 
 and sundry other influential elector , he begin to reflect that , 
 after all , it be not so very great a thing to be a member of 
 Parliament . it almost seem that that which have come to he so 
 easily could not be of much value . 
 on the follow day he go to the castle , and be there when the 
 Earl arrive . they two be alone together , and the Earl be very 
 kind to he . " so you have no opponent after all , " say the great man 
 of Loughton , with a slight smile . 
 " not the ghost of another candidate . " 
 " I do not think there would be . they have try it once or twice and 
 have always fail . there be only one or two in the place who like 
 to go one way just because their neighbour go the other . but , in 
 truth , there be no conservative feeling in the place ! " 
 Phineas , although he be at the present moment the member for 
 Loughton himself , could not but enjoy the joke of this . could there 
 be any liberal feeling in such a place , or , indeed , any political 
 feeling whatsoever ? would not Messrs. Grating and Shortribs have do 
 just the same have it happen that Lord Brentford have be a tory 
 peer ? " they all seem to be very obliging , " say Phineas , in answer 
 to the Earl . 
 " yes , they be . there be not   a house in the town , you know , let 
 for long than seven year , and most of they merely from year to 
 year . and , do you know , I have not   a farmer on the property with a 
 lease , - not one ; and they do not   want lease . they know they be safe . 
 but I do like the people round I to be of the same way of thinking 
 as myself about politic . " 
 on the second day after dinner , - the last evening of Finn 's visit to 
 Saulsby , - the Earl fall suddenly into a confidential conversation 
 about his daughter and his son , and about Violet Effingham . so 
 sudden , indeed , and so confidential be the conversation , that

 " he be so hard and dry , and what I call exact . that be just the

 always have her own way in everything , and I always find her fit 
 to have it . I do not understand why her husband should treat she

 " perhaps it be the temper of the man . " 
 " Temper , yes ; but what a bad prospect be that for she ! and she , too , 
 have a temper , and so he will find if he try she too far . I can not

 " yes , indeed ; it be sad for she , poor girl ; and very sad for I too .

 from she ! it seem that she have be take as much away from I as 
 though her husband live in China . I have lose they both now ! " 
 " I hope not , my lord . " 
 " I say I have . as to Chiltern , I can perceive that he become more 
 and more indifferent to I every day . he think of I only as a man 
 in his way who must die some day and may die soon . " 
 " you wrong he , Lord Brentford . " 
 " I do not wrong he at all . why have he answer every offer I have 
 make he with so much insolence as to make it impossible for I to 
 put myself into further communion with he ? " 
 " he think that you have wrong he . " 
 " yes ; - because I have be unable to shut my eye to his mode of 
 live . I be to go on pay his debt , and take no other notice 
 whatsoever of his conduct ! " 
 " I do not think he be in debt now . " 
 " because his sister the other day spend every shilling of her fortune 
 in pay they . she give he L40,000 ! do you think she would have 
 marry Kennedy but for that ? I do not . I could not prevent she . I have 
 say that I would not cripple my remain year of life by raise 
 the money , and I could not go back from my word . "

 " it would do no good now . she have marry Mr. Kennedy , and the money 
 be nothing to she or to he . chiltern might have put thing right by 
 marry Miss Effingham if he please . " 
 " I think he do his good there . " 
 " no ; - he do his bad . he ask she to be his wife as a man ask for 
 a railway - ticket or a pair of glove , which he buy with a price ;

 believe he even really want to marry she . I suppose he have some 
 disreputable connection to prevent it . " 
 " nothing of the kind . he would marry she to - morrow if he could . my 
 belief be that Miss Effingham be sincere in refuse he . " 
 " I do not   doubt her sincerity . " 
 " and that she will never change . " 
 " ah , well ; I do not   agree with you , and I daresay I know they both 
 well than you do . but everything go against I . I have set my 
 heart upon it , and therefore of course I shall be disappoint . what 
 be he go to do this autumn ? " 
 " he be yacht now . " 
 " and who be with he ? " 
 " I think the boat belong to Captain Colepepper . " 
 " the great blackguard in all England ! a man who shoot pigeon and 
 rid steeple - chase ! and the bad of Chiltern be this , that even if

 would go on just the same because he think it a fine thing not to

 continue the Earl , " while Laura could always watch he ; because , in 
 his way , he be fond of his sister . but that be all over now . she

 Phineas have feel that the Earl have put he down rather sharply when

 which he have just receive from she . " they be to be at Loughlinter

 " Lady Baldock and all ? " 
 " well , yes ; Lady Baldock and all . I be not very fond of Lady Baldock ,

 have Violet . she be more like a child of my own now than anybody 
 else . I shall not see she all the autumn afterwards . I can not stand 
 Loughlinter . " 
 " it will be well when the house be full . " 
 " you will be there , I suppose ? " 
 " well , no ; I think not , " say Phineas . 
 " you have have enough of it , have you ? " Phineas make no reply to this , 
 but smile slightly . " by Jove , I do not   wonder at it , " say the Earl . 
 Phineas , who would have give all he have in the world to be stay

 suppose you be ask ? " say the Earl . 
 " oh , yes , I be ask . nothing can be kind than they be . " 
 " Kennedy tell I that you be come as a matter of course . " 
 " I explain to he after that , " say Phineas , " that I should not 
 return . I shall go over to Ireland . I have a deal of hard reading to 
 do , and I can get through it there without interruption . " 
 he go up from Saulsby to London on that day , and find himself 
 quite alone in Mrs. Bunce 's lodging . I mean not only that he be 
 alone at his lodging , but he be alone at his club , and alone in the 
 street . July be not quite over , and yet all the bird of passage 
 have migrate . Mr. Mildmay , by his short session , have half ruin the 
 London tradesman , and have change the summer mode of life of all 
 those who account themselves to be anybody . Phineas , as he sit alone 
 in his room , feel himself to be nobody . he have tell the Earl that 
 he be go to Ireland , and to Ireland he must go ; - because he have 
 nothing else to do . he have be ask indeed to join one or two 
 party in their autumn plan . Mr. Monk have want he to go to the 
 Pyrenees , and Lord Chiltern have suggest that he should join the 
 yacht ; - but neither plan suit he . it would have suit he to be 
 at Loughlinter with Violet Effingham , but Loughlinter be a barred 
 house to he . his old friend , Lady Laura , have tell he not to come 
 thither , explain , with sufficient clearness , her reason for 
 exclude he from the number of her husband 's guest . as he think

 Banner of the People Office ,

 game , I think . "

 CHAPTER XXXIV 
 be he honest ?

 CHAPTER XXXV 
 Mr. Monk upon Reform 
 Phineas Finn go to Ireland immediately after his return from 
 Saulsby , have say nothing far to Violet Effingham , and have

 expression of love be always weak and vapid , - and deter also

 undoubtedly reply by a refusal . fifty time he ride again in his

 that the syren 's answer to he , - her no , no , no , - have be , of all

 but the doubt make his sojourn in Ireland very wearisome to he . 
 and there be other matter which tend also to his discomfort , 
 though he be not leave even at this period of his life without a 
 continuation of success which seem to be very wonderful . and , 
 first , I will say a word of his discomfort . he hear not a line from

 quietly severe manner with which , perhaps , most of we have be make 
 acquaint at some period of our life . " I think , Phineas , " say his

 at present , and probably you may not see she while you remain with 
 we . " " what be all that about ? " Phineas have demand , - understand 
 the whole matter thoroughly . then his sister have demurely refuse to

 person , - and that he could , without any real faithlessness , be very 
 much in love with Violet Effingham in his position of man of fashion

 and then there come a wonderful piece of luck in his way . there

 later in life a man work , the more likely he be to live . " Phineas ,

 he owe some l500 ; and the remainder he would , of course , invest . 
 there have be some talk of an autumnal session , but Mr. Mildmay 's 
 decision have at last be against it . who can not understand that such 
 would be the decision of any Minister to whom be leave the slight 
 fraction of free will in the matter ? why should any Minister court

 and incur the odium of summon all his friend from their rest ? 
 in the midst of the doubt as to the new and old Ministry , when 
 the political needle be vacillate so tremulously on its pivot , 
 point now to one set of man as the come government and then to 
 another , vague suggestion as to an autumn session might be useful . 
 and they be throw out in all good faith . Mr. Mildmay , when he 
 speak on the subject to the Duke , be earnest in think that the 
 question of Reform should not be postpone even for six month . 
 " do not   pledge yourself , " say the Duke ; - and Mr. Mildmay do not 
 pledge himself . afterwards , when Mr. Mildmay find that he be 
 once more assuredly Prime Minister , he change his mind , and feel 
 himself to be under a fresh obligation to the Duke . Lord de Terrier 
 have altogether fail , and the country might very well wait till 
 February . the country do wait till February , somewhat to the 
 disappointment of Phineas Finn , who have become tired of blue - book 
 at Killaloe . the difference between his english life and his life at 
 home be so great , that it be hardly possible that he should not 
 become weary of the latter . he do become weary of it , but strove 
 gallantly to hide his weariness from his father and mother . 
 at this time the world be talk much about Reform , though Mr. 
 Mildmay have become placidly patient . the feeling be grow , and 
 Mr. Turnbull , with his friend , be do all he could to make it 
 grow fast . there be a certain amount of excitement on the subject ; 
 but the excitement have grow downward , from the leader to the 
 people , - from the self - institute leader of popular politic down , 
 by mean of the press , to the rank of work man , instead of 
 grow upwards , from the dissatisfaction of the mass , till it 
 express itself by this mouthpiece and that , choose by the people 
 themselves . there be no strong throb through the country , make 
 man feel that safety be to be have by reform , and could not be have 
 without Reform . but there be an understanding that the press and the 
 orator be too strong to be ignore , and that some new measure of 
 reform must be concede to they . the soon the concession be make , 
 the less it might be necessary to concede . and all man of all party 
 be agree on this point . that Reform be in itself odious to many 
 of those who speak of it freely , who offer themselves willingly to 
 be its promoter , be acknowledge . it be not only odious to Lord de 
 Terrier and to most of those who work with he , but be equally so 
 to many of Mr. Mildmay 's most constant supporter . the Duke have no 
 wish for Reform . indeed it be hard to suppose that such a Duke can 
 wish for any change in a state of thing that must seem to he to be 
 so salutary . Workmen be get full wage . farmer be pay 
 their rent . capitalist by the dozen be create capitalist by the 
 hundred . nothing be wrong in the country , but the over - dominant 
 spirit of speculative commerce ; - and there be nothing in Reform to 
 check that . why should the Duke want reform ? as for such man as Lord 
 Brentford , Sir Harry Coldfoot , Lord Plinlimmon , and Mr. Legge Wilson , 
 it be know to all man that they advocate Reform as we all of we 
 advocate doctor . some amount of doctoring be necessary for we . we 
 may hardly hope to avoid it . but let we have as little of the doctor 
 as possible . Mr. Turnbull , and the cheap press , and the rise spirit 
 of the loud among the people , make it manifest that something must 
 be concede . let we be generous in our concession . that be now the 
 doctrine of many , - perhaps of most of the lead politician of the 
 day . let we be generous . let we at any rate seem to be generous . let 
 we give with an open hand , - but still with a hand which , though open , 
 shall not bestow too much . the coach must be allow to run down the 
 hill . indeed , unless the coach go on run no journey will be 
 make . but let we have the drag on both the hind wheel . and we must 
 remember that coach run down hill without drag be apt to come 
 to serious misfortune . 
 but there be man , even in the Cabinet , who have other idea of 
 public service than that of drag the wheel of the coach . Mr. 
 Gresham be in earnest . Plantagenet Palliser be in earnest . that 
 exceedingly intelligent young nobleman Lord Cantrip be in earnest . 
 Mr. Mildmay throw , perhaps , as much of earnestness into the matter 
 as be compatible with his age and his full appreciation of the 
 manner in which the present cry for Reform have be arouse . he be 
 thoroughly honest , thoroughly patriotic , and thoroughly ambitious 
 that he should be write of hereafter as one who to the end of a 
 long life have work sedulously for the welfare of the people ; - but 
 he disbelieve in Mr. Turnbull , and in the bottom of his heart 
 indulge an aristocratic contempt for the penny press . and there be 
 no man in England more in earnest , more truly desirous of reform , 
 than Mr. Monk . it be his great political idea that political 
 advantage should be extend to the people , whether the people 
 clamour for they or do not clamour for they , - even whether they 
 desire they or do not desire they . " you do not ask a child whether 
 he would like to learn his lesson , " he would say . " at any rate , you 
 do not wait till he cry for his book . " when , therefore , man say to 
 he that there be no earnestness in the cry for Reform , that the cry 
 be a false cry , get up for factious purpose by interested person , 
 he would reply that the thing to be do should not be do in 
 obedience to any cry , but because it be demand by justice , and be 
 a debt due to the people . 
 our hero in the autumn have write to Mr. Monk on the politic of the 
 moment , and the following have be Mr. Monk 's reply : - 
 Longroyston , October 12 , 186 - . 
 MY DEAR FINN , 
 I be stay here with the Duke and Duchess of St. 
 Bungay . the house be very full , and Mr. Mildmay be 
 here last week ; but as I do not   shoot , and can not   play 
 billiard , and have no taste for charade , I be become 
 tired of the gaiety , and shall leave they to - morrow . 
 of course you know that we be not to have the autumn 
 session . I think that Mr. Mildmay be right . could we have 
 be sure of pass our measure , it would have be very 
 well ; but we could not have be sure , and failure with 
 our bill in a session convene for the express purpose of 
 pass it would have injure the cause greatly . we could 
 hardly have go on with it again in the spring . indeed , 
 we must have resign . and though I may truly say that I 
 would as lief have a good measure from Lord de Terrier 
 as from Mr. Mildmay , and that I be indifferent to my own 
 present personal position , still I think that we should 
 endeavour to keep our seat as long as we honestly 
 believe ourselves to be more capable of pass a good 
 measure than be our opponent . 
 I be astonish by the difference of opinion which 
 exist about reform , - not only as to the difference in 
 the extent and exact tendency of the measure that be 
 need , - but that there should be such a divergence of 
 idea as to the grand thing to be do and the grand 
 reason for do it . we be all agree that we want 
 reform in order that the House of Commons may be return 
 by a large proportion of the people than be at present 
 employ upon that work , and that each member when 
 return should represent a somewhat more equal section 
 of the whole constituency of the country than our 
 member generally do at present . all man confess that a 
 L50 county franchise must be too high , and that a borough 
 with less than two hundred register voter must be 
 wrong . but it seem to I that but few among we perceive , 
 or at any rate acknowledge , the real reason for change 
 these thing and reform what be wrong without delay . 
 one great authority tell we the other day that the sole 
 object of legislation on this subject should be to get 
 together the well possible 658 member of Parliament . 
 that to I would be a most repulsive idea if it be 
 not that by its very vagueness it become inoperative . 
 who shall say what be good ; or what characteristic 
 constitute excellence in a member of Parliament ? if 
 the gentleman mean excellence in general wisdom , or 
 in statecraft , or in skill in talking , or in private 
 character , or even excellence in patriotism , then I say 
 that he be utterly wrong , and have never touch with 
 his intellect the true theory of representation . one 
 only excellence may be acknowledge , and that be the 
 excellence of likeness . as a portrait should be like the 
 person portray , so should a representative House be 
 like the people whom it represent . nor in arrange 
 a franchise do it seem to I that we have a right 
 to regard any other view . if a country be unfit for 
 representative government , - and it may be that there be 
 still people unable to use properly that great of 
 all blessing , - the question as to what state policy may 
 be good for they be a different question . but if we do 
 have representation , let the representative assembly be 
 like the people , whatever else may be its virtue , - and 
 whatever else its vice . 
 another great authority have tell we that our House of 
 Commons should be the mirror of the people . I say , not 
 its mirror , but its miniature . and let the artist be 
 careful to put in every line of the expression of that 
 ever - move face . to do this be a great work , and the 
 artist must know his trade well . in America the work have 
 be do with so coarse a hand that nothing be show 
 in the picture but the broad , plain , unspeaking outline 
 of the face . as you look from the represent to the 
 representation you can not but acknowledge the likeness ; 
  - but there be in that portrait more of the body than of 
 the mind . the true portrait should represent more than 
 the body . with we , hitherto , there have be snatch 
 of the countenance of the nation which have be 
 inimitable , - a turn of the eye here and a curl of the lip 
 there , which have seem to denote a power almost divine . 
 there have be marvel on the canvas so beautiful that 
 one approach the work of remodelle it with awe . 
 but not only be the picture imperfect , - a thing of 
 snatch , - but with year it become less and still less 
 like its original . 
 the necessity for remodelle it be imperative , and we 
 shall be coward if we decline the work . but let we be 
 specially careful to retain as much as possible of those 
 line which we all acknowledge to be so faithfully 
 representative of our nation . to give to a bare numerical 
 majority of the people that power which the numerical 
 majority have in the United States , would not be to 
 achieve representation . the nation as it now exist would 
 not be know by such a portrait ; - but neither can it 
 now be know by that which exist . it seem to I that 
 they who be adverse to change , look back with an 
 unmeasured respect on what our old Parliaments have do 
 for we , ignore the majestic growth of the english people , 
 and forget the present in their worship of the past . they 
 think that we must be what we be , - at any rate , what 
 we be thirty year since . they have not , perhaps , go 
 into the house of artisan , or , if there , they have not 
 look into the breast of the man . with population vice 
 have increase , and these politician , with ear but 
 no eye , hear of drunkenness and sin and ignorance . 
 and then they declare to themselves that this wicked , 
 half - barbarous , idle people should be control and not 
 represent . a wicked , half - barbarous , idle people may be 
 control ; - but not a people thoughtful , educated , and 
 industrious . we must look to it that we do not endeavour 
 to carry our control beyond the wickedness and the 
 barbarity , and that we be ready to submit to control from 
 thoughtfulness and industry . 
 I hope we shall find you help at the good work early 
 in the spring . 
 your , always faithfully , 
 JOSHUA MONK . 
 Phineas be up in London before the end of January , but do not find 
 there many of those whom he wish to see . Mr. Low be there , and to 
 he he show Mr. Monk 's letter , think that it must be convince 
 even to Mr. Low . this he do in Mrs. Low 's drawing - room , know that 
 Mrs. Low would also condescend to discuss politic on an occasion . 
 he have din with they , and they have be glad to see he , and Mrs. 
 Low have be less severe than hitherto against the great sin of her 
 husband 's late pupil . she have condescend to congratulate he on 
 become member for an English borough instead of an irish one , and 
 have ask he question about Saulsby Castle . but , nevertheless , Mr. 
 Monk 's letter be not receive with that respectful admiration which 
 Phineas think that it deserve . Phineas , foolishly , have read it 
 out loud , so that the attack come upon he simultaneously from the 
 husband and from the wife . 
 " it be just the usual claptrap , " say Mr. Low , " only put into 
 language somewhat more grandiloquent than usual . " 
 " Claptrap ! " say Phineas . 
 " it be what I call downright radical nonsense , " say Mrs. Low , nod 
 her head energetically . " portrait indeed ! why should we want to have 
 a portrait of ignorance and ugliness ? what we all want be to have 
 thing quiet and orderly . " 
 " then you would well have a paternal government at once , " say Phineas . 
 " just so , " say Mr. Low , - " only that what you call a paternal 
 government be not always quiet and orderly . national order I take to 
 be submission to the law . I should not think it quiet and orderly if 
 I be send to Cayenne without be bring before a jury . " 
 " but such a man as you would not be send to Cayenne , " say Phineas , 
 " my next - door neighbour might be , - which would be almost as bad . let 
 he be send to Cayenne if he deserve it , but let a jury say that 
 he have deserve it . my idea of government be this , - that we want 
 to be govern by law and not by caprice , and that we must have a 
 legislature to make our law . if I think that Parliament as at 
 present establish make the law badly , I would desire a change ; 
 but I doubt whether we shall have they well from any change in 
 Parliament which Reform will give we . " 
 " of course not , " say Mrs. Low . " but we shall have a lot of beggar 
 put on horseback , and we all know where they ride to . " 
 then Phineas become aware that it be not easy to convince any man or 
 any woman on a point of politic , - not even though he who argue may 
 have an eloquent letter from a philosophical Cabinet Minister in his 
 pocket to assist he .

 Phineas Finn make Progress 
 February be far advanced and the new Reform Bill have already be

 appear , do not quite know when he be to expect his wife ; and

 question except such as refer to the expected arrival . be 
 Miss Effingham to come to London with Lady Laura ? Mr. Kennedy 
 believe that Miss Effingham would be up before Easter , but he do

 so fond of mystery that one can never quite know what they intend to 
 do . " he correct himself at once however , perceive that he have 
 seem to say something against his wife , and explain that his

 the feeling with Phineas that something assuredly be wrong . " Miss

 side , " say Phineas . " as for her beauty , I do not   think much of it , "

 reference to grow person . child may be pet , and dog , - though 
 that too be bad ; but what you call pet for grown person be I 
 think frivolous and almost indecent . " Phineas could not help think

 the debate on the second reading of the bill be to be commence

 in Grosvenor Place . Phineas get a note from she in three word to 
 say that she be at home and would see he if he call on Sunday 
 afternoon . the Sunday to which she allude be the last day of

 be wrong . have there be nothing wrong between Lady Laura and her

 and of course he do as he be desire . he call on the Sunday , and 
 find Mrs. Bonteen sit with Lady Laura . " I be just in time for

 " of course you will . the great speech will be Mr. Gresham 's , Mr.

 high in the class . "

 Lady Laura . immediately after that Mrs. Bonteen take she leave .

 always play a game , and it be such a small game that she play ! 
 and she contribute so little to society . she be not witty nor 
 well - inform , - not even sufficiently ignorant or ridiculous to be a

 foot good in the world . " 
 " I think she be a friend of your . "

 to Mr. Gresham after his first speech . "

 " well ; - we shall see . my own impression be that nothing would save

 Mr. Turnbull to a high and a well sphere . "

 Phineas sit there for half an hour and then get up to go , have

 than yourself . that be all . look in when you can . I will not ask you

 on Tuesday , and then let we see you on Wednesday . good - bye . " 
 Phineas as he walk across the park towards his club make up his 
 mind that he would forget the scene by the waterfall . he have never 
 quite known what it have mean , and he would wipe it away from his

 rash word to he . that she be not happy with her husband be very 
 clear to he ; - but that be altogether another affair . she might be

 herself . there be other man and woman in London tie together for

 on the Monday morning Phineas call at Moroni 's Hotel at ten

 no message come to he at the Reform Club , and he be in his seat in

 I have get your letter this moment . of course we must

 Phineas at once perceive that the note be a hostile note , write 
 in an angry spirit , - write to one whom the writer do not at the 
 moment acknowledge to be his friend . this be certainly the case , 
 whatever Lord Chiltern may have say to his sister as to his

 of course determine that he would be in his room at the hour name . 
 the debate be open by a speech from Mr. Mildmay , in which that 
 gentleman at great length and with much perspicuity explain his 
 notion of that measure of parliamentary Reform which he think to 
 be necessary . he be listen to with the great attention to the 
 close , - and perhaps , at the end of his speech , with more attention 
 than usual , as there have go abroad a rumour that the Prime Minister 
 intend to declare that this would be the last effort of his life 
 in that course . but , if he ever intend to utter such a pledge , his 
 heart misgave he when the time come for utter it . he merely say 
 that as the management of the bill in committee would be an affair 
 of much labour , and probably spread over many night , he would be 
 assist in his work by his colleague , and especially by his right 
 honourable friend the Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs . it be 
 then understand that Mr. Gresham would take the lead should the bill 
 go into committee ; - but it be understand also that no resignation of 
 leadership have be make by Mr. Mildmay . 
 the measure now propose to the House be very much the same as that 
 which have be bring forward in the last session . the exist 
 theory of british representation be not to be change , but the 
 actual practice be to be bring near to the ideal theory . the 
 idea of manhood suffrage , and of electoral district , be to be as 
 for ever removed from the bulwark of the British Constitution . there 
 be to be county with agricultural constituency , purposely 
 arrange to be purely agricultural , whenever the nature of the 
 county would admit of its being so . no artificer at Reform , let 
 he be conservative or Liberal , can make Middlesex or Lancashire 
 agricultural ; but Wiltshire and Suffolk be to be preserve 
 inviolable to the plough , - and the apple of Devonshire be still 
 to have their sway . every town in the three kingdom with a certain 
 population be to have two member . but here there be much room 
 for cavil , - as all man know would be the case . who shall say what 
 be a town , or where shall be its limit ? bit of county might be 
 borrow , so as to lessen the Conservatism of the county without 
 endanger the Liberalism of the borough . and then there be the 
 borough with one member , - and then the group of little borough . 
 in the discussion of any such arrangement how easy be the picking 
 of hole ; how impossible the fabrication of a garment that shall be 
 impervious to such picking ! then again there be that great question 
 of the ballot . on that there be to be no mistake . Mr. Mildmay again 
 pledge himself to disappear from the Treasury bench should any 
 motion , clause , or resolution be carry by that House in favour of 
 the ballot . he speak for three hour , and then leave the carcass of 
 his bill to be fight for by the oppose army . 
 no reader of these page will desire that the speech in the debate 
 should be even indicate . it soon become know that the Conservatives 
 would not divide the House against the second reading of the bill . 
 they declare , however , very plainly their intention of so alter 
 the clause of the bill in committee , - or at least of attempt so 
 to do , - as to make the bill their bill , rather than the bill of their 
 opponent . to this Mr. Palliser reply that as long as nothing vital 
 be touch , the Government would only be too happy to oblige their 
 friend opposite . if anything vital be touch , the government 
 could only fall back upon their friend on that side . and in this way 
 man be very civil to each other . but Mr. Turnbull , who open the 
 debate on the Tuesday , thunder out an assurance to god and man 
 that he would divide the House on the second reading of the bill 
 itself . he do not doubt but that there be many good man and true 
 to go with he into the lobby , but into the lobby he would go if he 
 have no more than a single friend to support he . and he warn the 
 Sovereign , and he warn the House , and he warn the people of 
 England , that the measure of Reform now propose by a so - call 
 liberal Minister be a measure prepare in concert with the ancient 
 enemy of the people . he be very loud , very angry , and quite 
 successful in halloo down sundry attempt which be make to

 who do not know I yet , - young member , probably , who be green from

 soon , and then , may be , there will be less of this foolish noise ,

 arouse to a sense of its danger by other voice than these . " he 
 be call to order , but it be rule that he have not be out of

 be declare afterwards that Mr. Monk 's speech be one of the fine 
 piece of oratory that have ever be utter in that House . he make 
 one remark personal to Mr. Turnbull . " I quite agree with the right 
 honourable gentleman in the chair , " he say , " when he declare that 
 the honourable member be not out of order just now . we all of we 
 agree with he always on such point . the rule of our House have 
 be lay down with the utmost latitude , so that the course of our 
 debate may not be frivolously or too easily interrupt . but a 
 member may be so in order as to incur the displeasure of the House , 
 and to merit the reproach of his countryman . " this little duel 
 give great life to the debate ; but it be say that those two great 
 Reformers , Mr. Turnbull and Mr. Monk , could never again meet as 
 friend . 
 in the course of the debate on Tuesday , Phineas get upon his leg . 
 the reader , I trust , will remember that hitherto he have fail 
 altogether as a speaker . on one occasion he have lack even the 
 spirit to use and deliver an oration which he have prepare . on 
 a second occasion he have break down , - woefully , and past all 
 redemption , as say those who be not his friend , - unfortunately , 
 but not past redemption , as say those who be his true friend . 
 after that once again he have arise and say a few word which have 
 call for no remark , and have be speak as though he be in the 
 habit of address the House daily . it may be doubt whether there 
 be half - a - dozen man now present who recognise the fact that this 
 man , who be so well known to so many of they , be now about to

 attempt to forget that such be the case . he have prepare for 
 himself a few heading of what he intend to say , and on one or

 again he hardly know at which end of that long chamber the Speaker 
 be sit . but there arise within he a sudden courage , as soon as

 grace to acknowledge whence have come his inspiration . he hardly know ,

 always think that it would come . I never for a moment believe 
 but that it would come soon or later . " Phineas Finn answer 
 not a word ; but he go home and lie awake all night triumphant .

 succeed . 
 CHAPTER XXXVII 
 A Rough Encounter 
 Phineas , when he wake , have two matter to occupy his mind , - his

 he read every word of the debate , studiously postpone the perusal

 forward in the House , partly through a base fear for which I despise

 we be all talk about your speech . my father be in

 your , L. K.

 VOLUME II 
 CHAPTER XXXVIII 
 the Duel 
 " I know it be a duel ; - bedad I do , " say Laurence Fitzgibbon , 
 stand at the corner of Orchard Street and Oxford Street , when 
 Phineas have half tell his story . " I be sure of it from the tone of 
 your voice , my boy . we must not   let it come off , that be all ; - not 
 if we can help it . " then Phineas be allow to proceed and finish 
 his story . " I do not   see any way out of it ; I do not , indeed , " say 
 Laurence . by this time Phineas have come to think that the duel be in 
 very truth the good way out of the difficulty . it be a bad way out , 
 but then it be a way ; - and he could not see any other . " as for ill 
 treat he , that be nonsense , " say Laurence . " what be the girl to 
 do , if one fellow may not   come on as soon as another fellow be down ? 
 but then , you see , a fellow never know when he be down himself , and 
 therefore he think that he be ill use . I will tell you what now . I 
 should not   wonder if we could not   do it on the sly , - unless one of you 
 be stupid enough to hit the other in an awkward place . if you be 
 certain of your hand now , the right shoulder be the good spot . " 
 Phineas feel very certain that he would not hit Lord Chiltern in an 
 awkward place , although he be by no mean sure of his hand . let come 
 what might , he would not aim at his adversary . but of this he have 
 think it proper to say nothing to Laurence Fitzgibbon . 
 and the duel do come off on the sly . the meeting in the drawing - room 
 in Portman Square , of which mention be make in the last chapter , 
 take place on a Wednesday afternoon . on the Thursday , Friday , Monday , 
 and Tuesday follow , the great debate on Mr. Mildmay 's bill be 
 continue , and at three on the Tuesday night the House divide . there 
 be a majority in favour of the Ministers , not large enough to permit 
 they to claim a triumph for their party , or even an ovation for 
 themselves ; but still sufficient to enable they to send their bill 
 into committee . Mr. Daubeny and Mr. Turnbull have again join 
 their force together in opposition to the ministerial measure . on 
 the Thursday Phineas have show himself in the House , but during

 place , nor be he see at the club , nor do any man know of his 
 whereabouts . I think that Lady Laura Kennedy be the first to miss 
 he with any real sense of his absence . she would now go to Portman 
 Square on the afternoon of every Sunday , - at which time her husband 
 be attend the second service of his church , - and there she would 
 receive those whom she call her father 's guest . but as her father 
 be never there on the Sundays , and as these gathering have be 
 create by herself , the reader will probably think that she be 
 obey her husband 's behest in regard to the Sabbath after a very 
 indifferent fashion . the reader may be quite sure , however , that Mr. 
 Kennedy know well what be be do in Portman Square . whatever

 on the subject ; but we need not go very closely into that matter at 
 the present moment . 
 on the Sunday which afford some rest in the middle of the great 
 reform debate Lady Laura ask for Mr. Finn , and no one could answer

 be also absent . Barrington Erle know nothing of Phineas , - have hear 
 nothing ; but be able to say that Fitzgibbon have be with Mr. 
 Ratler , the patronage secretary and liberal whip , early on Thursday , 
 express his intention of absent himself for two day . Mr. 
 Ratler have be wroth , bid he remain at his duty , and point 
 out to he the great importance of the moment . then Barrington Erle 
 quote Laurence Fitzgibbon 's reply . " my boy , " say Laurence to poor

 be in at the death , Ratler , my boy , as sure as the sun 's in heaven . "

 two day , and I know nothing . " 
 " Mr. Finn have not be with you , then ? " 
 " with I ! no , - not with I . I have a job of business of my own which 
 take I over to Paris . and have Phinny flee too ? poor Ratler ! I 
 should not   wonder if it be not   an asylum he be in before the session be 
 over . " 
 Laurence Fitzgibbon certainly possess the rare accomplishment of 
 tell a lie with a good grace . have any man call he a liar he 
 would have consider himself to be not only insult , but injure 
 also . he believe himself to be a man of truth . there be , however , 
 in his estimation certain subject on which a man might depart as 
 wide as the pole be asunder from truth without subject himself 
 to any ignominy for falsehood . in deal with a tradesman as to his 
 debt , or with a rival as to a lady , or with any man or woman in 
 defence of a lady 's character , or in any such matter as that of a 
 duel , Laurence believe that a gentleman be bind to lie , and that 
 he would be no gentleman if he hesitate to do so . not the slight 
 prick of conscience disturb he when he tell Lady Laura that he 
 have be in Paris , and that he know nothing of Phineas Finn . but , in 
 truth , during the last day or two he have be in Flanders , and not in 
 Paris , and have stand as second with his friend Phineas on the sand 
 at Blankenberg , a little fishing - town some twelve mile distant 
 from bruge , and have leave his friend since that at an hotel at 
 Ostend , - with a wound just under the shoulder , from which a bullet 
 have be extract . 
 the manner of the meeting have be in this wise . Captain Colepepper 
 and Laurence Fitzgibbon have hold their meeting , and at this meeting 
 Laurence have take certain standing - ground on behalf of his friend , 
 and in obedience to his friend 's positive instruction ; - which be 
 this , that his friend could not abandon his right of address the 
 young lady , should he hereafter ever think fit to do so . let that 
 be grant , and Laurence would do anything . but then that could not 
 be grant , and Laurence could only shrug his shoulder . nor would 
 Laurence admit that his friend have be false . " the question lie in 
 a nutshell , " say Laurence , with that sweet Connaught brogue which 
 always come to he when he desire to be effective ; - " here it be . one 
 gentleman tell another that he be sweet upon a young lady , but that 
 the young lady have refuse he , and always will refuse he , for ever 
 and ever . that be the truth anyhow . be the second gentleman bind by 
 that not to address the young lady ? I say he be not bind . it would be a 
 d - - d hard tratement , Captain Colepepper , if a man 's mouth and all 
 the ardent affection of his heart be to be stop in that manner ! 
 by Jases , I do not   know who would like to be the friend of any man if 
 that be to be the way of it . " 
 Captain Colepepper be not very good at an argument . " I think they would 
 well see each other , " say colepepper , pull his thick grey 
 moustache . 
 " if you choose to have it so , so be it . but I think it the hard 
 thing in the world ; - I do indeed . " then they put their head together 
 in the most friendly way , and declare that the affair should , if 
 possible , be keep private . 
 on the Thursday night Lord Chiltern and Captain Colepepper go over 
 by Calais and Lille to bruge . Laurence Fitzgibbon , with his friend 
 Dr. O'Shaughnessy , cross by the direct boat from Dover to Ostend . 
 Phineas go to Ostend by Dover and Calais , but he take the day

 late friend Phineas would give no such pledge , and therefore Lord

 except the five concern , be at that time aware that a duel have 
 be fight among the sand - hill . 
 Laurence Fitzgibbon make his way to Dover by the Saturday night 's 
 boat , and be able to show himself in Portman Square on the Sunday . 
 " know anything about Phinny Finn ? " he say afterwards to Barrington 
 Erle , in answer to an inquiry from that anxious gentleman . " not

 he . " Barrington , however , do not feel quite so well assured of 
 Fitzgibbon 's truth as Lady Laura have do . 
 Dr. O'Shaughnessy remain during the Sunday and Monday at Ostend 
 with his patient , and the people at the inn only know that Mr. Finn 
 have sprain his shoulder badly ; and on the Tuesday they come back 
 to London again , via Calais and Dover . no bone have be break , and 
 Phineas , though his shoulder be very painful , bear the journey well . 
 O'Shaughnessy have receive a telegram on the Monday , tell he that 
 the division would certainly take place on the Tuesday , - and on the 
 Tuesday , at about ten in the evening , Phineas go down to the House . 
 " by - - , you be here , " say Ratler , take hold of he with an 
 affection that be too warm . " yes ; I be here , " say Phineas , wince 
 in agony ; " but be a little careful , there be a good fellow . I have   be 
 down in Kent and put my arm out . "

 CHAPTER XL 
 Madame Max Goesler 
 Day after day , and clause after clause , the bill be fight in 
 committee , and few man fight with more constancy on the side of the

 CHAPTER XLIII 
 Promotion 
 Phineas get no card from Lady Baldock , but one morning he receive 
 a note from Lord Brentford which be of more importance to he than 
 any card could have be . at this time , bit by bit , the Reform 
 Bill of the day have nearly make its way through the committee , but 
 have be so mutilate as to be almost impossible of recognition 
 by its progenitor . and there be still a clause or two as to 
 the rearrangement of seat , respect which it be know that 
 there would be a combat , - probably combat , - carry on after the 
 internecine fashion . there be a certain clipping of county to be 
 do , as to which it be say that Mr. Daubeny have declare that 
 he would not yield till he be make to do so by the brute force of 
 majority ; - and there be another clause for the drafting of certain 
 superfluous member from little borough , and bestow they on 
 populous town at which they be much want , respect which 
 Mr. Turnbull have proclaim that the clause as it now stand be a 
 faineant clause , capable of do , and intend to do , no good in the 
 proper direction ; a clause put into the bill to gull ignorant folk 
 who have not eye enough to recognise the fact that it be faineant ; a

 and Tory figment of representation , as to which there be at least 
 no hypocritical pretence of popular fitness . Mr. Turnbull have be 
 very loud and very angry , - have talk much of demonstration among

 Mr. Turnbull might help Mr. Daubeny , and that Mr. Daubeny might help 
 Mr. Turnbull . it be now May , - the middle of May , - and minister , who 
 have be at work on their Reform Bill ever since the beginning of the 
 session , be become weary of it . and then , should these odious 
 clause escape the threaten Turnbull - Daubeny alliance , - then there 
 be the House of Lords ! " what a pity we can not   pass our bill at the 
 Treasury , and have do with they ! " say Laurence Fitzgibbon . " yes , 
 indeed , " reply Mr. Ratler . " for myself , I be never so tired of a 
 session in my life . I would not   go through it again to be make , - no , 
 not to be make Chancellor of the Exchequer . " 
 Lord Brentford 's note to Phineas Finn be as follow : - 
 House of Lords , 16th May , 186 - . 
 MY DEAR MR . FINN , 
 you be no doubt aware that Lord Bosanquet 's death have 
 take Mr. Mottram into the Upper House , and that as 
 he be under - Secretary for the Colonies , and as the

 must be fill up . 
 the heart of Phineas Finn at this moment be almost in his mouth . not

 commission by Mr. Mildmay to offer it to you . perhaps

 BRENTFORD .

 but during the night cooler reflection tell he that he have be

 " just so ; just so , " say Lord Brentford , delight to see that his

 CHAPTER XLIV 
 Phineas and his friend 
 our hero 's friend be , I think , almost more elated by our hero 's

 for the matter of that I can not   see as they be of any mortal use . "

 they do , Mr. Finn . they wote ; that be what they do ! they wote hard ; 
 black or white , white or black . ai not   that true ? when you be a 
 ' lord , ' will you be able to wote against Mr. Mildmay to save your 
 very soul ? " 
 " if it come to be a question of soul - saving , Mr. Bunce , I shall not 
 save my place at the expense of my conscience . " 
 " not if you know it , you mean . but the bad of it be that a man 
 get so thick into the mud that he do not   know whether he be dirty or

 it be right enough . ai not   you be among Parliament gent long enough 
 to know that that be the way it go ? " 
 " you think no honest man can be a member of the government ? " 
 " I do not   say that , but I think honesty be a deal easier away from them . 
 the fact be , Mr. Finn , it be all wrong with we yet , and will be till 
 we get it nigh to the great american model . if a poor man get into 
 Parliament , - you will excuse I , Mr. Finn , but I call you a poor man . " 
 " certainly , - as a member of Parliament I be a very poor man . " 
 " just so , - and therefore what do you do ? you go and lay yourself

 have to live . you have win way , and a good physiognomy of your 
 own , and be as big as a life - guardsman . " Phineas as he hear this 
 doubtful praise laugh and blush . " very well ; you make your 
 way with the big wig , lord and earl and they like , and you get 
 return for a rotten borough ; - you will excuse I , but that be about 
 it , be not   it ? - and then you go in for government ! a man may have 
 a mission to govern , such as Washington and Cromwell and the like 
 o ' they . but when I hear of Mr. Fitzgibbon a - governing , why then I 
 say , - d - - n it all . " 
 " there must be good and bad you know . " 
 " we have   get to change a deal yet , Mr. Finn , and we will do it . when a 
 young man as have liberal feeling get into Parliament , he should not 
 be snap up and bring into the govern business just because 
 he be poor and want a salary . they do not   do it that way in the 
 States ; and they will not   do it that way here long . it be the system as I 
 hat , and not you , Mr. Finn . well , good - bye , sir . I hope you will like 
 the govern business , and find it suit your health . " 
 these condolement from Mr. Bunce be not pleasant , but they set

 Turnbull be wrong . bunce be ignorant . Quintus Slide be dishonest .

 CHAPTER XLV 
 Miss Effingham 's Four Lovers 
 one morning early in June Lady Laura call at Lady Baldock 's house 
 and ask for Miss Effingham . the servant be show she into 
 the large drawing - room , when she again ask specially for Miss 
 Effingham . " I think Miss Effingham be there , " say the man , open 
 the door . Miss Effingham be not there . Lady Baldock be sit 
 all alone , and Lady Laura perceive that she have be catch in 
 the net which she specially wish to avoid . now Lady Baldock have

 Lord Brentford , but she have conceive a strong idea that her niece 
 Violet be countenance in all impropriety by the standish family 
 generally , and that therefore the standish family be to be regard 
 as a family of enemy . there be doubtless in her mind considerable 
 confusion on the subject , for she do not know whether Lord Chiltern 
 or Mr. Finn be the suitor whom she most fear , - and she be aware , 
 after a sort of muddle fashion , that the claim of these two wicked 
 young man be antagonistic to each other . but they be both
