 cruel position . before the end of May she have propose to leave 
 London , and to take her daughter and Violet down to Baddingham , - or 
 to Brighton , if they prefer it , or to Switzerland . " Brighton in 
 June ! " Violet have exclaim . " would not a month among the glacier be 
 delightful ! " Miss Boreham have say . " do not   let I keep you in town , 
 aunt , " Violet reply ; " but I do not think I shall go till other 
 people go . I can have a room at Laura Kennedy 's house . " then Lady 
 Baldock , whose position be hard and cruel , resolve that she would

 " yes , I think she be at home , " say Lady Baldock , in answer to Lady

 sympathetically .

 have propose Switzerland . if you could only hear Augusta talk in

 " but you will not go ? " 
 " no ! - I will not   go . I know I be very naughty ; but I can not   help feel 
 that I can not be good without be a fool at the same time . I must 
 either fight my aunt , or give way to she . if I be to yield , what a 
 life I should have ; - and I should despise myself after all . "

 go to you . I know that would make her stay . "

 " why have you come to tell I to marry Mr. Phineas Finn ? that be what 
 I want to know . Mr. Phineas Finn , as far as I be aware , have not a

 " my dear , I never tell gentleman 's secret . it seem that if he have ,

 CHAPTER XLVI 
 the Mousetrap

 " we shall none of we have seat to - morrow , " say Mr. Gresham . 
 " but I shall be at a loss to find a place to stand for . " 
 " the election will not come on till November , and you must look about 
 you . both Mr. Monk and Lord Brentford seem to think you will be in 
 the House . " 
 and so the bill be carry , and the session be end . 
 CHAPTER XLVIII 
 " the Duke " 
 by the middle of September there be assemble a large party at

 the Duellists Meet

 CHAPTER LI 
 Troubles at Loughlinter

 CHAPTER LII 
 the First Blow

 show how Phineas Bore the blow 
 when Phineas receive Lady Laura Kennedy 's letter , he be sit in

 CHAPTER LIV 
 Consolation 
 on the day follow Madame Goesler 's dinner party , Phineas , though

 CHAPTER LV 
 Lord Chiltern at Saulsby 
 Lord Chiltern do exactly as he say he would do . he write to his

 my LORD ,

 CHAPTER lvi 
 what the People in Marylebone Thought

 CHAPTER LVII 
 the Top Brick of the Chimney 
 Madame Max Goesler be a lady who know that in fight the battle 
 which fall to her lot , in arrange the social difficulty which she 
 find in her way , in do the work of the world which come to she 
 share , very much more care be necessary , - and care too about thing 
 apparently trifle , - than be demand by the affair of people in 
 general . and this be not the case so much on account of any special 
 disadvantage under which she labour , as because she be ambitious 
 of do the very uttermost with those advantage which she 
 possess . her own birth have not be high , and that of her husband , 
 we may perhaps say , have be very low . he have be old when she have 
 marry he , and she have have little power of make any progress till 
 he have leave she a widow . then she find herself possess of money , 
 certainly ; of wit , - as she believe ; and of a something in her 
 personal appearance which , as she plainly tell herself , she might 
 perhaps palm off upon the world as beauty . she be a woman who do 
 not flatter herself , who do not strongly believe in herself , who 
 could even bring herself to wonder that man and woman in high 
 position should condescend to notice such a one as she . with all her 
 ambition , there be a something of genuine humility about she ; and 
 with all the hardness she have learn there be a touch of womanly 
 softness which would sometimes obtrude itself upon her heart . when 
 she find a woman really kind to she , she would be very kind in 
 return . and though she prize wealth , and know that her money be she 
 only rock of strength , she could be lavish with it , as though it be 
 dirt . 
 but she be highly ambitious , and she play her game with 
 great skill and great caution . her door be not open to all 
 caller ; - be shut even to some who find but few door close 
 against they ; - be shut occasionally to those whom she most 
 specially wish to see within they . she know how to allure by 
 deny , and to make the gift rich by delay it . we be tell by the 
 latin proverb that he who give quickly give twice ; but I say that 
 she who give quickly seldom give more than half . when in the early 
 spring the Duke of Omnium first knock at Madame Max Goesler 's door , 
 he be inform that she be not at home . the Duke feel very cross as 
 he hand his card out from his dark green brougham , - on the panel 
 of which there be no blazon to tell the owner 's rank . he be very 
 cross . she have tell he that she be always at home between four and 
 six on a Thursday . he have condescend to remember the information , 
 and have act upon it , - and now she be not at home ! she be not at

 CHAPTER LVIII 
 Rara Avis in Terris 
 " come and see the country and judge for yourself , " say Phineas . 
 " I should like nothing well , " say Mr. Monk . 
 " it have often seem to I that man in Parliament know less about 
 Ireland than they do of the interior of Africa , " say Phineas . 
 " it be seldom that we know anything accurately on any subject that 
 we have not make matter of careful study , " say Mr. Monk , " and very 
 often do not do so even then . we be very apt to think that we man 
 and woman understand one another ; but most probably you know nothing 
 even of the mode of thought of the man who live next door to you . " 
 " I suppose not . " 
 " there be general law current in the world as to morality . ' thou 
 shalt not steal , ' for instance . that have necessarily be current as 
 a law through all nation . but the first man you meet in the street 
 will have idea about theft so different from your , that , if you 
 know they as you know your own , you would say that this law and your 
 be not even found on the same principle . it be compatible with 
 this man 's honesty to cheat you in a matter of horseflesh , with that 
 man be in a traffic of railway share , with that other man 's as to a 
 woman 's fortune ; with a fourth 's anything may be do for a seat in 
 Parliament , while the fifth man , who stand high among we , and who 
 implore his God every Sunday to write that law on his heart , spend 
 every hour of his daily toil in a system of fraud , and be regard as 
 a pattern of the national commerce ! " 
 Mr. Monk and Phineas be din together at Mr. Monk 's house , and 
 the eld politician of the two in this little speech have recur to 
 certain matter which have already be discuss between they . Mr. 
 Monk be become somewhat sick of his place in the Cabinet , though 
 he have not as yet whisper a word of his sickness to any living 
 ear ; and he have begin to pine for the lose freedom of a seat below 
 the gangway . he have be discuss political honesty with Phineas , 
 and hence have come the sermon of which I have venture to reproduce 
 the conclude denunciation . 
 Phineas be fond of such discussion and fond of hold they with 
 Mr. Monk , - in this matter flutter like a moth round a candle . he 
 would not perceive that as he have make up his mind to be a servant 
 of the public in Parliament , he must abandon all idea of independent 
 action ; and unless he do so he could be neither successful as 
 regard himself , or useful to the public whom he serve . could a man 
 be honest in Parliament , and yet abandon all idea of independence ? 
 when he put such question to Mr. Monk he do not get a direct 
 answer . and indeed the question be never put directly . but the 
 teaching which he receive be ever of a nature to make he uneasy . 
 it be always to this effect : " you have take up the trade now , and 
 seem to be fit for success in it . you have well give up thinking 
 about its special honesty . " and yet Mr. Monk would on an occasion 
 preach to he such a sermon as that which he have just utter ! 
 perhaps there be no question more difficult to a man 's mind than that 
 of the expediency or inexpediency of scruple in political life . 
 whether would a candidate for office be more liable to rejection from 
 a leader because he be know to be scrupulous , or because he be 
 know to be the reverse ? 
 " but put aside the fourth commandment and all the theory , you 
 will come to Ireland ? " say Phineas . 
 " I shall be delighted . " 
 " I do not   live in a castle , you know . " 
 " I think everybody do live in a castle in Ireland , " say Mr. Monk . 
 " they seem to do when I be there twenty year ago . but for myself , 
 I prefer a cottage . " 
 this trip to Ireland have be propose in consequence of certain 
 idea respect tenant - right which Mr. Monk be begin to adopt , 
 and as to which the mind of politician be become move . it 
 have be all very well to put down Fenianism , and Ribandmen , and 
 Repeal , - and everything that have be put down in Ireland in the way 
 of rebellion for the last seventy - five year . England and Ireland 
 have be apparently join together by law of nature so fix , 
 that even politician liberal as be Mr. Monk , - liberal as be Mr. 
 Turnbull , - could not trust themselves to think that disunion could 
 be for the good of the Irish . they have teach themselves that it 
 certainly could not be good for the English . but if it be incumbent 
 on England to force upon Ireland the maintenance of the Union for her 
 own sake , and for England 's sake , because England could not afford 
 independence establish so close against her own rib , - it be at 
 any rate necessary to England 's character that the bride thus 
 bind in a compulsory wedlock should be endow with all the good 
 privilege that a wife can enjoy . let she at least not be a kept 
 mistress . let it be bone of my bone and flesh of my flesh , if we 
 be to live together in the married state . between husband and 
 wife a warm word now and then matter but little , if there be a 
 thoroughly good understanding at bottom . but let there be that good 
 understanding at bottom . what about this Protestant Church ; and what 
 about this tenant - right ? Mr. Monk have be ask himself these 
 question for some time past . in regard to the Church , he have long 
 make up his mind that the Establishment in Ireland be a cry sin . 
 a man have marry a woman whom he know to be of a religion different 
 from his own , and then insist that his wife should say that she 
 believe those thing which he know very well that she do not 
 believe . but , as Mr. Monk well know , the subject of the Protestant 
 Endowments in Ireland be so difficult that it would require almost 
 more than human wisdom to adjust it . it be one of those matter 
 which almost seem to require the interposition of some high 
 power , - the coming of some apparently chance event , - to clear away 
 the evil ; as a fire come , and pestilential alley be remove ; as a 
 famine come , and man be drive from want and ignorance and dirt to 
 seek new home and new thought across the broad water ; as a war 
 come , and slavery be banish from the face of the earth . but in 
 regard to tenant - right , to some arrangement by which a tenant in 
 Ireland might be at least encourage to lay out what little capital 
 he might have in labour or money without be at once call upon to 
 pay rent for that outlay which be his own , as well as for the land 
 which be not his own , - Mr. Monk think that it be possible that if 
 a man would look hard enough he might perhaps be able to see his way 
 as to that . he have speak to two of his colleague on the subject ,

 honest in their idea as public servant , the Duke and Mr. Gresham . 
 there be so much to be do ; - and then so little be know upon the 
 subject ! " I will endeavour to study it , " say Mr. Monk . " if you can 
 see your way , do ; " say Mr. Gresham , - " but of course we can not bind 
 ourselves . " " I should be glad to see it name in the Queen 's speech 
 at the beginning of the next session , " say Mr. Monk . " that be a long 
 way off as yet , " say Mr. Gresham , laugh . " who will be in then , 
 and who will be out ? " so the matter be dispose of at the time , but 
 Mr. Monk do not abandon his idea . he rather feel himself the more 
 bind to cling to it because he receive so little encouragement . 
 what be a seat in the Cabinet to he that he should on that account

 far behind the Treasury bench or below the gangway for many a year , 
 without owe any man a shilling , - and could afford to do so again . 
 but it be different with Phineas Finn , as Mr. Monk himself

 be you , I would take up this matter . " 
 " and why not ? you do not   want to fight the battle singlehande ? " 
 " no ; I desire no such glory , and would wish to have no well 
 lieutenant than you . but you have a subject of which you be really 
 fond , which you be begin to understand , and in regard to which 
 you can make yourself useful . " 
 " you mean this Canada business ? " 
 " yes ; - and that will grow to other matter as regard the colony . 
 there be nothing so important to a public man as that he should have 
 his own subject ; - the thing which he understand , and in respect of 
 which he can make himself really useful . " 
 " then there come a change . " 
 " yes ; - and the man who have half learn how to have a ship build 
 without waste be send into opposition , and be then bring back

 " but , " say Phineas , " if a man take up his own subject , independent 
 of the Government , no man can drive he from it . " 
 " and how often do he do anything ? look at the annual motion which 
 come forward in the hand of private man , - Maynooth and the ballot

 carry in obedience to the express wish of the people . the true

 " then leave tenant - right to the people and the Cabinet . why should 
 you take it up ? " 
 Mr. Monk pause a moment or two before he reply . " if I choose to 
 run a - muck , there be no reason why you should follow I . I be old and 
 you be young . I want nothing from politic as a profession , and you 
 do . moreover , you have a congenial subject where you be , and need 
 not disturb yourself . for myself , I tell you , in confidence , that I 
 can not speak so comfortably of my own position . " 
 " we will go and see , at any rate , " say Phineas . 
 " yes , " say Mr. Monk , " we will go and see . " and thus , in the month of 
 May , it be settle between they that , as soon as the session should 
 be over , and the incidental work of his office should allow Phineas 
 to pack up and be off , they two should start together for Ireland . 
 Phineas feel rather proud as he write to his father and ask 
 permission to bring home with he a Cabinet Minister as a visitor . at 
 this time the reputation of Phineas at Killaloe , as well in the mind 
 of the Killaloeians generally as in those of the inhabitant of the 
 paternal house , stand very high indeed . how could a father think that 
 a son have do badly when before he be thirty year of age he be 
 earn L2,000 a year ? and how could a father not think well of a 
 son who have absolutely pay back certain money into the paternal

 miraculous . the news of Mr. Monk 's come fly about the town , about

 thing about the old doctor 's only son . Mrs. Finn have long since

 Flood Jones , her eye become suffuse with tear as in her solitude

 still ; - that she would love he always . might he bring home with he

 Phineas , during the spring , have , of course , meet Mr. Kennedy 
 frequently in and about the House , and have become aware that Lady 
 Laura 's husband , from time to time , make little overture of civility

 and hope that he might succeed at last . so he write an answer , not

 I be engage on the 28th . your always , Phineas Finn . " at this period

 " I be sorry you can not come to we on the 28th , " Mr. Kennedy say , as 
 soon as he be seat . 
 Phineas be take so much by surprise that all his cunning fail 
 he . " well , yes , " say he ; " I be very sorry ; - very sorry indeed . " 
 " it seem to I , Finn , that you have have some reason for avoid I 
 of late . I do not know that I have do anything to offend you . " 
 " nothing on earth , " say Phineas . 
 " I be wrong , then , in suppose that anything beyond mere chance have 
 prevent you from come to my house ? " Phineas feel that he be in 
 a terrible difficulty , and he feel also that he be be rather

 to a gentleman 's dinner . he think that a man ought to be allow 
 to choose where he would go and where he would not go , and that 
 question such as these be very uncommon . Mr. Kennedy be sit 
 opposite to he , look more grave and more sour than usual ; - and 
 now his own countenance also become a little solemn . it be 
 impossible that he should use Lady Laura 's name , and yet he must , in 
 some way , let his persecuting friend know that no further invitation 
 would be of any use ; - that there be something beyond mere chance 
 in his not go to Grosvenor Place . but how be he to do this ? the 
 difficulty be so great that he could not see his way out of it . so 
 he sit silent with a solemn face . Mr. Kennedy then ask he another

 Another Duel

 the Letter that be send to Brighton

 the Duke should be such a fool ! - the widow of a Jew banker ! he , the 
 Duke of Omnium , - and thus to cut away from himself , for the rest of 
 his life , all honour , all peace of mind , all the grace of a noble 
 end to a career which , if not very noble in itself , have receive 
 the praise of nobility ! and to do this for a thin , black - browe , 
 yellow - visage woman with ringlet and devil 's eye , and a beard on 
 her upper lip , - a Jewess , - a creature of whose habit of life and 
 manner of thought they all be absolutely ignorant ; who drink , 
 possibly ; who might have be a forger , for what any one know ; 
 an adventuress who have find her way into society by her art and 
 perseverance , - and who do not even pretend to have a relation in 
 the world ! that such a one should have influence enough to intrude 
 herself into the house of Omnium , and blot the scutcheon , and , - 
 what be bad of all , - perhaps be the mother of future duke ! Lady 
 Glencora , in her anger , be very unjust to Madame Goesler , think

 her all charm , all beauty . have the Duke forget himself and his

 grey eye , and smooth hair , and a father , Lady Glencora think that 
 she would have forgive it well . it might be that Madame Goesler 
 would win her way to the coronet ; but when she come to put it on , she 
 should find that there be sharp thorn inside the lining of it . not 
 a woman worth the knowing in all London should speak to she ; - nor a 
 man either of those man with whom a Duchess of Omnium would wish to 
 hold converse . she should find her husband rat as a doting fool , 
 and herself rat as a scheming female adventuress . and it should go 
 hard with Lady Glencora , if the Duke be not separate from his new 
 Duchess before the end of the first year ! in her anger Lady Glencora 
 be very unjust . 
 the Duke , when he leave his house without tell his household

 " what do you mean by that , Lotta ? "

 " but I know . that will do , Lotta . now leave I . " then Madame Goesler

 Park Lane , Monday . 
 MY DEAR DUKE of OMNIUM , 
 I find so great a difficulty in express myself to your 
 Grace in a write letter , that since you leave I I have 
 never cease to wish that I have be less nervous , less 
 doubting , and less foolish when you be present with I 
 here in my room . I might then have say in one word what 
 will take so many awkward word to explain . 
 great as be the honour you propose to confer on I , rich 
 as be the gift you offer I , I can not accept it . I can not 
 be your grace 's wife . I may almost say that I know it 
 be so when you part from I ; but the surprise of the 
 situation take away from I a part of my judgment , and 
 make I unable to answer you as I should have do . my 
 lord , the truth be , that I be not fit to be the wife of 
 the Duke of Omnium . I should injure you ; and though I 
 should raise myself in name , I should injure myself in 
 character . but you must not think , because I say this , 
 that there be any reason why I should not be an honest 
 man 's wife . there be none . I have nothing on my conscience 
 which I could not tell you , - or to another man ; nothing 
 that I need fear to tell to all the world . indeed , my 
 lord , there be nothing to tell but this , - that I be not 
 fit by birth and position to be the wife of the Duke of 
 Omnium . you would have to blush for I , and that no man 
 shall ever have to do on my account . 
 I will own that I have be ambitious , too ambitious , and 
 have be pleased to think that one so exalted as you be , 
 one whose high position be so rife in the eye of all man , 
 should have take pleasure in my company . I will confess 
 to a foolish woman 's silly vanity in have wish to be 
 know to be the friend of the Duke of Omnium . I be like 
 the other moth that flutter near the light and have their 
 wing burn . but I be wise than they in this , that 
 have be scorch , I know that I must keep my distance . 
 you will easily believe that a woman , such as I be , do 
 not refuse to ride in a carriage with your grace 's arm on 
 the panel without a regret . I be no philosopher . I do not 
 pretend to despise the rich thing of the world , or the 
 high thing . accord to my way of think a woman ought 
 to wish to be Duchess of Omnium ; - but she ought to wish 
 also to be able to carry her coronet with a proper grace . 
 as Madame Goesler I can live , even among my superior , at 
 my ease . as your grace 's wife , I should be easy no long ; 
  - nor would your Grace . 
 you will think perhaps that what I write be heartless , 
 that I speak altogether of your rank , and not at all of 
 the affection you have show I , or of that which I might 
 possibly bear towards you . I think that when the first 
 flush of passion be over in early youth man and woman 
 should strive to regulate their love , as they do their 
 other desire , by their reason . I could love your grace , 
 fondly , as your wife , if I think it well for your grace 
 or for myself that we should be man and wife . as I think 
 it would be ill for both of we , I will restrain that 
 feeling , and remember your grace ever with the pure 
 feeling of true friendship . 
 before I close this letter , I must utter a word of 
 gratitude . in the kind of life which I have lead as a 
 widow , a life which have be very isolated as regard 
 true fellowship , it have be my great effort to obtain 
 the good opinion of those among whom I have attempt to 
 make my way . I may , perhaps , own to you now that I have 
 have many difficulty . a woman who be alone in the world 
 be ever regard with suspicion . in this country a woman 
 with a foreign name , with mean derive from foreign 
 source , with a foreign history , be specially suspect . 
 I have strive to live that down , and I have succeed . 
 but in my wild dream I never dream of such success 
 as this , - that the Duke of Omnium should think I the 
 worthiest of the worthy . you may be sure that I be not 
 ungrateful , - that I never will be ungrateful . and I trust 
 it will not derogate from your opinion of my worth , that 
 I have know what be due to your grace 's highness . 
 I have the honour to be , 
 my Lord Duke , 
 your most obliged and faithful servant , 
 MARIE MAX GOESLER . 
 " how many unmarried woman in England be there would do the same ? "

 MY DEAR LADY GLENCORA , 
 I venture to send you a line to put you out of your 
 misery ; - for you be very miserable when you be so good 
 as to come here yesterday . your dear little boy be safe 
 from I ; - and , what be more to the purpose , so be you and 
 your husband , - and your uncle , whom , in truth , I love . you 
 ask I a downright question which I do not then choose 
 to answer by a downright answer . the downright answer be 
 not at that time due to you . it have since be give , and 
 as I like you too well to wish you to be in torment , I 
 send you a line to say that I shall never be in the way of 
 you or your boy . 
 and now , dear Lady Glencora , one word more . should it 
 ever again appear to you to be necessary to use your zeal 
 for the protection of your husband or your child , do not 
 endeavour to dissuade a woman by try to make she think 
 that she , by her alliance , would bring degradation into 
 any house , or to any man . if there could have be an 
 argument powerful with I , to make I do that which you 
 wish to prevent , it be the argument which you use . but 
 my own comfort , and the happiness of another person whom 
 I value almost as much as myself , be too important to 
 be sacrifice even to a woman 's revenge . I take mine by 
 write to you and tell you that I be well and more 
 rational and wise than you take I to be . 
 if , after this , you choose to be on good term with I , I 
 shall be happy to be your friend . I shall want no further 
 revenge . you owe I some little apology ; but whether you 
 make it or not , I will be content , and will never do 
 more than ask whether your darling 's prospect be still 
 safe . there be more woman than one in the world , you 
 know , and you must not consider yourself to be out of the 
 wood because you have escape from a single danger . if 
 there arise another , come to I , and we will consult 
 together . 
 Dear Lady Glencora , your always sincerely , 
 MARIE M. G.

 show how the Duke stand his Ground 
 Mr. Low the barrister , who have give so many lecture to our friend

 CHAPTER LXIV 
 the Horns 
 while look for Violet Effingham , Phineas encounter Madame

 the thing be over , the boat be vote to have be a bore . 
 " be you go to venture ? " say Phineas to the lady .

 will you come ? "

 certainty . they be go down beneath the bridge too , and we should

 oar would destroy the whole symmetry of my dress . look . that green

 town soon , Madame Goesler ? " 
 " almost immediately . "

 mind my business . I wonder whether you would know I , if you see 
 I ; - sometimes sit on a stool in a counting - house , sometimes 
 go about among old house , settle what must be do to save they 
 from tumble down . I dress so differently at such time , and talk so

 another person . " 
 " be it a great trouble to you ? " 
 " no , - I rather like it . it make I feel that I do something in the

 " do you go alone ? " 
 " quite alone . I take a german maid with I , and never speak a word to 
 any one else on the journey . "

 year all through , and I be a solitary during four - fifth of they . and

 have one sister , who live with her husband at Riga . she be my only

 Duke would do as much as any one else . I will not   have the Duke abuse . "

 " oh , yes ; - and too stiff , and too old , and too pompous , and too cold , 
 and too make - believe , and too gingerbread . " 
 " Mr. Finn ! " 
 " the Duke be all buckram , you know . "

 LAURA kennedy . 
 " it be at any rate true , " she say , when Phineas have read the letter .

 CHAPTER LXV 
 the Cabinet Minister at Killaloe 
 Phineas do not throw himself into the river from the Duke 's garden ;

 Violet have speak to he after such a fashion that he could not bring

 but she have convince he that he could not be allow to take Lord

 but he be ready for Mr. Monk on the 7th of August , and they start

 incidental to the passage from Holyhead to Kingstown . nevertheless ,

 " it be sad to I to have to confess it , " he say , " but the truth be 
 that my old rival , Turnbull , be right . a man who begin his political 
 life as I begin mine , be not the man of whom a Minister should 
 be form . I be inclined to think that Ministers of Government 
 require almost as much education in their trade as shoemaker or 
 tallow - chandler . I doubt whether you can make a good public servant 
 of a man simply because he have get the ear of the House of Commons . "

 from begin to end , in our way of arrange these thing ? "

 statesman since our present mode of government be form , - from the 
 day in which it be form itself , say from Walpole down , and you 
 will find that all who have be of real use have early training as 
 public servant . " 
 " be we never to get out of the old groove ? " 
 " not if the groove be good , " say Mr. Monk , " those who have be 
 efficient as minister suck in their efficacy with their mother 's 
 milk . Lord Brock do so , and Lord de Terrier , and Mr. Mildmay . they 
 seat themselves in office chair the moment they leave college . 
 Mr. Gresham be in office before he be eight - and - twenty . the 
 Duke of St. Bungay be at work as a private Secretary when he be 
 three - and - twenty . you , luckily for yourself , have do the same . " 
 " and regret it every hour of my life . " 
 " you have no cause for regret , but it be not so with I . if there be 
 any man unfit by his previous career for office , it be he who have 
 become , or who have endeavour to become , a popular politician , - an 
 exponent , if I may say so , of public opinion . as far as I can see , 
 office be offer to such man with one view only , - that of clip 
 their wing . " 
 " and of obtain their help . " 
 " it be the same thing . help from Turnbull would mean the withdrawal 
 of all power of opposition from he . he could not give other help for 
 any long term , as the very fact of his accept power and patronage 
 would take from he his popular leadership . the mass outside 
 require to have their minister as the Queen have her ; but the same 
 man can not be minister to both . if the people 's minister choose to 
 change his master , and to take the Queen 's shilling , something of 
 temporary relief may be gain by government in the fact that the 
 other place will for a time be vacant . but there be candidate 
 enough for such place , and the vacancy be not a vacancy long . of 
 course the Crown have this pull , that it pay wage , and the people do 
 not . " 
 " I do not think that that influence you , " say Phineas . 
 " it do not influence I . to you I will make bold to state so much 
 positively , though it would be foolish , perhaps , to do so to other . 
 I do not go for the shilling , though I be so poor a man that the 
 shilling be more to I than it would be to almost any man in the

 this , that I be ashamed of be afraid to take it . they tell

 my friend . such mistake make at my time of life can not be wholly 
 rectify ; but , be convince of my error , I must do the good in my

 there be a bitterness in all this to Phineas himself of which he

 " there I think you be wrong . if you mean that you can not do joint

 " I can not see that you suffer anything that ought to go against the

 there be a large dinner party at the doctor 's on the day after Mr.

 phinea on this occasion have take out to dinner the mother of his

 CHAPTER LXVI 
 Victrix 
 Mr. Monk 's holiday programme allow he a week at Killaloe , and 
 from thence he be to go to Limerick , and from Limerick to Dublin , 
 in order that , at both place , he might be entertain at a public 
 dinner and make a speech about tenant - right . foresee that Phineas 
 might commit himself if he attend these meeting , Mr. Monk have 
 counsel he to remain at Killaloe . but Phineas have refuse to 
 subject himself to such cautious abstinence . Mr. Monk have come to 
 Ireland as his friend , and he would see he through his travel . " I 
 shall not , probably , be ask to speak , " say Phineas , " and if I be 
 ask , I need not say more than a few word . and what if I do speak 
 out ? " 
 " you might find it disadvantageous to you in London . " 
 " I must take my chance of that . I be not go to tie myself down for 
 ever and ever for the sake of be under - secretary to the Colonies . " 
 Mr. Monk say very much to he on the subject , - be constantly say 
 very much to he about it ; but in spite of all that Mr. Monk say ,

 he have not , since his arrival at Killaloe , be a moment alone with 
 Mary Flood Jones till the evening before he start with Mr. Monk . 
 she have keep out of his way successfully , though she have constantly 
 be with he in company , and be begin to plume herself on the 
 strength and valour of her conduct . but her self - praise have in it 
 nothing of joy , and her glory be very sad . of course she would care 
 for he no more , - more especially as it be so very evident that he

 his way , make she acknowledge to herself that her position be very

 go to Killaloe with safety , - that it would be well for she to put 
 herself in the way of meet he as an old friend , - that the idea of

 desert she , and she find herself to be miserable , hop for

 feel during every moment of his presence with she that some 
 special conduct be necessary on her part . she could not make further 
 confession to her mother and ask to be carry back to Floodborough ; 
 but she know that she be very wretched at Killaloe . 
 as for Phineas , he have feel that his old friend be very cold to he . 
 he be in that humour with reference to Violet Effingham which seem 
 especially to require consolation . he know now that all hope be 
 over there . Violet Effingham could never be his wife . even be she 
 not to marry Lord Chiltern for the next five year , she would not , 
 during those five year , marry any other man . such be our hero 's

 avoid I purposely ever since I have be at home . " she smile and

 for it , Mary ? " 
 " no reason at all that I know of , " she say . 
 " we use to be such great friend . " 
 " that be before you be a great man , Phineas . it must necessarily 
 be different now . you know so many people now , and people of such a 
 different sort , that of course I fall a little into the background . " 
 " when you talk in that way , Mary , I know that you be laugh at 
 I . " 
 " indeed , indeed I be not . "

 Phineas go with Mr. Monk first to Limerick and then to Dublin , and

 tenant - right ; - could anything be do to make it profitable for man

 it be September when Phineas find himself back at Killaloe , and he

 Job 's Comforters

 the Joint Attack

 CHAPTER LXIX 
 the Temptress

 Floodborough , Thursday .

 we have just get home from Killaloe , and mean to remain

 this be very nice . such a man as be Phineas Finn always take a

 the Prime Minister 's House

 CHAPTER LXXI 
 compare note 
 it would , perhaps , be difficult to decide , - between Lord Chiltern and

 " I can not afford it . "

 CHAPTER LXXII

 when Phineas Finn leave Mr. Gresham 's house he have quite resolve what

 be a man with a heart within his breast , - there would have be no 
 comfort for he , in his outlook on any side . but even now , when he

 CHAPTER LXXIII 
 Amantium Irae 
 Miss Effingham 's life at this time be not the happy in the world . 
 her line , as she once say to her friend Lady Laura , be not 
 lay for she in pleasant place . her residence be still with her 
 aunt , and she have come to find that it be almost impossible any 
 long to endure Lady Baldock , and quite impossible to escape from 
 Lady Baldock . in former day she have have a dream that she might 
 escape , and live alone if she choose to be alone ; that she might be 
 independent in her life , as a man be independent , if she choose to 
 live after that fashion ; that she might take her own fortune in her 
 own hand , as the law certainly allow she to do , and act with it as 
 she might please . but latterly she have learn to understand that all 
 this be not possible for she . though one law allow it , another law 
 disallow it , and the latter law be at least as powerful as the

 possess . Hitherto she have always be able to escape from Lady 
 Baldock to the house of her friend , but now such escape be out of

 and Violet could not live with they . 
 Lady Baldock understand all this , and torture her niece accordingly . 
 it be not premeditate torture . the aunt do not mean to make she 
 niece 's life a burden to she , and , so intending , systematically work 
 upon a principle to that effect . Lady Baldock , no doubt , desire 
 to do her duty conscientiously . but the result be torture to poor 
 Violet , and a strong conviction on the mind of each of the two lady 
 that the other be the most unreasonable be in the world .

 Lord Chiltern . this arise partly from a belief that the quarrel be

 Violet by this expression of pity , - partly from a feeling that it 
 would be well that her niece should marry Lord Chiltern than that

 principle that , as she think it right to scold her niece on all

 all question to that take by the niece to be scold . Violet be

 other lover , she have begin to perceive that their condition be

 no chance remain either for Phineas , or for Lord Fawn , or for Mr. 
 Appledom . " I believe she will be an old maid , on purpose to bring I 
 to my grave , " say Lady Baldock . when , therefore , Lady Baldock be

 see Miss Effingham , she do not at once faint away , and declare that

 since . she be perplex by a double duty . if it be possible that 
 Violet should relent and be reconcile , then it would be her duty to 
 save Violet from the claw of the wild beast . but if there be no 
 such chance , then it would be her duty to poor Lord Chiltern to see 
 that he be not treat with contumely and ill - humour . 
 " do she know that he be here ? " Lady Baldock ask her daughter . 
 " not yet , mamma . " 
 " oh dear , oh dear ! I suppose she ought to see he . she have give he 
 so much encouragement ! " 
 " I suppose she will do as she please , mamma . " 
 " Augusta , how can you talk in that way ? be I to have no control in my 
 own house ? " it be , however , soon apparent to she that in this matter 
 she be to have no control . 
 " Lord Chiltern be down - stair , " say Violet , come into the room 
 abruptly . 
 " so Augusta tell I . sit down , my dear . " 
 " I can not sit down , aunt , - not just now . I have send down to say that 
 I would be with he in a minute . he be the most impatient soul alive , 
 and I must not keep he wait . " 
 " and you mean to see he ? " 
 " certainly I shall see he , " say Violet , as she leave the room . 
 " I wonder that any woman should ever take upon herself the charge of 
 a niece ! " say Lady Baldock to her daughter in a despondent tone , as 
 she hold up her hand in dismay . in the meantime , Violet have go 
 down - stair with a quick step , and have then boldly enter the room 
 in which her lover be wait to receive she . 
 " I have to thank you for come to I , Violet , " say Lord Chiltern . 
 there be still in his face something of savagery , - an expression 
 partly of anger and partly of resolution to tame the thing with which 
 he be angry . Violet do not regard the anger half so keenly as she 
 do that resolution of taming . an angry lord , she think , she could 
 endure , but she could not bear the idea of be tame by any one . 
 " why should I not come ? " she say . " of course I come when I be tell 
 that you be here . I do not think that there need be a quarrel 
 between we , because we have change our mind . " 
 " such change make quarrel , " say he . 
 " it shall not do so with I , unless you choose that it shall , " say 
 Violet . " why should we be enemy , - we who have know each other 
 since we be child ? my dear friend be your father and your 
 sister . why should we be enemy ? " 
 " I have come to ask you whether you think that I have ill - use you ? " 
 " Ill - use I ! certainly not . have any one tell you that I have accuse 
 you ? " 
 " no one have tell I so . " 
 " then why do you ask I ? " 
 " because I would not have you think so , - if I could help it . I do 
 not intend to be rough with you . when you tell I that my life be 
 disreputable - " 
 " oh , Oswald , do not let we go back to that . what good will it do ? " 
 " but you say so . " 
 " I think not . " 
 " I believe that that be your word , - the harsh word that you could 
 use in all the language . " 
 " I do not mean to be harsh . if I use it , I will beg your pardon . 
 only let there be an end of it . as we think so differently about life 
 in general , it be well that we should not be marry . but that 
 be settle , and why should we go back to word that be speak in 
 haste , and which be simply disagreeable ? " 
 " I have come to know whether it be settle . " 
 " certainly . you settle it yourself , Oswald . I tell you what I 
 think myself bind to tell you . perhaps I use language which I

 wife ; - and I think you be right , quite right . " 
 " I be wrong , quite wrong , " he say impetuously . " so wrong , that I

 for an angry word . "

 " say it again , and let I have another chance to answer it . " 
 " I think I say that idleness be not , - respectable , or something

 " my wish be that my husband , - should I ever have one , - should work , 
 not exactly as a coal - heaver . " 
 " come , Violet , " he say , - and now the look of savagery depart from

 I much . "

 " but not since ? if it be so , I have well go . love on one side only 
 be a poor affair at well . " 
 " a very poor affair . "

 " that be my affair , my lord . " 
 " by heaven , and it be mine too . tell I that you do , and I will

 " I will not answer you your question , - not that one . "

 " any that may concern yourself and myself . none that may concern 
 other people . "

 " this moment I tell you that I do so , - year ago . " 
 " but now ? " 
 " that be another matter . " 
 " Violet , do you love I now ? " 
 " that be a point - blank question at any rate , " she say . 
 " and you will answer it ? " 
 " I must answer it , - I suppose . " 
 " well , then ? " 
 " oh , Oswald , what a fool you be ! love you ! of course I love you .

 " yes ; - you be ; but I do not   love you the less on that account . "

 " no ; - do not ; and , for I , I will not say another word , whether you 
 choose to heave coal or not . you shall do as you please . I mean to

 " you be the grand girl that ever be make . " 
 " I do not want to be grand at all , and I never will be wise any more . 
 only do not frown at I and look savage . " then she put up her hand

 aunt ; " I wash my hand of it . " that evening , when she discuss the

 CHAPTER LXXIV 
 the beginning of the End 
 the day of the debate have come , and Phineas Finn be still sit in

 neither on the Treasury bench nor on any other in that House be

 CHAPTER LXXV 
 P. P. C. 
 on the Thursday morning before Phineas go to Mr. Monk , a gentleman 
 call upon he at his lodging . Phineas request the servant to 
 bring up the gentleman 's name , but tempt perhaps by a shill the 
 girl bring up the gentleman instead . it be Mr. Quintus Slide from 
 the office of the " Banner of the People . " 
 " Mr. Finn , " say Quintus , with his hand extend , " I have come to 
 offer you the calumet of peace . " Phineas certainly desire no such

 of sense who know the world , you will accept the calumet of peace , " 
 continue Mr. Slide . 
 " I do not   know why I should be ask particularly to accept war or 
 peace , " say Phineas . 
 " well , Mr. Finn , - I do not   often quote the Bible ; but those who be 
 not for we must be against we . you will agree to that . now that 
 you have   free yourself from the iniquity of that sink of abomination 
 in Downing Street , I look upon you as a man again . "

  Banner into my own ' and now . " Phineas be oblige to explain that

 against we I have to be down on you , and I be down upon you ; - that be 
 just about all of it . now you be come among we again , and so I come

 " Lies , Mr. Finn ! do you say lie , sir ? "

 it or starve . but do not come to I again . " then he open the door

 " very well , sir . I shall know how to punish this . " 
 " exactly . but if you please you will go and do your punishment at the 
 office of the Banner , - unless you like to try it here . you want to 
 kick I and spit at I , but you will prefer to do it in print . "

 Mr. Quintus Slide do not so much impede the business of his day but 
 what Phineas be with Mr. Monk by two , and in his place in the House 
 when prayer be read at four . as he sit in his place , conscious

 " but we be near to it . " 
 " in one sense , yes . such a debate and such a majority will make man 
 think . but no ; - think be too high a word ; as a rule man do not   think . 
 but it will make they believe that there be something in it . many who

 two day after this Mr. Gresham declare his intention of dissolve

 Sunday morning .

 CHAPTER LXXVI 
 Conclusion 
 we be tell that it be a bitter moment with the Lord Mayor when he 
 leave the Mansion House and become once more Alderman Jones , of no . 
 75 , Bucklersbury . Lord Chancellors go out of office have a great 
 fall though they take pension with they for their consolation . and 
 the President of the United States when he leave the glory of the 
 White House and once more become a simple citizen must feel the 
 change severely . but our hero , Phineas Finn , as he turn his back 
 upon the scene of his many success , and prepare himself for 
 permanent residence in his own country , be , I think , in a bad 
 plight than any of the reduce divinity to whom I have allude . 
 they at any rate have know that their fall would come . he , like 
 Icarus , have fly up towards the sun , hop that his wing of wax 
 would bear he steadily aloft among the god . see that his wing 
 be wing of wax , we must acknowledge that they be very good . but 
 the celestial light have be too strong for they , and now , have 
 live for five year with lord and countess , with Ministers and 
 orator , with beautiful woman and man of fashion , he must start again 
 in a little lodging in Dublin , and hope that the attorney of that 
 litigious city might be good to he . on his journey home he make but 
 one resolution . he would make the change , or attempt to make it , 
 with manly strength . during his last month in London he have allow 
 himself to be sad , depressed , and melancholy . there should be an end 
 of all that now . nobody at home should see that he be depressed . 
 and Mary , his own Mary , should at any rate have no cause to think 
 that her love and his own engagement have ever be the cause to he 
 of depression . do he not value her love more than anything in the 
 world ? a thousand time he tell himself that he do . 
 she be there in the old house at Killaloe to greet he . her 
 engagement be an affair know to all the county , and she have no 
 idea that it would become she to be coy in her love . she be in his 
 arm before he have speak to his father and mother , and have make she 
 little speech to he , - very inaudibly indeed , - while he be cover 
 her sweet face with kiss . " oh , Phineas , I be so proud of you ; and 
 I think you be so right , and I be so glad you have do it . " again 
 he cover her face with kiss . could he ever have have such 
 satisfaction as this have he allow Madame Goesler 's hand to remain 
 in his ? 
 on the first night of his arrival he sit for an hour downstairs 
 with his father talk over his plan . he feel , - he could not but 
 feel , - that he be not the hero now that he have be when he be last 
 at Killaloe , - when he have come thither with a Cabinet Minister under 
 his wing . and yet his father do his good to prevent the growth of 
 any such feeling . the old doctor be not quite as well off as he have 
 be when Phineas first start with his high hope for London . since 
 that day he have abandon his profession and be now live on the 
 fruit of his life 's labour . for the last two year he have be 
 absolve from the necessity of provide an income for his son , and 
 have probably allow himself to feel that no such demand upon he 
 would again be make . now , however , it be necessary that he should do 
 so . could his son manage to live on two hundred a - year ? there would 
 then be four hundred a - year leave for the want of the family at home . 
 Phineas swear that he could fight his battle on a hundred and fifty ,

 receive the following letter from Lord Cantrip : - 
 Downing Street , August 4 , 186 - . 
 MY DEAR MR . FINN , - 
 Mr. Gresham have be talk to I , and we both think 
 that possibly a permanent Government appointment may be 
 acceptable to you . we have no doubt , that should this be 
 the case , your service would be very valuable to the 
 country . there be a vacancy for a poor - law inspector at 
 present in Ireland , whose residence I believe should be 
 in Cork . the salary be a thousand a - year . should the 
 appointment suit you , Mr. Gresham will be most happy to 
 nominate you to the office . let I have a line at your 
 early convenience . 
 believe I , 
 most sincerely your , 
 cantrip . 
 he receive the letter one morning in Dublin , and within three hour

 " a thousand a - year ! " say Mary Flood Jones , open her eye wide

 " it be nothing very great for a perpetuity , " say Phineas . 
 " oh , Phineas ; surely a thousand a - year will be very nice . " 
 " it will be certain , " say Phineas , " and then we can be marry 
 to - morrow . " 
 " but I have be make up my mind to wait ever so long , " say Mary . 
 " then your mind must be unmade , " say Phineas . 
 what be the nature of the reply to Lord Cantrip the reader may 
 imagine , and thus we will leave our hero an Inspector of Poor Houses
